question_html:
1. Define 'work function' of a metal. How can its value be determined from a graph between stopping potential and frequency of the incident radiation?
  2. The work function of a metal is {tex}2 \cdot 4 {eV}{/tex}. A stopping potential of 0.6 V is required to reduce the photocurrent to zero, in a photoelectric experiment. Calculate the wavelength of light used.
question_text: Define 'work function' of a metal. How can its value be determined from a graph between stopping potential and frequency of the incident radiation? The work function of a metal is {tex}2 \cdot 4 {eV}{/tex} . A stopping potential of 0.6 V is required to reduce the photocurrent to zero, in a photoelectric experiment. Calculate the wavelength of light used.
explanation: Minimum energy required by an electron to escape from metal surface. The intercept on the y axis for a graph between stopping potential & frequency gives {tex}\phi_0 / e{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \phi_0={e} \times{/tex} intercept on y-axis. {tex} {eV}_0 =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi_0 {/tex} {tex} 0.6 =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}-2.4 {/tex} {tex} 3 =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda} \Rightarrow 3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{\lambda} {/tex} {tex} \lambda =\frac{1241}{3}=413.6 {~nm} {/tex}
solution: Minimum energy required by an electron to escape from metal surface. The intercept on the y axis for a graph between stopping potential & frequency gives {tex}\phi_0 / e{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \phi_0={e} \times{/tex} intercept on y-axis. {tex} {eV}_0 =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi_0 {/tex} {tex} 0.6 =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}-2.4 {/tex} {tex} 3 =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda} \Rightarrow 3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{\lambda} {/tex} {tex} \lambda =\frac{1241}{3}=413.6 {~nm} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Minimum energy required by an electron to escape from metal surface.
    The intercept on the y axis for a graph between stopping potential & frequency gives {tex}\phi_0 / e{/tex}.
    {tex}\therefore \phi_0={e} \times{/tex} intercept on y-axis.
  2. {tex} {eV}_0 =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}-\phi_0 {/tex}
    {tex} 0.6 =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}-2.4 {/tex}
    {tex} 3 =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda} \Rightarrow 3 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{\lambda} {/tex}
    {tex} \lambda =\frac{1241}{3}=413.6 {~nm} {/tex}

question_html: The threshold frequency for a given metal is {tex}3.6 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex}. If monochromatic radiations of frequency {tex}6.8 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} are incident on this metal, find the cut-off potential for the photoelectrons.
question_text: The threshold frequency for a given metal is {tex}3.6 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} . If monochromatic radiations of frequency {tex}6.8 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} are incident on this metal, find the cut-off potential for the photoelectrons.
solution_html:
{tex} {eV}_0 ={h}\left(v-v_0\right) {/tex}
{tex} {V}_0 =\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times(6.8-3.6) \times 10^{14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex}
{tex} =1.33 {~V} {/tex}

question_html:
1. Mention any three features of results of experiment on photoelectric effect which cannot be explained using the wave theory of light.
  2. In his experiment on photoelectric effect, Robert A. Millikan found the slope of the cut-off voltage versus frequency of incident light plot to be {tex}4.12 \times 10^{-15}{/tex} Vs. Calculate the value of Planck's constant from it.
question_text: Mention any three features of results of experiment on photoelectric effect which cannot be explained using the wave theory of light. In his experiment on photoelectric effect, Robert A. Millikan found the slope of the cut-off voltage versus frequency of incident light plot to be {tex}4.12 \times 10^{-15}{/tex} Vs. Calculate the value of Planck's constant from it.
explanation: Three features The existence of threshold frequency ( {tex}v_{0}{/tex} ) Maximum Kinetic energy of photoelectrons is independent of intensity of incident radiation. Instantaneous nature of photoelectric effect. slope {tex}=\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {h} ={e} \times {slope}{/tex} {tex}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4.12 \times 10^{-15}{/tex} {tex}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} {Js}{/tex}
solution: Three features The existence of threshold frequency ( {tex}v_{0}{/tex} ) Maximum Kinetic energy of photoelectrons is independent of intensity of incident radiation. Instantaneous nature of photoelectric effect. slope {tex}=\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {h} ={e} \times {slope}{/tex} {tex}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4.12 \times 10^{-15}{/tex} {tex}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} {Js}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Three features
    1. The existence of threshold frequency ({tex}v_{0}{/tex})
    2. Maximum Kinetic energy of photoelectrons is independent of intensity of incident radiation.
    3. Instantaneous nature of photoelectric effect.
  2. slope {tex}=\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {h} ={e} \times {slope}{/tex}
    {tex}=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 4.12 \times 10^{-15}{/tex}
    {tex}=6.6 \times 10^{-34} {Js}{/tex}

question_html: A laser beam of frequency {tex}3.0 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} produces average power of 9 mW. Find (i) the energy of photon of the beam, and (ii) the number of photons emitted per second on an average by the source.
question_text: A laser beam of frequency {tex}3.0 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} produces average power of 9 mW. Find (i) the energy of photon of the beam, and (ii) the number of photons emitted per second on an average by the source.
explanation: {tex}{E}={h} v{/tex} {tex}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.0 \times 10^{14}{/tex} {tex}=1.99 \times 10^{-19} {~J}{/tex} {tex}N=\frac{P}{E}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-3}}{1.99 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} {tex}=4.5 \times 10^{16}{/tex}
solution: {tex}{E}={h} v{/tex} {tex}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.0 \times 10^{14}{/tex} {tex}=1.99 \times 10^{-19} {~J}{/tex} {tex}N=\frac{P}{E}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-3}}{1.99 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} {tex}=4.5 \times 10^{16}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}{E}={h} v{/tex}
    {tex}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.0 \times 10^{14}{/tex}
    {tex}=1.99 \times 10^{-19} {~J}{/tex}
  2. {tex}N=\frac{P}{E}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{9 \times 10^{-3}}{1.99 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
    {tex}=4.5 \times 10^{16}{/tex}

question_html: A light of wavelength 400 nm is incident on metal surface whose work function is {tex}3.0 \times 10^{-19} {~J}{/tex}. Calculate the speed of the fastest photoelectrons emitted.
question_text: A light of wavelength 400 nm is incident on metal surface whose work function is {tex}3.0 \times 10^{-19} {~J}{/tex} . Calculate the speed of the fastest photoelectrons emitted.
explanation: {tex} {hv}=\phi_o+{K}_{\max } {/tex} {tex} \frac{{hc}}{\lambda}=\phi_0+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_{\max }^2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^{-7}}=3 \times 10^{-19}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_{\max }^2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{19.89}{4} \times 10^{-19}=3 \times 10^{-19}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_{\max }^2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{4 \times 10^{-19}}{9 \times 10^{-31}}={v}_{\max }^2 {/tex} {tex} {v}_{\max }=\frac{2}{3} \times 10^6 {~m} / {s} {/tex}
solution: {tex} {hv}=\phi_o+{K}_{\max } {/tex} {tex} \frac{{hc}}{\lambda}=\phi_0+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_{\max }^2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^{-7}}=3 \times 10^{-19}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_{\max }^2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{19.89}{4} \times 10^{-19}=3 \times 10^{-19}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_{\max }^2 {/tex} {tex} \frac{4 \times 10^{-19}}{9 \times 10^{-31}}={v}_{\max }^2 {/tex} {tex} {v}_{\max }=\frac{2}{3} \times 10^6 {~m} / {s} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} {hv}=\phi_o+{K}_{\max } {/tex}
{tex} \frac{{hc}}{\lambda}=\phi_0+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_{\max }^2 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 10^{-7}}=3 \times 10^{-19}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_{\max }^2 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{19.89}{4} \times 10^{-19}=3 \times 10^{-19}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_{\max }^2 {/tex}
{tex} \frac{4 \times 10^{-19}}{9 \times 10^{-31}}={v}_{\max }^2 {/tex}
{tex} {v}_{\max }=\frac{2}{3} \times 10^6 {~m} / {s} {/tex}

question_html: The threshold wavelength of a metal is 450 nm. Calculate (i) the work function of the metal in eV and (ii) the maximum energy of the ejected photoelectrons in eV by incident radiation of 250 nm.
question_text: The threshold wavelength of a metal is 450 nm. Calculate (i) the work function of the metal in eV and (ii) the maximum energy of the ejected photoelectrons in eV by incident radiation of 250 nm.
explanation: {tex} \text {} \phi=\frac{{hc}}{\lambda_{{o}}} {/tex} {tex} \phi=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{450 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex} {tex} \phi=2.76 {eV} {/tex} {tex} {K}_{\max } ={h} v-\phi {/tex} {tex} {h} v =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{250 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=4.97 {eV} {/tex} {tex} {K}_{\max } =4.97-2.76 {/tex} {tex} {~K}_{\max } =2.21 {eV} {/tex}
solution: {tex} \text {} \phi=\frac{{hc}}{\lambda_{{o}}} {/tex} {tex} \phi=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{450 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex} {tex} \phi=2.76 {eV} {/tex} {tex} {K}_{\max } ={h} v-\phi {/tex} {tex} {h} v =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{250 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=4.97 {eV} {/tex} {tex} {K}_{\max } =4.97-2.76 {/tex} {tex} {~K}_{\max } =2.21 {eV} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex} \text {} \phi=\frac{{hc}}{\lambda_{{o}}} {/tex}
    {tex} \phi=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{450 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex}
    {tex} \phi=2.76 {eV} {/tex}
  2. {tex} {K}_{\max } ={h} v-\phi {/tex}
    {tex} {h} v =\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{250 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=4.97 {eV} {/tex}
    {tex} {K}_{\max } =4.97-2.76 {/tex}
    {tex} {~K}_{\max } =2.21 {eV} {/tex}

question_html: Radiations of two frequencies are incident on a metal surface of work function 2.0 eV one by one. The energies of their photons are 2.5 eV and 4.5 eV respectively. Find the ratio of the maximum speed of the electrons emitted in the two cases.
question_text: Radiations of two frequencies are incident on a metal surface of work function 2.0 eV one by one. The energies of their photons are 2.5 eV and 4.5 eV respectively. Find the ratio of the maximum speed of the electrons emitted in the two cases.
explanation: {tex} {h} v=\phi_{{o}}+{K}_{\max } {/tex} For radiation having photons of energy 2.5 eV {tex} 2.5 {eV}=2 {eV}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_1^2 {/tex} {tex} 0.5 {eV}=\frac{1}{2} {mv}_1^2 \ldots ({i}) {/tex} For radiation having photons of energy 4.5 eV {tex} 4.5 {eV}=2 {eV}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_2^2 {/tex} {tex} 2.5 {eV}=\frac{1}{2} {mv}_2^2 \ldots(ii){/tex} Dividing eq. (i) by (ii): {tex} \frac{{v}_1}{{v}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{5}} {/tex} OR {tex} \frac{v_2}{v_1}=\sqrt{5} {/tex}
solution: {tex} {h} v=\phi_{{o}}+{K}_{\max } {/tex} For radiation having photons of energy 2.5 eV {tex} 2.5 {eV}=2 {eV}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_1^2 {/tex} {tex} 0.5 {eV}=\frac{1}{2} {mv}_1^2 \ldots ({i}) {/tex} For radiation having photons of energy 4.5 eV {tex} 4.5 {eV}=2 {eV}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_2^2 {/tex} {tex} 2.5 {eV}=\frac{1}{2} {mv}_2^2 \ldots(ii){/tex} Dividing eq. (i) by (ii): {tex} \frac{{v}_1}{{v}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{5}} {/tex} OR {tex} \frac{v_2}{v_1}=\sqrt{5} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} {h} v=\phi_{{o}}+{K}_{\max } {/tex}
For radiation having photons of energy 2.5 eV
{tex} 2.5 {eV}=2 {eV}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_1^2 {/tex}
{tex} 0.5 {eV}=\frac{1}{2} {mv}_1^2 \ldots ({i}) {/tex}
For radiation having photons of energy 4.5 eV
{tex} 4.5 {eV}=2 {eV}+\frac{1}{2} {mv}_2^2 {/tex}
{tex} 2.5 {eV}=\frac{1}{2} {mv}_2^2 \ldots(ii){/tex}
Dividing eq. (i) by (ii):
{tex} \frac{{v}_1}{{v}_2}=\sqrt{\frac{1}{5}} {/tex}
OR
{tex} \frac{v_2}{v_1}=\sqrt{5} {/tex}

question_html:
1. Draw a plot of frequency {tex}v{/tex} of incident radiations as a function of stopping potential {tex}V_{0}{/tex} for a given photo emissive material. What information can be obtained from the value of the intercept on the stopping potential axis?

question_text: Draw a plot of frequency {tex}v{/tex} of incident radiations as a function of stopping potential {tex}V_{0}{/tex} for a given photo emissive material. What information can be obtained from the value of the intercept on the stopping potential axis? Calculate: (i) the momentum and (ii) de Broglie wavelength, of an electron with kinetic energy of 80 eV.
explanation: Value of work function can be obtained from intercept. {tex} {p} =\sqrt{2 {mK}} {/tex} {tex} =\sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 80 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex} {tex} = 4.8 \times 10^{-24} {~kg} {~m} / {s} {/tex} {tex} \lambda =\frac{{h}}{{p}}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{4.8 \times 10^{-25}}=1.38 \times 10^{-9} {~m} {/tex}
solution: Value of work function can be obtained from intercept. {tex} {p} =\sqrt{2 {mK}} {/tex} {tex} =\sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 80 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex} {tex} = 4.8 \times 10^{-24} {~kg} {~m} / {s} {/tex} {tex} \lambda =\frac{{h}}{{p}}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{4.8 \times 10^{-25}}=1.38 \times 10^{-9} {~m} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. Value of work function can be obtained from intercept.
    1. {tex} {p} =\sqrt{2 {mK}} {/tex}
      {tex} =\sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 80 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex}
      {tex} = 4.8 \times 10^{-24} {~kg} {~m} / {s} {/tex}
    2. {tex} \lambda =\frac{{h}}{{p}}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{4.8 \times 10^{-25}}=1.38 \times 10^{-9} {~m} {/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
Explain the following observations using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

  1. Photoelectric emission does not occur from a surface when the frequency of the light incident on it is less than a certain minimum value.
  2. It is the frequency, and not the intensity of the incident light which affects the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons.
  3. The cut-off voltage ({tex}{V}_{{o}}{/tex}) versus frequency ({tex}v{/tex}) of the incident light curve is a straight line with a slope {tex}\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex}.
question_text: Explain the following observations using Einstein's photoelectric equation: Photoelectric emission does not occur from a surface when the frequency of the light incident on it is less than a certain minimum value. It is the frequency, and not the intensity of the incident light which affects the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons. The cut-off voltage ( {tex}{V}_{{o}}{/tex} ) versus frequency ( {tex}v{/tex} ) of the incident light curve is a straight line with a slope {tex}\frac{{h}}{{e}}{/tex} .
explanation: Einstein Photo electric equation {tex}{h} v={h} v_{o}+{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex} For {tex}v \lt v_{{o}}, K_{\text {max }}{/tex} will be negative Hence, Photoelectric emission is not possible. According to Einstein Photoelectric equation {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex} Hence {tex}{K}_{\text {max }} \alpha v{/tex} It shows {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} depends upon frequency only and not depends upon intensity {tex}{eV}_{{o}}={h} \nu-{h} \nu_{{o}}{/tex} {tex}{V}_{{o}}=\frac{{h}}{{e}} v-\frac{{h}}{{e}} v_{{o}}{/tex} This equation represents the equation of straight line ( {tex}y=m x+c{/tex} ) with the slope {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex} .
solution: Einstein Photo electric equation {tex}{h} v={h} v_{o}+{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex} For {tex}v \lt v_{{o}}, K_{\text {max }}{/tex} will be negative Hence, Photoelectric emission is not possible. According to Einstein Photoelectric equation {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex} Hence {tex}{K}_{\text {max }} \alpha v{/tex} It shows {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} depends upon frequency only and not depends upon intensity {tex}{eV}_{{o}}={h} \nu-{h} \nu_{{o}}{/tex} {tex}{V}_{{o}}=\frac{{h}}{{e}} v-\frac{{h}}{{e}} v_{{o}}{/tex} This equation represents the equation of straight line ( {tex}y=m x+c{/tex} ) with the slope {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. Einstein Photo electric equation
    {tex}{h} v={h} v_{o}+{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex}
    {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex}
    For {tex}v \lt v_{{o}}, K_{\text {max }}{/tex} will be negative
    Hence, Photoelectric emission is not possible.
  2. According to Einstein Photoelectric equation
    {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}={h}\left(v-v_{o}\right){/tex}
    Hence {tex}{K}_{\text {max }} \alpha v{/tex}
    It shows {tex}{K}_{\text {max }}{/tex} depends upon frequency only and not depends upon intensity
  3. {tex}{eV}_{{o}}={h} \nu-{h} \nu_{{o}}{/tex}
    {tex}{V}_{{o}}=\frac{{h}}{{e}} v-\frac{{h}}{{e}} v_{{o}}{/tex}
    This equation represents the equation of straight line ({tex}y=m x+c{/tex}) with the slope {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex}.

question_html:
Answer the following giving reason:

  1. All the photo electrons do not eject with the same kinetic energy when monochromatic light is incident on a metal surface.
  2. The saturation current in case (a) is different for different intensity.
  3. If one goes on increasing the wavelength of light incident on a metal surface, keeping its intensity constant, emission of photo electrons stop at a certain wavelength for this metal.
question_text: Answer the following giving reason: All the photo electrons do not eject with the same kinetic energy when monochromatic light is incident on a metal surface. The saturation current in case (a) is different for different intensity. If one goes on increasing the wavelength of light incident on a metal surface, keeping its intensity constant, emission of photo electrons stop at a certain wavelength for this metal.
explanation: When monochromatic light is incident on a metal surface then more/less tightly bound electrons will emerge with less/more kinetic energy. So all the photoelectrons do not eject with same kinetic energy. Maximum number of photoelectrons ejected per second (saturation current) is directly proportional to the Intensity of incident radiation Hence saturation current is different for different intensities. when {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases, {tex}\nu{/tex} decreases and energy of incident photon ( {tex}{h} \nu{/tex} ) also decreases. When {tex}\lambda \gt \lambda_{0}, v \lt v_{0}{/tex} (threshold frequency), no photoelectron is ejected. Emission of photoelectrons stop at {tex}\lambda \gt \lambda_{0}{/tex} .
solution: When monochromatic light is incident on a metal surface then more/less tightly bound electrons will emerge with less/more kinetic energy. So all the photoelectrons do not eject with same kinetic energy. Maximum number of photoelectrons ejected per second (saturation current) is directly proportional to the Intensity of incident radiation Hence saturation current is different for different intensities. when {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases, {tex}\nu{/tex} decreases and energy of incident photon ( {tex}{h} \nu{/tex} ) also decreases. When {tex}\lambda \gt \lambda_{0}, v \lt v_{0}{/tex} (threshold frequency), no photoelectron is ejected. Emission of photoelectrons stop at {tex}\lambda \gt \lambda_{0}{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. When monochromatic light is incident on a metal surface then more/less tightly bound electrons will emerge with less/more kinetic energy. So all the photoelectrons do not eject with same kinetic energy.
  2. Maximum number of photoelectrons ejected per second (saturation current) is directly proportional to the Intensity of incident radiation Hence saturation current is different for different intensities.
  3. when {tex}\lambda{/tex} increases, {tex}\nu{/tex} decreases and energy of incident photon ({tex}{h} \nu{/tex}) also decreases. When {tex}\lambda \gt \lambda_{0}, v \lt v_{0}{/tex} (threshold frequency), no photoelectron is ejected. Emission of photoelectrons stop at {tex}\lambda \gt \lambda_{0}{/tex}.

question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
Einstein explained photoelectric effect on the basis of Planck's quantum theory, where light travels in the form of small bundles of energy called photons. The energy of each photon is {tex}{h} v{/tex}, where {tex}v{/tex} is the frequency of incident light and {tex}h{/tex} is Planck's constant. The number of photons in a beam of light determines the intensity of the incident light. A photon incident on a metal surface transfers its total energy {tex}h v{/tex} to a free electron in the metal. A part of this energy is used in ejecting the electron from the metal and is called its work function. The rest of the energy is carried by the ejected electron as its kinetic energy.

  1. Which of the following graphs shows the variation of photoelectric current I with the intensity of light?
  2.  When the frequency of the incident light is increased without changing its intensity, the saturation current:
    1. increases linearly
    2. decreases
    3. increases non-linearly
    4. remains the same
  3.  Which of the following graphs can be used to obtain the value of Planck's constant?
    1. Photocurrent versus Intensity of incident light
    2. Photocurrent versus Frequency of incident light
    3. Cut-off potential versus Frequency of incident light
    4. Cut-off potential versus Intensity of incident light
    1. Red light, yellow light and blue light of the same intensity are incident on a metal surface successively. {tex}{K}_{{R}}, {K}_{{Y}}{/tex} and {tex}{K}_{{B}}{/tex} represent the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons respectively, then:
      1. {tex}{K}_{{R}}>{K}_{{Y}}>{K}_{{B}}{/tex}
      2. {tex}{K}_{{Y}}>{K}_{{B}}>{K}_{{R}}{/tex}
      3. {tex}{K}_{{B}}>{K}_{{Y}}>{K}_{{R}}{/tex}
      4. {tex}{K}_{{R}}>{K}_{{B}}>{K}_{{Y}}{/tex}

        OR

    2. Which of the following metals exhibits photoelectric effect with visible light?
      1. Caesium
      2. Zinc
      3. Cadmium
      4. Magnesium

question_html:
Answer the following, giving reasons:

  1. The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of incident radiation.
  2. Photoelectric current increases with the increase in the intensity of the incident radiation.
  3. The stopping potential {tex}{V}_{0}{/tex} varies linearly with the frequency {tex}v{/tex} of the incident radiation for a given photosensitive surface.
question_text: Answer the following, giving reasons: The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of incident radiation. Photoelectric current increases with the increase in the intensity of the incident radiation. The stopping potential {tex}{V}_{0}{/tex} varies linearly with the frequency {tex}v{/tex} of the incident radiation for a given photosensitive surface.
explanation: With the variation in the intensity of incident radiations, there is a change in the number of photons not on the energies of the photons so the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons remains same. With the increase in intensity of incident radiation number of photons increases as a result photoelectric current increase. With the increase in frequency of incident radiation , the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons increases. Hence higher value of stopping potential (retarding potential) is required.
solution: With the variation in the intensity of incident radiations, there is a change in the number of photons not on the energies of the photons so the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons remains same. With the increase in intensity of incident radiation number of photons increases as a result photoelectric current increase. With the increase in frequency of incident radiation , the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons increases. Hence higher value of stopping potential (retarding potential) is required.
solution_html:
1. With the variation in the intensity of incident radiations, there is a change in the number of photons not on the energies of the photons so the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons remains same.
  2. With the increase in intensity of incident radiation number of photons increases as a result photoelectric current increase.
  3. With the increase in frequency of incident radiation , the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons increases. Hence higher value of stopping potential (retarding potential) is required.

What will happen if the laser source is brought closer to a distance of 50 cm?
explanation: {tex} {h} v=\phi_0+K_{\text {max }}{/tex} {tex}{h} v={/tex} Energy of incident radiation {tex}\phi_0={/tex} Work function or minimum energy required to emit an electron from metal surface {tex}K_{\max }={/tex} maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron {tex}\lambda=330 {~nm}{/tex} {tex} E =\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{330 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex} {tex} =3.77 {eV} {/tex} Mo and Ni will not show photoelectric emission.
solution: {tex} {h} v=\phi_0+K_{\text {max }}{/tex} {tex}{h} v={/tex} Energy of incident radiation {tex}\phi_0={/tex} Work function or minimum energy required to emit an electron from metal surface {tex}K_{\max }={/tex} maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron {tex}\lambda=330 {~nm}{/tex} {tex} E =\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{330 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex} {tex} =3.77 {eV} {/tex} Mo and Ni will not show photoelectric emission.
solution_html:
1. {tex} {h} v=\phi_0+K_{\text {max }}{/tex}
    {tex}{h} v={/tex} Energy of incident radiation
    {tex}\phi_0={/tex} Work function or minimum energy required to emit an electron from metal surface
    {tex}K_{\max }={/tex} maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron
  2. {tex}\lambda=330 {~nm}{/tex}
    {tex} E =\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{330 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} {/tex}
    {tex} =3.77 {eV} {/tex}
    Mo and Ni will not show photoelectric emission.

When a photon of suitable frequency is incident on a metal surface, photoelectron is emitted from it. If the frequency is below a threshold frequency ({tex}v_{{o}}{/tex}) for the surface, no photoelectron is emitted. For a photon of frequency {tex}v\left(v>v_{{o}}\right){/tex}, the kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is {tex}{h}\left(v-v_{{o}}\right){/tex}. The photocurrent can be stopped by applying a potential {tex}{V}_{{o}}{/tex} called 'stopping potential' on the anode. Thus maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons {tex}{K}_{{m}}={eV}_{{o}}={h}\left(v-v_{{o}}\right){/tex}. The experimental graph between {tex}{V}_{{o}}{/tex} and {tex}v{/tex} for a metal is shown in figure. This is a straight line of slope m.

question_html: Consider a neutron (mass m) of kinetic energy E and a photon of the same energy. Let {tex}\lambda_{{n}}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{{p}}{/tex} be the de Broglie wavelength of neutron and the wavelength of photon respectively. Obtain an expression for {tex}\frac{\lambda_{{n}}}{\lambda_{{p}}}{/tex}.
question_text: Consider a neutron (mass m) of kinetic energy E and a photon of the same energy. Let {tex}\lambda_{{n}}{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_{{p}}{/tex} be the de Broglie wavelength of neutron and the wavelength of photon respectively. Obtain an expression for {tex}\frac{\lambda_{{n}}}{\lambda_{{p}}}{/tex} .
explanation: {tex} E=\frac{h c}{\lambda p} \Rightarrow \lambda p=\frac{h c}{E} {/tex} {tex} \lambda n=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{(2 m E})} {/tex} {tex} \frac{\lambda n}{\lambda p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{(2 m E})} \times \frac{E}{h c} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda \mathrm{n}}{\lambda \mathrm{p}}=\sqrt{\frac{E}{2mc^2}}{/tex}
solution: {tex} E=\frac{h c}{\lambda p} \Rightarrow \lambda p=\frac{h c}{E} {/tex} {tex} \lambda n=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{(2 m E})} {/tex} {tex} \frac{\lambda n}{\lambda p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{(2 m E})} \times \frac{E}{h c} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda \mathrm{n}}{\lambda \mathrm{p}}=\sqrt{\frac{E}{2mc^2}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex} E=\frac{h c}{\lambda p} \Rightarrow \lambda p=\frac{h c}{E} {/tex}
{tex} \lambda n=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{(2 m E})} {/tex}
{tex} \frac{\lambda n}{\lambda p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{(2 m E})} \times \frac{E}{h c} {/tex}
{tex}\frac{\lambda \mathrm{n}}{\lambda \mathrm{p}}=\sqrt{\frac{E}{2mc^2}}{/tex}

question_text: The work function for a photosensitive surface is 3.315 eV. The cut-off wavelength for photoemission of electrons from this surface is

question_text: Briefly explain de Broglie hypothesis for wave nature of matter. Find the ratio of de Broglie wavelength associated with a proton and an alpha particle when both are accelerated from rest through the same potential difference, and moving with the same kinetic energy.
explanation: oving particles of matter display wave like properties under suitable conditions. The wave length {tex}\lambda{/tex} associated with a particle of momentum {tex}p{/tex} is given as {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the attribute of a wave while momentum is a typical attribute of particle. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 4 m_p \times 2 e \times V}}{\sqrt{2 \times m_p \times e \times V}}{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 4 m_p \times K}}{\sqrt{2 \times m_p \times K}}{/tex} = 2
solution: oving particles of matter display wave like properties under suitable conditions. The wave length {tex}\lambda{/tex} associated with a particle of momentum {tex}p{/tex} is given as {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the attribute of a wave while momentum is a typical attribute of particle. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 4 m_p \times 2 e \times V}}{\sqrt{2 \times m_p \times e \times V}}{/tex} {tex}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 4 m_p \times K}}{\sqrt{2 \times m_p \times K}}{/tex} = 2
solution_html:
1. oving particles of matter display wave like properties under suitable conditions.
    The wave length {tex}\lambda{/tex} associated with a particle of momentum {tex}p{/tex} is given as
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{m v}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the attribute of a wave while momentum is a typical attribute of particle.
    1. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 4 m_p \times 2 e \times V}}{\sqrt{2 \times m_p \times e \times V}}{/tex}
      {tex}=2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

    2. {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 4 m_p \times K}}{\sqrt{2 \times m_p \times K}}{/tex}
      = 2

question_html: The momentum (in SI units) associated with a photon of energy 1.5 eV is:
question_text: The momentum (in SI units) associated with a photon of energy 1.5 eV is:

options.explanation: To find the momentum of a photon with energy 1.5 eV , we use the formula {tex}p=\frac{E}{c}{/tex} , where {tex}E{/tex} is energy and {tex}c{/tex} is the speed of light ( {tex}3 \times 10^8 {~m} / {s}{/tex} ). First, convert the energy to joules: {tex}1.5 {eV}={/tex} {tex}1.5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} {~J}=2.4 \times 10^{-19} {~J}{/tex} . Now, calculating momentum gives: {tex} p=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-19} {~J}}{3 \times 10^8 {~m} / {s}}=8 \times 10^{-28} {~kg} {~m} / {s}{/tex}

explanation: To find the momentum of a photon with energy 1.5 eV , we use the formula {tex}p=\frac{E}{c}{/tex} , where {tex}E{/tex} is energy and {tex}c{/tex} is the speed of light ( {tex}3 \times 10^8 {~m} / {s}{/tex} ). First, convert the energy to joules: {tex}1.5 {eV}={/tex} {tex}1.5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} {~J}=2.4 \times 10^{-19} {~J}{/tex} . Now, calculating momentum gives: {tex} p=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-19} {~J}}{3 \times 10^8 {~m} / {s}}=8 \times 10^{-28} {~kg} {~m} / {s}{/tex}

solution: {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex} =\frac{1240 {eV} {~nm}}{600 {~nm}} {/tex} {tex} =2.06 {eV} {/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} Work function {tex}\phi_0=2.3 {eV}{/tex} {tex}\because E<\phi_0 {/tex} No emission will take place. {tex} \lambda_{\max }= \frac{h c}{\phi} {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1240 {eV} {~nm}}{2.3 {eV}} {/tex} {tex} \lambda_{\max }= 539.13 {~nm} {/tex}

{tex} =\frac{1240 {eV} {~nm}}{600 {~nm}} {/tex}
{tex} =2.06 {eV} {/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Work function {tex}\phi_0=2.3 {eV}{/tex}
{tex}\because E<\phi_0 {/tex} No emission will take place.

question_html: Light of wavelength 500 nm is incident on caesium metal (work function 2.14 eV) and photoemission of electrons occurs. Calculate the (i) kinetic energy (in eV) of the fastest electrons and (ii) stopping potential for this situation. (Take hc = 1240 eV.nm)
question_text: Light of wavelength 500 nm is incident on caesium metal (work function 2.14 eV) and photoemission of electrons occurs. Calculate the (i) kinetic energy (in eV) of the fastest electrons and (ii) stopping potential for this situation. (Take hc = 1240 eV.nm)
explanation: Using Einstein Photoelectric equation {tex} \frac{h c}{\lambda} =K \cdot E_{\max }+\phi_0 {/tex} {tex} K \cdot E_{\max } =\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi_0 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1240 {eVnm}}{500 {~nm}}-2.14 {eV} {/tex} {tex} K \cdot E_{\max } =0.34 {eV} {/tex} {tex} K \cdot E_{\max } =e V_0 {/tex} {tex} \therefore V_0 =0.34 {~V} {/tex}
solution: Using Einstein Photoelectric equation {tex} \frac{h c}{\lambda} =K \cdot E_{\max }+\phi_0 {/tex} {tex} K \cdot E_{\max } =\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi_0 {/tex} {tex} =\frac{1240 {eVnm}}{500 {~nm}}-2.14 {eV} {/tex} {tex} K \cdot E_{\max } =0.34 {eV} {/tex} {tex} K \cdot E_{\max } =e V_0 {/tex} {tex} \therefore V_0 =0.34 {~V} {/tex}
solution_html:
Using Einstein Photoelectric equation
{tex} \frac{h c}{\lambda} =K \cdot E_{\max }+\phi_0 {/tex}
{tex} K \cdot E_{\max } =\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi_0 {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{1240 {eVnm}}{500 {~nm}}-2.14 {eV} {/tex}
{tex} K \cdot E_{\max } =0.34 {eV} {/tex}
{tex} K \cdot E_{\max } =e V_0 {/tex}
{tex} \therefore V_0 =0.34 {~V} {/tex}

The minimum intensity of white light that our eyes can perceive is about {tex}0.1 {nWm}^{-2}{/tex}. Calculate the number of photons of this light entering our pupil (area {tex}0.4 {~cm}^2{/tex}) per second.
(Take average wavelength of white light {tex}=500 {~nm}{/tex} and Planck's constant {tex}\left.=6.6 \times 10^{-34} {Js}\right){/tex}
question_text: The minimum intensity of white light that our eyes can perceive is about {tex}0.1 {nWm}^{-2}{/tex} . Calculate the number of photons of this light entering our pupil (area {tex}0.4 {~cm}^2{/tex} ) per second. (Take average wavelength of white light {tex}=500 {~nm}{/tex} and Planck's constant {tex}\left.=6.6 \times 10^{-34} {Js}\right){/tex}
explanation: Total Energy gained per second from photon = IA {tex}E=N h v{/tex} {tex} I A =N \times \frac{h c}{\lambda} {/tex} {tex} N =\frac{[I A] \lambda}{h c} {/tex} {tex} N =\frac{\left[0.1 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.4 \times 10^{-4}\right] \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8} {/tex} {tex} N =1.01 \times 10^4 {/tex}
solution: Total Energy gained per second from photon = IA {tex}E=N h v{/tex} {tex} I A =N \times \frac{h c}{\lambda} {/tex} {tex} N =\frac{[I A] \lambda}{h c} {/tex} {tex} N =\frac{\left[0.1 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.4 \times 10^{-4}\right] \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8} {/tex} {tex} N =1.01 \times 10^4 {/tex}
solution_html:
Total Energy gained per second from photon = IA
{tex}E=N h v{/tex}
{tex} I A =N \times \frac{h c}{\lambda} {/tex}
{tex} N =\frac{[I A] \lambda}{h c} {/tex}
{tex} N =\frac{\left[0.1 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.4 \times 10^{-4}\right] \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8} {/tex}
{tex} N =1.01 \times 10^4 {/tex}

question_text: What are matter waves? A proton, an electron and an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle have the same kinetic energy. Write the de Broglie wavelengths associated with them in increasing order.
explanation: The waves associated with every moving particle are called matter waves. {tex}\lambda=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {mK}}}{/tex} For same kinetic energy, {tex}\lambda \alpha \frac{1}{\sqrt{{~m}}}{/tex} {tex}{m}_{\alpha} \gt {m}_{{p}} \gt {m}_{{e}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda_{\alpha} \lt \lambda_{{p}} \lt \lambda_{{e}}{/tex}
solution: The waves associated with every moving particle are called matter waves. {tex}\lambda=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {mK}}}{/tex} For same kinetic energy, {tex}\lambda \alpha \frac{1}{\sqrt{{~m}}}{/tex} {tex}{m}_{\alpha} \gt {m}_{{p}} \gt {m}_{{e}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda_{\alpha} \lt \lambda_{{p}} \lt \lambda_{{e}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The waves associated with every moving particle are called matter waves.
{tex}\lambda=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {mK}}}{/tex}
For same kinetic energy, {tex}\lambda \alpha \frac{1}{\sqrt{{~m}}}{/tex}
{tex}{m}_{\alpha} \gt {m}_{{p}} \gt {m}_{{e}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \lambda_{\alpha} \lt \lambda_{{p}} \lt \lambda_{{e}}{/tex}

question_html:
1. What are matter waves? A particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge {tex}q{/tex} is accelerated from rest through a potential difference V. Obtain an expression for de Broglie wavelength associated with the particle.
  2. Monochromatic light of frequency {tex}5.0 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} is produced by a source of power output 3.315 mW. Calculate:
    1. energy of the photon in the beam
    2. number of photons emitted per second by the source
question_text: What are matter waves? A particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge {tex}q{/tex} is accelerated from rest through a potential difference V. Obtain an expression for de Broglie wavelength associated with the particle. Monochromatic light of frequency {tex}5.0 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} is produced by a source of power output 3.315 mW. Calculate: energy of the photon in the beam number of photons emitted per second by the source
explanation: Wave associated with a mass in motion is called matter wave. Particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge q gains energy in the form of kinetic energy. {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=q V 1 / 2{/tex} {tex}(m v)^{2}=2 q V m 1 / 2{/tex} {tex}m v=\sqrt{2 m q V}{/tex} Accordingly to de-Broglie relation {tex}\lambda=\frac{{h}}{{mv}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {mqV}}}{/tex} {tex}{E}={hv} 1 / 2{/tex} {tex}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 5 \times 10^{14}{/tex} {tex}=3.315 \times 10^{-19} {~J}{/tex} {tex}n=\frac{P}{E}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3.315 \times 10^{-3}}{3.315 \times 10^{-19}}=10^{16} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}
solution: Wave associated with a mass in motion is called matter wave. Particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge q gains energy in the form of kinetic energy. {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=q V 1 / 2{/tex} {tex}(m v)^{2}=2 q V m 1 / 2{/tex} {tex}m v=\sqrt{2 m q V}{/tex} Accordingly to de-Broglie relation {tex}\lambda=\frac{{h}}{{mv}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {mqV}}}{/tex} {tex}{E}={hv} 1 / 2{/tex} {tex}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 5 \times 10^{14}{/tex} {tex}=3.315 \times 10^{-19} {~J}{/tex} {tex}n=\frac{P}{E}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{3.315 \times 10^{-3}}{3.315 \times 10^{-19}}=10^{16} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Wave associated with a mass in motion is called matter wave.
    Particle of mass {tex}m{/tex} and charge q gains energy in the form of kinetic energy.
    {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=q V 1 / 2{/tex}
    {tex}(m v)^{2}=2 q V m 1 / 2{/tex}
    {tex}m v=\sqrt{2 m q V}{/tex}
    Accordingly to de-Broglie relation
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{{h}}{{mv}}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {mqV}}}{/tex}
    1. {tex}{E}={hv} 1 / 2{/tex}
      {tex}=6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 5 \times 10^{14}{/tex}
      {tex}=3.315 \times 10^{-19} {~J}{/tex}
    2. {tex}n=\frac{P}{E}{/tex}
      {tex}=\frac{3.315 \times 10^{-3}}{3.315 \times 10^{-19}}=10^{16} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}

question_html: The threshold frequency for a metal is {tex}3.0 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex}. A beam of frequency {tex}9.0 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} is incident on the metal. Calculate (i) the work function (in eV) of the metal and (ii) the maximum speed of photoelectrons.
question_text: The threshold frequency for a metal is {tex}3.0 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} . A beam of frequency {tex}9.0 \times 10^{14} {~Hz}{/tex} is incident on the metal. Calculate (i) the work function (in eV) of the metal and (ii) the maximum speed of photoelectrons.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\phi_{0}={hv}_{0}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3.0 \times 10^{14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
    {tex}=1.24 {eV}{/tex}
  2. {tex}{K}_{\max }={hv}-{hv}_{0}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{1}{2} {mV}_{\max }^{2}={h}\left({v}-{v}_{0}\right){/tex}
    {tex}{~V}_{\max }=\left[\frac{2 {~h}\left({v}-{v}_{0}\right)}{{m}}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}{~V}_{\max } =\left[\frac{2 \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}(9-3) \times 10^{14}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}=9.35 \times 10^{5} {~m} / {s}{/tex}

question_html: A proton and {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle are accelerated through different potentials {tex}V_{1}{/tex} and {tex}V_{2}{/tex} respectively so that they have the same de Broglie wavelengths. Find {tex}\frac{{V}_{1}}{{~V}_{2}}{/tex}.
question_text: A proton and {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle are accelerated through different potentials {tex}V_{1}{/tex} and {tex}V_{2}{/tex} respectively so that they have the same de Broglie wavelengths. Find {tex}\frac{{V}_{1}}{{~V}_{2}}{/tex} .
explanation: De Broglie wavelength of a proton {tex}\lambda_{{p}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {~m}_{{p}} {q}_{{p}} {~V}_1}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_1}}{/tex} De Broglie wavelength of an {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle {tex}\lambda_\alpha=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {~m}_\alpha {q}_\alpha {V}_2}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2(4 {~m})(2 {e}) {V}_2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{{p}}=\lambda_\alpha{/tex} {tex}\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_1}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{16 {meV}_2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{~V}_1}{{~V}_2}=8{/tex}
solution: De Broglie wavelength of a proton {tex}\lambda_{{p}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {~m}_{{p}} {q}_{{p}} {~V}_1}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_1}}{/tex} De Broglie wavelength of an {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle {tex}\lambda_\alpha=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {~m}_\alpha {q}_\alpha {V}_2}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2(4 {~m})(2 {e}) {V}_2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{{p}}=\lambda_\alpha{/tex} {tex}\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_1}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{16 {meV}_2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{~V}_1}{{~V}_2}=8{/tex}
solution_html:
De Broglie wavelength of a proton
{tex}\lambda_{{p}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {~m}_{{p}} {q}_{{p}} {~V}_1}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_1}}{/tex}
De Broglie wavelength of an {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle
{tex}\lambda_\alpha=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {~m}_\alpha {q}_\alpha {V}_2}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2(4 {~m})(2 {e}) {V}_2}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_{{p}}=\lambda_\alpha{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_1}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{16 {meV}_2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{{~V}_1}{{~V}_2}=8{/tex}

question_html: A photosensitive surface of work function 2.1 eV is irradiated by radiation of wavelength 150 nm. Calculate (i) the threshold wavelength, (ii) energy (in eV) of an incident photon, and (iii) maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectron.
question_text: A photosensitive surface of work function 2.1 eV is irradiated by radiation of wavelength 150 nm. Calculate (i) the threshold wavelength, (ii) energy (in eV) of an incident photon, and (iii) maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectron.
explanation: {tex}\phi_{0}=\frac{{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2.1 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} {tex}=5.92 \times 10^{-7} {~m}{/tex} Energy of incident photon {tex}=\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{150 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} {tex}=8.29 {eV}{/tex} Using Einstein equation {tex} \frac{h c}{\lambda}=\phi_0+K_{\max } {/tex} {tex} K_{\max } =(8.29-2.1) {eV} {/tex} {tex} =6.2 {eV} {/tex}
solution: {tex}\phi_{0}=\frac{{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2.1 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} {tex}=5.92 \times 10^{-7} {~m}{/tex} Energy of incident photon {tex}=\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{150 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} {tex}=8.29 {eV}{/tex} Using Einstein equation {tex} \frac{h c}{\lambda}=\phi_0+K_{\max } {/tex} {tex} K_{\max } =(8.29-2.1) {eV} {/tex} {tex} =6.2 {eV} {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\phi_{0}=\frac{{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex}

  1. {tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2.1 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
    {tex}=5.92 \times 10^{-7} {~m}{/tex}
  2. Energy of incident photon {tex}=\frac{{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{150 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
    {tex}=8.29 {eV}{/tex}
  3. Using Einstein equation
    {tex} \frac{h c}{\lambda}=\phi_0+K_{\max } {/tex}
    {tex} K_{\max } =(8.29-2.1) {eV} {/tex}
    {tex} =6.2 {eV} {/tex}

question_html: The ratio of de Broglie wavelengths of a proton and a deuteron accelerated by potential {tex}{V}_{{p}}{/tex} and {tex}{V}_{{d}}{/tex} respectively, {tex}\left(\frac{\lambda_{{p}}}{\lambda_{{d}}}\right){/tex} is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}. Find {tex}\frac{{V}_{{p}}}{{V}_{{d}}}{/tex}.
question_text: The ratio of de Broglie wavelengths of a proton and a deuteron accelerated by potential {tex}{V}_{{p}}{/tex} and {tex}{V}_{{d}}{/tex} respectively, {tex}\left(\frac{\lambda_{{p}}}{\lambda_{{d}}}\right){/tex} is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} . Find {tex}\frac{{V}_{{p}}}{{V}_{{d}}}{/tex} .
explanation: De Broglie wavelength of proton {tex}\lambda_{{p}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_{{p}}}}{/tex} De Broglie wavelength of deutron {tex}\lambda_{{d}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2(2 {~m}) {eV}_{{d}}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_{{p}}}{\lambda_{{d}}}=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{2(2 {~m}) {eV}_{{d}}}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_{{p}}}}{/tex} On solving {tex}\frac{V_{p}}{V_{d}}=8{/tex}
solution: De Broglie wavelength of proton {tex}\lambda_{{p}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_{{p}}}}{/tex} De Broglie wavelength of deutron {tex}\lambda_{{d}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2(2 {~m}) {eV}_{{d}}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_{{p}}}{\lambda_{{d}}}=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{2(2 {~m}) {eV}_{{d}}}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_{{p}}}}{/tex} On solving {tex}\frac{V_{p}}{V_{d}}=8{/tex}
solution_html:
De Broglie wavelength of proton
{tex}\lambda_{{p}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_{{p}}}}{/tex}
De Broglie wavelength of deutron
{tex}\lambda_{{d}}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2(2 {~m}) {eV}_{{d}}}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\lambda_{{p}}}{\lambda_{{d}}}=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{\sqrt{2(2 {~m}) {eV}_{{d}}}}{\sqrt{2 {meV}_{{p}}}}{/tex}
On solving
{tex}\frac{V_{p}}{V_{d}}=8{/tex}

question_text: The figure shows {tex}\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{m}}^2{/tex} versus {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} graph for photoelectrons emitted from a surface where v m is the maximum speed of electrons and {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the wavelength of incident radiation. Using this graph and Einstein's photoelectric equation, obtain the expression for Planck's constant and work function of the surface.
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_m^2=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi_0{/tex} {tex}v_m^2=\left(\frac{2 h c}{m}\right) \frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{2}{m} \phi_0{/tex} According to this equation a plot of {tex}\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{m}}^2{/tex} versus ( {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} ) is a straight line. Slope of the graph = {tex}\frac{2 h c}{m}{/tex} Intercept = {tex}\frac{2}{m} \phi_0{/tex} Slope and intercept can be found from the graph {tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{m}{2 c} \times{/tex} slope {tex}\phi_0=\frac{m}{2} \times{/tex} intercept
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_m^2=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi_0{/tex} {tex}v_m^2=\left(\frac{2 h c}{m}\right) \frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{2}{m} \phi_0{/tex} According to this equation a plot of {tex}\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{m}}^2{/tex} versus ( {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} ) is a straight line. Slope of the graph = {tex}\frac{2 h c}{m}{/tex} Intercept = {tex}\frac{2}{m} \phi_0{/tex} Slope and intercept can be found from the graph {tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{m}{2 c} \times{/tex} slope {tex}\phi_0=\frac{m}{2} \times{/tex} intercept
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_m^2=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\phi_0{/tex}
{tex}v_m^2=\left(\frac{2 h c}{m}\right) \frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{2}{m} \phi_0{/tex}
According to this equation a plot of {tex}\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{m}}^2{/tex} versus ({tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex}) is a straight line.
Slope of the graph = {tex}\frac{2 h c}{m}{/tex}
Intercept = {tex}\frac{2}{m} \phi_0{/tex}
Slope and intercept can be found from the graph
{tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{m}{2 c} \times{/tex} slope
{tex}\phi_0=\frac{m}{2} \times{/tex} intercept

explanation: Effect of frequency of incident radiation on stopping potential With increase in frequency stopping potential will increase. {tex}V_0=\frac{h}{e} v-\frac{\phi_0}{e}{/tex} Effect of intensity of incident radiation on stopping potential Stopping potential remains same. Energy of photoelectron ejected is independent of intensity of radiation
solution: Effect of frequency of incident radiation on stopping potential With increase in frequency stopping potential will increase. {tex}V_0=\frac{h}{e} v-\frac{\phi_0}{e}{/tex} Effect of intensity of incident radiation on stopping potential Stopping potential remains same. Energy of photoelectron ejected is independent of intensity of radiation
solution_html:
1. Effect of frequency of incident radiation on stopping potential
    With increase in frequency stopping potential will increase.
    {tex}V_0=\frac{h}{e} v-\frac{\phi_0}{e}{/tex}
  2. Effect of intensity of incident radiation on stopping potential
    Stopping potential remains same.
    Energy of photoelectron ejected is independent of intensity of radiation

question_html:
How does Einstein's photoelectric equation explain the emission of electrons from a metal surface? Explain briefly.
Plot the variation of photocurrent with:

  1. collector plate potential for different intensity of incident radiation, and
  2. intensity of incident radiation.
question_text: How does Einstein's photoelectric equation explain the emission of electrons from a metal surface? Explain briefly. Plot the variation of photocurrent with: collector plate potential for different intensity of incident radiation, and intensity of incident radiation.
explanation: According to Einstein’s photoelectric equation An electron absorbs a quantum of energy ‘h {tex}\nu{/tex} ’ of incident radiation. If the energy of absorbed quantum exceeds the minimum energy needed by the electron to escape from the metal surface (work function {tex}\varphi_0{/tex} ), the electron is emitted. this phenomena is called photoelectric effect. {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\mathrm{h} v-\varphi_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
solution: According to Einstein’s photoelectric equation An electron absorbs a quantum of energy ‘h {tex}\nu{/tex} ’ of incident radiation. If the energy of absorbed quantum exceeds the minimum energy needed by the electron to escape from the metal surface (work function {tex}\varphi_0{/tex} ), the electron is emitted. this phenomena is called photoelectric effect. {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\mathrm{h} v-\varphi_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
solution_html:
According to Einstein’s photoelectric equation An electron absorbs a quantum of energy ‘h{tex}\nu{/tex}’ of incident radiation. If the energy of absorbed quantum exceeds the minimum energy needed by the electron to escape from the metal surface (work function {tex}\varphi_0{/tex}), the electron is emitted. this phenomena is called photoelectric effect.
{tex}\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\mathrm{h} v-\varphi_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html: The threshold wavelength for a metal is 3315 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}. What should be the wavelength of light incident on the metal surface so that the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons be 1.25 eV?
question_text: The threshold wavelength for a metal is 3315 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} . What should be the wavelength of light incident on the metal surface so that the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons be 1.25 eV?
explanation: {tex}\lambda_o=3315 \overset oA{/tex} K max = 1.25 eV = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 J Work function = {tex}\phi_o=\frac{h c}{\lambda_o}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{3315 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 J Using hv = {tex}\phi_o+K_{\max }{/tex} {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\phi_o+K_{\max }{/tex} = (0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 + 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 )J = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 J {tex}\lambda=\frac{h c}{0.8 \times 10^{-18}}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{0.8 \times 10^{-18}}{/tex} = 24.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 m = 2486 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m = 2486 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} Hence the wavelength of light incident on the metal surface be 2486 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} .
solution: {tex}\lambda_o=3315 \overset oA{/tex} K max = 1.25 eV = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 J Work function = {tex}\phi_o=\frac{h c}{\lambda_o}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{3315 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 J Using hv = {tex}\phi_o+K_{\max }{/tex} {tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\phi_o+K_{\max }{/tex} = (0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 + 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 )J = 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 J {tex}\lambda=\frac{h c}{0.8 \times 10^{-18}}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{0.8 \times 10^{-18}}{/tex} = 24.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 m = 2486 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m = 2486 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} Hence the wavelength of light incident on the metal surface be 2486 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} .
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda_o=3315 \overset oA{/tex}
Kmax = 1.25 eV = 1.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
= 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J
Work function = {tex}\phi_o=\frac{h c}{\lambda_o}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{3315 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J
Using hv = {tex}\phi_o+K_{\max }{/tex}
{tex}\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\phi_o+K_{\max }{/tex}
= (0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 + 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18)J
= 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18J
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h c}{0.8 \times 10^{-18}}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{0.8 \times 10^{-18}}{/tex}
= 24.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 m
= 2486 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-10 m = 2486 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}
Hence the wavelength of light incident on the metal surface be  2486 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}.

question_html: A particle is associated with a de Broglie wave of wavelength 1.2 nm. The kinetic energy of the particle is made four times. Calculate the new value of the wavelength of the wave.
question_text: A particle is associated with a de Broglie wave of wavelength 1.2 nm. The kinetic energy of the particle is made four times. Calculate the new value of the wavelength of the wave.
explanation: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda^{\prime}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m} \times 4 \mathrm{~K}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1.2 \mathrm{~nm}}{2}{/tex} = 0.6 nm Hence the new value of the wavelength of the wave be 0.6 nm.
solution: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda^{\prime}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m} \times 4 \mathrm{~K}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1.2 \mathrm{~nm}}{2}{/tex} = 0.6 nm Hence the new value of the wavelength of the wave be 0.6 nm.
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda^{\prime}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m} \times 4 \mathrm{~K}}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1.2 \mathrm{~nm}}{2}{/tex}
= 0.6 nm
Hence the new value of the wavelength of the wave be 0.6 nm.

question_html:
1. The figure shows de Broglie wavelength ({tex}\lambda{/tex}) for two particles A and B having same charges but different masses as a function of {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt V}{/tex}, where V is the potential through which the particles are accelerated.
    1. Which of them is heavier and why?
    2. What does the slope of the line represent?
  2. Calculate the momentum of an electron having de Broglie wavelength of 3.0 {tex}\overset o {A}{/tex}.
question_text: The figure shows de Broglie wavelength ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) for two particles A and B having same charges but different masses as a function of {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt V}{/tex} , where V is the potential through which the particles are accelerated. Which of them is heavier and why? What does the slope of the line represent? Calculate the momentum of an electron having de Broglie wavelength of 3.0 {tex}\overset o {A}{/tex} .
explanation: B is heavier than A Reason- Slope {tex}\alpha \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}{/tex} Slope of A > slope of B; Hence m B > m A {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{V}}{/tex} So, slope = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q}}{/tex} Momentum, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{3 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} p = 2.21 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -24 kgm/s Hence the momentum of an electron having de Broglie wavelength of 3.0 {tex}\overset o {A}{/tex} .
solution: B is heavier than A Reason- Slope {tex}\alpha \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}{/tex} Slope of A > slope of B; Hence m B > m A {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{V}}{/tex} So, slope = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q}}{/tex} Momentum, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{3 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} p = 2.21 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -24 kgm/s Hence the momentum of an electron having de Broglie wavelength of 3.0 {tex}\overset o {A}{/tex} .
solution_html:
1. B is heavier than A
      Reason- Slope {tex}\alpha \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}{/tex}
      Slope of A > slope of B;
      Hence mB > mA
    2. {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex}
      {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{V}}{/tex}
      So, slope = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q}}{/tex}
  2. Momentum, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{3 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
    p = 2.21 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-24 kgm/s
    Hence the momentum of an electron having de Broglie wavelength of 3.0 {tex}\overset o {A}{/tex}.

question_text: Light of wavelength 3500 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} is incident on two metals A and B. Which of them will yield photoelectrons, if their work functions are 4.2 eV and 1.9 eV respectively? Make the necessary calculations to justify your answer.
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{E}=\frac{\lambda c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{3500 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} E = 3.55 eV Metal B Since energy of incident light is more than the work function of metal ‘B’.

{tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{3500 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
E = 3.55 eV
Metal B
Since energy of incident light is more than the work function of metal ‘B’.

question_html:
The figure shows the variation of stopping potential V0 with frequencies of incident monochromatic beam for a metal. Such a graph was first obtained by R.A. Millikan in 1916 for sodium.

Explain how, using Einstein's photoelectric equation and the graph, you can obtain the value of (i) Planck's constant, and (ii) work function of the metal, given the value of charge on electron.
question_text: The figure shows the variation of stopping potential V 0 with frequencies of incident monochromatic beam for a metal. Such a graph was first obtained by R.A. Millikan in 1916 for sodium. Explain how, using Einstein's photoelectric equation and the graph, you can obtain the value of (i) Planck's constant, and (ii) work function of the metal, given the value of charge on electron.
explanation: According to Einstein’s equation {tex}h v=\phi_o+e V_o{/tex} {tex}e V_o=h v-\phi_o{/tex} {tex}V_o=\frac{h}{e} v-\frac{\phi_o}{e}{/tex} This is equation of straight line as shown in figure, where slope is {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex} . So from the value of slope from the graph and value of charge on electron value of Planck’s constant can be calculated. Extrapolating the graph we get y - intercept {tex}\frac{\phi_o}{e}{/tex} . From this we can find work function given value of e.
solution: According to Einstein’s equation {tex}h v=\phi_o+e V_o{/tex} {tex}e V_o=h v-\phi_o{/tex} {tex}V_o=\frac{h}{e} v-\frac{\phi_o}{e}{/tex} This is equation of straight line as shown in figure, where slope is {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex} . So from the value of slope from the graph and value of charge on electron value of Planck’s constant can be calculated. Extrapolating the graph we get y - intercept {tex}\frac{\phi_o}{e}{/tex} . From this we can find work function given value of e.
solution_html:
According to Einstein’s equation
{tex}h v=\phi_o+e V_o{/tex}
{tex}e V_o=h v-\phi_o{/tex}
{tex}V_o=\frac{h}{e} v-\frac{\phi_o}{e}{/tex}
This is equation of straight line as shown in figure, where slope is {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex}.
So from the value of slope from the graph and value of charge on electron value of Planck’s constant can be calculated. Extrapolating the graph we get y - intercept {tex}\frac{\phi_o}{e}{/tex}. From this we can find work function given value of e.

question_html: The figure shows the variation of photoelectric current (I) versus applied voltage (V) for two photosensitive materials and for two different intensities of the incident radiation. Identify the pairs of curves that correspond to different materials for same intensity of incident radiation. Justify your answers in brief.
question_text: The figure shows the variation of photoelectric current (I) versus applied voltage (V) for two photosensitive materials and for two different intensities of the incident radiation. Identify the pairs of curves that correspond to different materials for same intensity of incident radiation. Justify your answers in brief.
explanation: Curves 1 and 2, 3 and 4 meet at the same point on the voltage axis (same cut off voltage) therefore they belong to same material. For same intensity and different materials curves 1 and 3, 2 and 4 because they have same saturation current and correspond to different materials for same intensity of incident radiation.
solution: Curves 1 and 2, 3 and 4 meet at the same point on the voltage axis (same cut off voltage) therefore they belong to same material. For same intensity and different materials curves 1 and 3, 2 and 4 because they have same saturation current and correspond to different materials for same intensity of incident radiation.
solution_html: Curves 1 and 2, 3 and 4 meet at the same point on the voltage axis (same cut off voltage) therefore they belong to same material. For same intensity and different materials curves 1 and 3, 2 and 4 because they have same saturation current and correspond to different materials for same intensity of incident radiation.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html: A beam of high energy photons, each of energy of E is incident on a metal surface and electrons are ejected from the surface. Assuming that the work function of the metal, {tex}\phi{/tex} is negligible, find the relation between the de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} associated with photoelectrons and the energy of photons E. Show in a figure, the variation of {tex}\lambda{/tex} as a function of E.
question_text: A beam of high energy photons, each of energy of E is incident on a metal surface and electrons are ejected from the surface. Assuming that the work function of the metal, {tex}\phi{/tex} is negligible, find the relation between the de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} associated with photoelectrons and the energy of photons E. Show in a figure, the variation of {tex}\lambda{/tex} as a function of E.
explanation: {tex}\mathrm{h} v=\phi_o+E_v^{\max }{/tex} As {tex}\phi_o{/tex} is negligible {tex}E_v^{\max}= hv{/tex} e = hv {tex}E=\frac{p^2}{2 m}{/tex} p = {tex}\sqrt {2mE}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}\mathrm{h} v=\phi_o+E_v^{\max }{/tex} As {tex}\phi_o{/tex} is negligible {tex}E_v^{\max}= hv{/tex} e = hv {tex}E=\frac{p^2}{2 m}{/tex} p = {tex}\sqrt {2mE}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\mathrm{h} v=\phi_o+E_v^{\max }{/tex}
As {tex}\phi_o{/tex} is negligible
{tex}E_v^{\max}= hv{/tex}
e = hv
{tex}E=\frac{p^2}{2 m}{/tex}
p = {tex}\sqrt {2mE}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}{/tex}

question_html: In photon picture of light, what determines the intensity of light?
question_text: In photon picture of light, what determines the intensity of light?
explanation: It is defined as the energy transmitting per unit area, per unit time. Alternatively: It is proportional to number of photons per unit area per unit time.
solution: It is defined as the energy transmitting per unit area, per unit time. Alternatively: It is proportional to number of photons per unit area per unit time.
solution_html:
It is defined as the energy transmitting per unit area, per unit time.
Alternatively: It is proportional to number of photons per unit area per unit time.

question_html: Lights of wavelengths 430 nm, 450 nm and 660 nm are incident on a metal surface whose threshold wavelength is 600 nm. In which case/cases will photoemission take place and why? Calculate the threshold frequency of the metal surface.
question_text: Lights of wavelengths 430 nm, 450 nm and 660 nm are incident on a metal surface whose threshold wavelength is 600 nm. In which case/cases will photoemission take place and why? Calculate the threshold frequency of the metal surface.
explanation: For photoelectric emission wavelength of radiation must be lesser than the threshold wavelength. Thus lights of wavelength 430 nm and 450 nm can cause photoelectric emission Threshold frequency ( {tex}\nu_o{/tex} ) {tex}\nu_o{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda_o}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^8}{600 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz
solution: For photoelectric emission wavelength of radiation must be lesser than the threshold wavelength. Thus lights of wavelength 430 nm and 450 nm can cause photoelectric emission Threshold frequency ( {tex}\nu_o{/tex} ) {tex}\nu_o{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda_o}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^8}{600 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz
solution_html:
For photoelectric emission wavelength of radiation must be lesser than the threshold wavelength.
Thus lights of wavelength 430 nm and 450 nm can cause photoelectric emission
Threshold frequency ({tex}\nu_o{/tex})
{tex}\nu_o{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda_o}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3 \times 10^8}{600 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz

question_html:
Photoelectrons are emitted from a metal surface when illuminated with UV light of wavelength 330 nm. The minimum amount of energy required to emit the electrons from the surface is 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J. Calculate :

  1. the energy of the incident radiation, and
  2. the kinetic energy of the photoelectron.
question_text: Photoelectrons are emitted from a metal surface when illuminated with UV light of wavelength 330 nm. The minimum amount of energy required to emit the electrons from the surface is 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J. Calculate : the energy of the incident radiation, and the kinetic energy of the photoelectron.
explanation: Energy of incident radiation E = hv = h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{330 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 6.027 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Kinetic energy of photoelectron K.E. = {tex}E-\phi_0{/tex} = (6.027 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 - 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 ) J = 2.527 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J
solution: Energy of incident radiation E = hv = h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{330 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 6.027 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Kinetic energy of photoelectron K.E. = {tex}E-\phi_0{/tex} = (6.027 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 - 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 ) J = 2.527 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J
solution_html:
1. Energy of incident radiation
    E = hv = h{tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{330 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex}
    = 6.027 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
  2. Kinetic energy of photoelectron
    K.E. = {tex}E-\phi_0{/tex}
    = (6.027 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 - 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19) J
    = 2.527 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
1. Monochromatic light is incident on a surface separating two media. The frequency of the light after refraction remains unaffected but its wavelength changes. Why?
  2. The frequency of an electromagnetic radiation is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1011 Hz. Identify the radiation and mention its two uses.
question_text: Monochromatic light is incident on a surface separating two media. The frequency of the light after refraction remains unaffected but its wavelength changes. Why? The frequency of an electromagnetic radiation is 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 11 Hz. Identify the radiation and mention its two uses.
explanation: Refraction arises through interaction of incident light with the atomic constituents of matter. Atoms may be viewed as oscillators which take up the frequency of the external agency causing forced oscillations. Thus the frequency of refracted light equals the frequency of incident light. Alternatively Frequency is the characteristic of the source of light. So it remains unaffected. But {tex}\lambda{/tex} depends on refractive index ( {tex}\mu{/tex} ) of the medium as- {tex}\lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_o}{\mu}{/tex} Infrared/ Microwaves/ Radio waves Uses of Infrared rays (any two) (i) Remote control (ii) Green house effect (iii) Photography in foggy condition (iv) To reveal secret writings (v) Infrared lamps Uses of Microwaves (any two) (i) Radar System (ii) Geostationary satellite (iii) Microwave ovens Uses of Radiowaves (any two) (i) TV transmission (ii) Radio broadcast (iii) Mobile transmission
solution: Refraction arises through interaction of incident light with the atomic constituents of matter. Atoms may be viewed as oscillators which take up the frequency of the external agency causing forced oscillations. Thus the frequency of refracted light equals the frequency of incident light. Alternatively Frequency is the characteristic of the source of light. So it remains unaffected. But {tex}\lambda{/tex} depends on refractive index ( {tex}\mu{/tex} ) of the medium as- {tex}\lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_o}{\mu}{/tex} Infrared/ Microwaves/ Radio waves Uses of Infrared rays (any two) (i) Remote control (ii) Green house effect (iii) Photography in foggy condition (iv) To reveal secret writings (v) Infrared lamps Uses of Microwaves (any two) (i) Radar System (ii) Geostationary satellite (iii) Microwave ovens Uses of Radiowaves (any two) (i) TV transmission (ii) Radio broadcast (iii) Mobile transmission
solution_html:
1. Refraction arises through interaction of incident light with the atomic constituents of matter. Atoms may be viewed as oscillators which take up the frequency of the external agency causing forced oscillations. Thus the frequency of refracted light equals the frequency of incident light.
    Alternatively
    Frequency is the characteristic of the source of light. So it remains unaffected. But {tex}\lambda{/tex} depends on refractive index ({tex}\mu{/tex}) of the medium as-
    {tex}\lambda_m{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\lambda_o}{\mu}{/tex}
  2. Infrared/ Microwaves/ Radio waves
    Uses of Infrared rays (any two)
    (i) Remote control
    (ii) Green house effect
    (iii) Photography in foggy condition
    (iv) To reveal secret writings
    (v) Infrared lamps
    Uses of Microwaves (any two)
    (i) Radar System
    (ii) Geostationary satellite
    (iii) Microwave ovens
    Uses of Radiowaves (any two)
    (i) TV transmission
    (ii) Radio broadcast
    (iii) Mobile transmission

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
Ultra-violet light of wavelength 200 nm from a source is incident on a metal surface. If the stopping potential is -2.5 V,

  1. Calculate the work function of the metal, and
  2. How would the surface respond to a high intensity red light of wavelength 6328 A produced by a laser?
question_text: Ultra-violet light of wavelength 200 nm from a source is incident on a metal surface. If the stopping potential is -2.5 V, Calculate the work function of the metal, and How would the surface respond to a high intensity red light of wavelength 6328 A produced by a laser?
explanation: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 200 nm, stopping potential = -2.5 V K.E. = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J E = hv =h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{200 \times 10^{-9}} {/tex} = 9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Work function = E - eV = (9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 )J = 5.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Wavelength of red light = 6328 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{\mathrm{~A}}{/tex} E = hv = h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6328 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 0.00314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 J = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Energy of red light < work function of metal surface {tex}\therefore{/tex} No emission of photoelectrons takes place.
solution: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 200 nm, stopping potential = -2.5 V K.E. = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J E = hv =h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{200 \times 10^{-9}} {/tex} = 9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Work function = E - eV = (9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 )J = 5.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Wavelength of red light = 6328 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{\mathrm{~A}}{/tex} E = hv = h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6328 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex} = 0.00314 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 J = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Energy of red light < work function of metal surface {tex}\therefore{/tex} No emission of photoelectrons takes place.
solution_html:
1. {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 200 nm, stopping potential = -2.5 V
    K.E. = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex}1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J = 4 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J
    E = hv =h{tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{200 \times 10^{-9}} {/tex}
    = 9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
    Work function = E - eV
    = (9.945 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 - 4 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19)J
    = 5.595 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
  2. Wavelength of red light = 6328 {tex}\stackrel{\circ}{\mathrm{~A}}{/tex}
    E = hv = h{tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6328 \times 10^{-10}}{/tex}
    = 0.00314 {tex}\times{/tex}10-16 J
    = 3.14 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J

Energy of red light < work function of metal surface
{tex}\therefore{/tex} No emission of photoelectrons takes place.

question_html:
The de-Broglie wavelength associated with an electron is 0.30 nm. Calculate:

  1. the speed, and
  2. the kinetic energy (in eV), of the electron.
question_text: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with an electron is 0.30 nm. Calculate: the speed, and the kinetic energy (in eV), of the electron.
explanation: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m} \lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.30 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 2.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m/s K.E. = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 {tex}\times{/tex} (2.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ) 2 J = 26.42 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J K.E. in eV = {tex}\frac{26.42 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{19}}{/tex} = 16.5 eV
solution: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m} \lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.30 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 2.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 m/s K.E. = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 {tex}\times{/tex} (2.41 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 ) 2 J = 26.42 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J K.E. in eV = {tex}\frac{26.42 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{19}}{/tex} = 16.5 eV
solution_html:
1. {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{mv}}{/tex}
    v = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m} \lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.30 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex}
    = 2.41 {tex}\times{/tex}106 m/s
  2. K.E. = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex}10-31 {tex}\times{/tex} (2.41 {tex}\times{/tex}106)2 J
    = 26.42 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
    K.E. in eV
    = {tex}\frac{26.42 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{19}}{/tex} = 16.5 eV

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html: Photoelectric emission occurs when a surface is irradiated with the radiation of frequency (i) {tex}\nu_1{/tex}, and (ii) {tex}\nu_2{/tex}. The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons emitted in the two cases are K and 2K respectively. Obtain the expression for the threshold frequency for the surface.
question_text: Photoelectric emission occurs when a surface is irradiated with the radiation of frequency (i) {tex}\nu_1{/tex} , and (ii) {tex}\nu_2{/tex} . The maximum kinetic energy of the electrons emitted in the two cases are K and 2K respectively. Obtain the expression for the threshold frequency for the surface.
explanation: K = hv 1 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} and 2K = hv 2 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(hv 1 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} ) = hv 2 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2hv 1 - 2 {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = hv 2 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2v 1 - v 2 ) = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = v 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v 0 = 2v 1 - v 2
solution: K = hv 1 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} and 2K = hv 2 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(hv 1 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} ) = hv 2 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2hv 1 - 2 {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = hv 2 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2v 1 - v 2 ) = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = v 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v 0 = 2v 1 - v 2
solution_html:
K = hv1 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} and 2K = hv2 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2(hv1 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex}) = hv2 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2hv1 - 2{tex}\phi_0{/tex} = hv2 - {tex}\phi_0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (2v1 - v2) = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = v0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} v0 = 2v1 - v2
