question_text: Calculate the frequency of a photon of energy 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J. Can this photon cause emission of an electron from the surface of Cs of work function 2.14 eV? If yes, what will be maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectron?
explanation: E = hv v = {tex}\frac{E}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.5 \times 10^{-19}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{/tex} = 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz Energy of photon in eV E = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 4.06 eV {tex}\because{/tex} E > {tex}\emptyset_0 {/tex} There will be photoelectric emission. K.E of photoelectron E k = E - {tex}\emptyset_0{/tex} = 4.06 - 2.14 = 1.92 eV
solution: E = hv v = {tex}\frac{E}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.5 \times 10^{-19}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{/tex} = 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz Energy of photon in eV E = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 4.06 eV {tex}\because{/tex} E > {tex}\emptyset_0 {/tex} There will be photoelectric emission. K.E of photoelectron E k = E - {tex}\emptyset_0{/tex} = 4.06 - 2.14 = 1.92 eV
solution_html:
1. E = hv
    v = {tex}\frac{E}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.5 \times 10^{-19}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{/tex}
    = 9.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz
  2. Energy of photon in eV
    E = 6.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J = 4.06 eV
    {tex}\because{/tex} E > {tex}\emptyset_0 {/tex} There will be photoelectric emission.
    K.E of photoelectron Ek = E - {tex}\emptyset_0{/tex}
    = 4.06 - 2.14
    = 1.92 eV

question_text: The work function of a metal is 2.31 eV. Photoelectric emission occurs when light of frequency 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz is incident on the metal surface. Calculate: the energy of the incident radiation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron and the stopping potential of the surface.
explanation: E = hv = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 = 4.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 2.65 eV K max = E - {tex}\emptyset_0{/tex} = 2.65 - 2.31 = 0.34 eV {tex}\mathrm{e} V_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = K max {tex}V_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = 0.34 V
solution: E = hv = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 = 4.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 2.65 eV K max = E - {tex}\emptyset_0{/tex} = 2.65 - 2.31 = 0.34 eV {tex}\mathrm{e} V_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = K max {tex}V_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = 0.34 V
solution_html:
1. E = hv
    = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex}10-34 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014
    = 4.24 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J
    = 2.65 eV
  2. Kmax = E - {tex}\emptyset_0{/tex}
    = 2.65 - 2.31
    = 0.34 eV
  3. {tex}\mathrm{e} V_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}= Kmax
    {tex}V_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}= 0.34 V

question_html: What are matter waves? A proton and an alpha particle are accelerated through the same potential difference. Find the ratio of the de Broglie wavelength associated with the proton to that with the alpha particle.
question_text: What are matter waves? A proton and an alpha particle are accelerated through the same potential difference. Find the ratio of the de Broglie wavelength associated with the proton to that with the alpha particle.
explanation: Wave associated with moving material particle is called matter wave {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 q m V}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{q_\alpha m_\alpha}{q_p m_p}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 e \cdot 4 m}{e \cdot m}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex}
solution: Wave associated with moving material particle is called matter wave {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 q m V}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{q_\alpha m_\alpha}{q_p m_p}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 e \cdot 4 m}{e \cdot m}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
Wave associated with moving material particle is called matter wave
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 q m V}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{q_\alpha m_\alpha}{q_p m_p}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_p}{\lambda_\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 e \cdot 4 m}{e \cdot m}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{1}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Use Einstein photoelectric equation to depict the variation of the maximum kinetic energy (Ek) of electrons emitted, with the frequency (v) of the incident radiation.
  2. A photosensitive surface is illuminated with a beam of (i) yellow light, and (ii) red light, both of the same intensity. In which case will
    1. photoelectrons have more Ek?
    2. more numbers of electrons be emitted?

Justify your answer in each case.
question_text: Use Einstein photoelectric equation to depict the variation of the maximum kinetic energy (E k ) of electrons emitted, with the frequency (v) of the incident radiation. A photosensitive surface is illuminated with a beam of (i) yellow light, and (ii) red light, both of the same intensity. In which case will photoelectrons have more E k ? more numbers of electrons be emitted? Justify your answer in each case.
explanation: E K = hv - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} as {tex}\quad v_{Y}{/tex} > {tex}\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} (E K ) y > (E K ) R For a given photosensitive surface, K.E. of photo-electrons will be more for yellow light. Since the number of photons incident on unit area per unit time for same intensity of two lights will be more in Red light (N R > N Y ) hence more number of photoelectrons will be ejected for Red light.
solution: E K = hv - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} as {tex}\quad v_{Y}{/tex} > {tex}\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{R}}{/tex} (E K ) y > (E K ) R For a given photosensitive surface, K.E. of photo-electrons will be more for yellow light. Since the number of photons incident on unit area per unit time for same intensity of two lights will be more in Red light (N R > N Y ) hence more number of photoelectrons will be ejected for Red light.
solution_html:
1. EK = hv - {tex}\phi_0{/tex}
      as {tex}\quad v_{Y}{/tex} > {tex}\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{R}}{/tex}
      (EK)y > (EK)R
      For a given photosensitive surface, K.E. of photo-electrons will be more for yellow light.
    2. Since the number of photons incident on unit area per unit time for same intensity of two lights will be more in Red light (NR > NY) hence more number of photoelectrons will be ejected for Red light.

question_html: A proton is accelerated through a potential difference V. After acceleration, the de Broglie wavelength associated with it is {tex}\lambda{/tex}. If the proton is replaced by an alpha particle, then find the de Broglie wavelength associated with it if it were accelerated through the same potential difference V. What will be the momentum of the alpha particle?
question_text: A proton is accelerated through a potential difference V. After acceleration, the de Broglie wavelength associated with it is {tex}\lambda{/tex} . If the proton is replaced by an alpha particle, then find the de Broglie wavelength associated with it if it were accelerated through the same potential difference V. What will be the momentum of the alpha particle?
explanation: {tex}\lambda_p{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 q_p m_p V}}{/tex} , {tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 2 q_p \cdot 4 m_p V}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p_p}{/tex} , {tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_\alpha}{p_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} , {tex}p_\alpha{/tex} = 2 {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} p p
solution: {tex}\lambda_p{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 q_p m_p V}}{/tex} , {tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 2 q_p \cdot 4 m_p V}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p_p}{/tex} , {tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\frac{p_\alpha}{p_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} , {tex}p_\alpha{/tex} = 2 {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} p p
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda_p{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 q_p m_p V}}{/tex},
{tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 \cdot 2 q_p \cdot 4 m_p V}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p_p}{/tex}, {tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{p_\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{p_\alpha}{p_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex},
{tex}p_\alpha{/tex} = 2{tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex} pp

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
1. Calculate the energy and momentum of a photon in a monochromatic beam of wavelength 331.5 nm.
  2. How fast should a hydrogen atom travel in order to have the same momentum as that of the photon in part (a)?
question_text: Calculate the energy and momentum of a photon in a monochromatic beam of wavelength 331.5 nm. How fast should a hydrogen atom travel in order to have the same momentum as that of the photon in part (a)?
explanation: Energy of photon, E = hv E = h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \cdot 63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{331 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} E = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J momentum (p) p = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \cdot 63 \times 10^{-34}}{331 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} p = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg ms -1 Momentum of H atom = momentum of the photon = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg ms -1 p = mu u = {tex}\frac{p}{m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 10^{-27}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}{/tex} u = 1.20 ms -1
solution: Energy of photon, E = hv E = h {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \cdot 63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{331 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} E = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J momentum (p) p = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \cdot 63 \times 10^{-34}}{331 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} p = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg ms -1 Momentum of H atom = momentum of the photon = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg ms -1 p = mu u = {tex}\frac{p}{m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 10^{-27}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}{/tex} u = 1.20 ms -1
solution_html:
1. Energy of photon, E = hv
    E = h{tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6 \cdot 63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{331 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex}
    E = 6 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J
    momentum (p)
    p = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{6 \cdot 63 \times 10^{-34}}{331 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex}
    p = 2 {tex}\times{/tex}10-27 kg ms-1
  2. Momentum of H atom = momentum of the photon = 2 {tex}\times{/tex}10-27 kg ms-1
    p = mu
    u = {tex}\frac{p}{m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \times 10^{-27}}{1.67 \times 10^{-27}} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}{/tex}
    u = 1.20 ms-1

question_html:
An alpha particle is accelerated through a potential difference of 100 V. Calculate:

  1. The speed acquired by the alpha particle, and
  2. The de-Broglie wavelength associated with it.
    (Take mass of alpha particle = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg)
question_text: An alpha particle is accelerated through a potential difference of 100 V. Calculate: The speed acquired by the alpha particle, and The de-Broglie wavelength associated with it. (Take mass of alpha particle = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg)
explanation: {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = qV {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = 2e {tex}\times{/tex} 100 or, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = 2e {tex}\times{/tex} 100 or, mv 2 = 400 eV or, v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{400 \mathrm{eV}}{m}}{/tex} or, v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{400 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{6.4 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v = 10 5 m/s de-Broglie wavelength = {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} Or, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-27} \times 2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 100}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 m
solution: {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = qV {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = 2e {tex}\times{/tex} 100 or, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = 2e {tex}\times{/tex} 100 or, mv 2 = 400 eV or, v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{400 \mathrm{eV}}{m}}{/tex} or, v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{400 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{6.4 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v = 10 5 m/s de-Broglie wavelength = {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} Or, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-27} \times 2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 100}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 m
solution_html:
1. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2 = qV
    {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2 = 2e {tex}\times{/tex}100
    or, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2 = 2e {tex}\times{/tex}100
    or, mv2 = 400 eV
    or, v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{400 \mathrm{eV}}{m}}{/tex}
    or, v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{400 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{6.4 \times 10^{-27}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} v = 105 m/s
  2. de-Broglie wavelength = {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex}
    Or, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-27} \times 2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 100}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 1.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 m

question_html:
1. Give an example each of a metal from which photoelectric emission takes place when irradiated by (i) UV light (ii) visible light.
  2. The work function of a metal is 4.50 eV. Find the frequency of light to be used to eject electrons from the metal surface with a maximum kinetic energy of 6.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J.
question_text: Give an example each of a metal from which photoelectric emission takes place when irradiated by (i) UV light (ii) visible light. The work function of a metal is 4.50 eV. Find the frequency of light to be used to eject electrons from the metal surface with a maximum kinetic energy of 6.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J.
explanation: UV light - Zinc, Magnesium, Cadmium Visible light - Alkali metals like Sodium, Potassium, Lithium (Any one example of each) From Einstein for equation of photo electric effect hv = W o + KE max = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 + 6.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 = (7.2 + 6.06) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 13.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J v = {tex}\frac{13.26 \times 10^{-19}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz
solution: UV light - Zinc, Magnesium, Cadmium Visible light - Alkali metals like Sodium, Potassium, Lithium (Any one example of each) From Einstein for equation of photo electric effect hv = W o + KE max = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 + 6.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 = (7.2 + 6.06) {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 13.26 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J v = {tex}\frac{13.26 \times 10^{-19}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz
solution_html:
1. UV light - Zinc, Magnesium, Cadmium
    2. Visible light - Alkali metals like Sodium, Potassium, Lithium
      (Any one example of each)
  2. From Einstein for equation of photo electric effect
    hv = Wo + KEmax
    = 4.5 {tex}\times{/tex}1.6 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 + 6.06 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
    = (7.2 + 6.06) {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
    = 13.26 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J
    v = {tex}\frac{13.26 \times 10^{-19}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{/tex}
    = 2 {tex}\times{/tex}1015 Hz

question_html: A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle are moving with the same speed. Which one of them has greater value of de Broglie wavelength associated with it? Justify your answer.
question_text: A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex} particle are moving with the same speed. Which one of them has greater value of de Broglie wavelength associated with it? Justify your answer.
explanation: Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h}{mv}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}=\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}{/tex} As, {tex}m\alpha{/tex} > m p {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda_p{/tex} > {tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex}
solution: Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h}{mv}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}=\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}{/tex} As, {tex}m\alpha{/tex} > m p {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda_p{/tex} > {tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex}
solution_html:
Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h}{mv}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}=\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}{/tex}
As, {tex}m\alpha{/tex} > mp {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\lambda_p{/tex} > {tex}\lambda_\alpha{/tex}

question_html: For the light of wavelength 400 nm incident on the cathode of a photocell, the stopping potential is 6 V. If the wavelength of incident light is increased to 600 nm, calculate the new stopping potential. (Take h = 4{tex}\cdot{/tex}14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 eV.s)
question_text: For the light of wavelength 400 nm incident on the cathode of a photocell, the stopping potential is 6 V. If the wavelength of incident light is increased to 600 nm, calculate the new stopping potential. (Take h = 4 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 14 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 eV.s)
explanation: As K max = {tex}h\nu{/tex} = W 0 or {tex}e V_{0}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-W_{0}{/tex} or {tex}V_{0}=\frac{h c}{e \lambda}-\frac{W_{0}}{e}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \Delta V_{0}=\left(V_{0}\right)_{2}-\left(V_{0}\right)_{1}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{h c}{e \lambda_{2}}-\frac{W_{0}}{e}\right]-\left[\frac{h c}{e \lambda_{1}}-\frac{W_{0}}{e}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{h c}{e}\left[\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}\left[\frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-7}}-\frac{1}{6 \times 10^{-7}}\right]{/tex} = 1.03 (V 0 ) 2 = (V 0 ) 1 - 1.03 = 6 - 1.03 = 4.97 V
solution: As K max = {tex}h\nu{/tex} = W 0 or {tex}e V_{0}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-W_{0}{/tex} or {tex}V_{0}=\frac{h c}{e \lambda}-\frac{W_{0}}{e}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \Delta V_{0}=\left(V_{0}\right)_{2}-\left(V_{0}\right)_{1}{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{h c}{e \lambda_{2}}-\frac{W_{0}}{e}\right]-\left[\frac{h c}{e \lambda_{1}}-\frac{W_{0}}{e}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{h c}{e}\left[\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}\left[\frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-7}}-\frac{1}{6 \times 10^{-7}}\right]{/tex} = 1.03 (V 0 ) 2 = (V 0 ) 1 - 1.03 = 6 - 1.03 = 4.97 V
solution_html:
As Kmax = {tex}h\nu{/tex} = W0
or {tex}e V_{0}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-W_{0}{/tex}
or {tex}V_{0}=\frac{h c}{e \lambda}-\frac{W_{0}}{e}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \Delta V_{0}=\left(V_{0}\right)_{2}-\left(V_{0}\right)_{1}{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\frac{h c}{e \lambda_{2}}-\frac{W_{0}}{e}\right]-\left[\frac{h c}{e \lambda_{1}}-\frac{W_{0}}{e}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{h c}{e}\left[\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}}-\frac{1}{\lambda_{1}}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}\left[\frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-7}}-\frac{1}{6 \times 10^{-7}}\right]{/tex}
= 1.03
(V0)2 = (V0)1 - 1.03 = 6 - 1.03 = 4.97 V

question_html: A proton and a deuteron are moving with the same speed. Find the ratio of the de Broglie wavelength {tex}(\frac {\lambda_p} {\lambda_d}){/tex} associated with them.
question_text: A proton and a deuteron are moving with the same speed. Find the ratio of the de Broglie wavelength {tex}(\frac {\lambda_p} {\lambda_d}){/tex} associated with them.
explanation: {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{d}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{m_p v_p}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{m_d v_d}{h}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 m_p}{m_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 21{/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{d}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{m_p v_p}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{m_d v_d}{h}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 m_p}{m_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 21{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}}{\lambda_{\mathrm{d}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{m_p v_p}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{m_d v_d}{h}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2 m_p}{m_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac 21{/tex}

Reason (R): An uncharged zinc plate becomes positively charged when it is irradiated by visible light.

He found that negatively charged particles were emitted from the zinc plate under the action of visible light. Reason (R): An uncharged zinc plate becomes positively charged when it is irradiated by visible light.

  2. Draw a graph showing variation of photocurrent with the anode potential of a photocell.
question_text: Write two main observations of photoelectric effect experiment which could only be explained by Einstein’s photoelectric equation. Draw a graph showing variation of photocurrent with the anode potential of a photocell.
explanation: The two main observation of the photoelectric effect experiment which could only be explained by Einstein's photoelectric equations are: The photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process For a given photosensitive material, there exists a certain minimum cutoff frequency below which no photoelectrons are emitted, howsoever high is the intensity of incident radiations. This frequency is called the threshold frequency. The reverse potential at which the photo-current stops (stopping potential) is independent of the intensity of light. Therefore, no matter how intense your source of light is, it can’t defeat the stopping voltage.
solution: The two main observation of the photoelectric effect experiment which could only be explained by Einstein's photoelectric equations are: The photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process For a given photosensitive material, there exists a certain minimum cutoff frequency below which no photoelectrons are emitted, howsoever high is the intensity of incident radiations. This frequency is called the threshold frequency. The reverse potential at which the photo-current stops (stopping potential) is independent of the intensity of light. Therefore, no matter how intense your source of light is, it can’t defeat the stopping voltage.
solution_html:
1. The two main observation of the photoelectric effect experiment which could only be explained by Einstein's photoelectric equations are:
    1. The photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process
    2. For a given photosensitive material, there exists a certain minimum cutoff frequency below which no photoelectrons are emitted, howsoever high is the intensity of incident radiations. This frequency is called the threshold frequency.
    3. The reverse potential at which the photo-current stops (stopping potential) is independent of the intensity of light. Therefore, no matter how intense your source of light is, it can’t defeat the stopping voltage.

question_html: What is the wavelength of a photon of energy 3.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10–19 J?
question_text: What is the wavelength of a photon of energy 3.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 –19 J?
explanation: Energy of photon, E = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{hc}{E}{/tex} Where, h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 j s, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 Given, E = 3.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J/atom By putting all these values, we get {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{\left(3.03 \times 10^{-19}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 656 nm
solution: Energy of photon, E = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{hc}{E}{/tex} Where, h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 j s, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 Given, E = 3.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J/atom By putting all these values, we get {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{\left(3.03 \times 10^{-19}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m = 656 nm
solution_html:
Energy of photon, E = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{hc}{E}{/tex} 
Where, h = 6.626 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 j s, c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1 
Given, E = 3.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J/atom
By putting all these values, we get
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{\left(3.03 \times 10^{-19}\right)}{/tex} 
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 6.56 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m = 656 nm

question_html: Define the terms threshold frequency and stopping potential. How were these terms incorporated in Einstein’s photoelectric equation?
question_text: Define the terms threshold frequency and stopping potential. How were these terms incorporated in Einstein’s photoelectric equation?
explanation: Threshold frequency {tex}\left(\nu_0\right){/tex} = The minimum frequency that's required for emission of electrons from a metal surface when electromagnetic radiation falls on the metal surface Stopping potential {tex}\left(V_0\right){/tex} It is the necessary potential applied externally to prevent the most energetic electron from reaching the endplate. Einstein's Photoelectric equation KE max = hv - hv 0 eV 0 = hv - hv 0
solution: Threshold frequency {tex}\left(\nu_0\right){/tex} = The minimum frequency that's required for emission of electrons from a metal surface when electromagnetic radiation falls on the metal surface Stopping potential {tex}\left(V_0\right){/tex} It is the necessary potential applied externally to prevent the most energetic electron from reaching the endplate. Einstein's Photoelectric equation KE max = hv - hv 0 eV 0 = hv - hv 0
solution_html:
Threshold frequency {tex}\left(\nu_0\right){/tex} = The minimum frequency that's required for emission of electrons from a metal surface when electromagnetic radiation falls on the metal surface
Stopping potential {tex}\left(V_0\right){/tex}
It is the necessary potential applied externally to prevent the most energetic electron from reaching the endplate.
Einstein's Photoelectric equation
KEmax = hv - hv0
eV0 = hv - hv0

question_html:
Light of same wavelength is incident on three photo-sensitive surfaces A, B and C. The following observations are recorded.

  1. From surface A, photo electrons are not emitted.
  2. From surface B, photo electrons are just emitted.
  3. From surface C, photo electrons with some kinetic energy are emitted.
    Compare the threshold frequencies of the three surfaces and justify your answer.
question_text: Light of same wavelength is incident on three photo-sensitive surfaces A, B and C. The following observations are recorded. From surface A, photo electrons are not emitted. From surface B, photo electrons are just emitted. From surface C, photo electrons with some kinetic energy are emitted. Compare the threshold frequencies of the three surfaces and justify your answer.
explanation: From surface A, electrons are not emitted. So, the value of threshold frequency is more than the frequency of the incident radiation. From surface B, photoelectrons are just emitted. So, the value of threshold frequency is equal to the frequency of the incident radiation. From surface C, photoelectrons are emitted with some kinetic energy. So, the value of threshold frequency isless than the frequency of the incident radiation. Threshold frequency is defined as the minimum frequency of light which causes electron emission from a metal surface. No electron emission means that the frequency of the light is less than the threshold frequency and electrons emission means that the frequency of the light is more than the threshold frequency.
solution: From surface A, electrons are not emitted. So, the value of threshold frequency is more than the frequency of the incident radiation. From surface B, photoelectrons are just emitted. So, the value of threshold frequency is equal to the frequency of the incident radiation. From surface C, photoelectrons are emitted with some kinetic energy. So, the value of threshold frequency isless than the frequency of the incident radiation. Threshold frequency is defined as the minimum frequency of light which causes electron emission from a metal surface. No electron emission means that the frequency of the light is less than the threshold frequency and electrons emission means that the frequency of the light is more than the threshold frequency.
solution_html:
1. From surface A, electrons are not emitted. So, the value of threshold frequency is more than the frequency of the incident radiation.
  2. From surface B, photoelectrons are just emitted. So, the value of threshold frequency is equal to the frequency of the incident radiation.
  3. From surface C, photoelectrons are emitted with some kinetic energy. So, the value of threshold frequency isless than the frequency of the incident radiation.
    Threshold frequency is defined as the minimum frequency of light which causes electron emission from a metal surface. No electron emission means that the frequency of the light is less than the threshold frequency and electrons emission means that the frequency of the light is more than the threshold frequency.

question_html:
If the frequency of light incident on the cathode of a photocell is increased, how will the following be affected? Justify your answer.

  1. Energy of the photo-electrons
  2. Photo current
question_text: If the frequency of light incident on the cathode of a photocell is increased, how will the following be affected? Justify your answer. Energy of the photo-electrons Photo current
explanation: If the frequency of light incident on the cathode of a photocell is increased, then Energy of the photoelectrons increases. From Einstein's photoelectric equation KE max = hv - hv 0 If v increases, hv 0 remaining same, the KE of electron increases. Rate of electron emission is not increased by increase in frequency. Hence, current remains same.
solution: If the frequency of light incident on the cathode of a photocell is increased, then Energy of the photoelectrons increases. From Einstein's photoelectric equation KE max = hv - hv 0 If v increases, hv 0 remaining same, the KE of electron increases. Rate of electron emission is not increased by increase in frequency. Hence, current remains same.
solution_html:
If the frequency of light incident on the cathode of a photocell is increased, then

  1. Energy of the photoelectrons increases. From Einstein's photoelectric equation KEmax = hv - hv0
    If v increases, hv0 remaining same, the KE of electron increases.
  2. Rate of electron emission is not increased by increase in frequency. Hence, current remains same.

question_html: The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted is doubled when the wavelength of light incident on the photosensitive surface changes from {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} to {tex}\lambda_2{/tex}. Deduce expressions for the threshold wavelength and work function for the metal surface in terms of {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_2{/tex}.
question_text: The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted is doubled when the wavelength of light incident on the photosensitive surface changes from {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} to {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} . Deduce expressions for the threshold wavelength and work function for the metal surface in terms of {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} and {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} .
explanation: Given that Initial kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by = K 1​ Final kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by K 2 = 2K 1 ​ Wavelength of light changes from {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} to {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} Let the threshold frequency is {tex}\nu_0{/tex} ​ and work function is {tex}\phi_0{/tex} Now, we know that:- {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + KE {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K 1 ...(i) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K 2 ...(ii) K 2 = 2K 1 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + 2K 1 ...(iii) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{2hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}2\phi_0{/tex} + 2K 1 (eq (i) {tex}\times{/tex} 2) {tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{2}}=\phi_{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{0}=\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} We know work function is given by {tex}\phi_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}= \mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}=\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{/tex}
solution: Given that Initial kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by = K 1​ Final kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by K 2 = 2K 1 ​ Wavelength of light changes from {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} to {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} Let the threshold frequency is {tex}\nu_0{/tex} ​ and work function is {tex}\phi_0{/tex} Now, we know that:- {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + KE {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K 1 ...(i) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K 2 ...(ii) K 2 = 2K 1 {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + 2K 1 ...(iii) {tex}\frac{\mathrm{2hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}2\phi_0{/tex} + 2K 1 (eq (i) {tex}\times{/tex} 2) {tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{2}}=\phi_{0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{0}=\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} We know work function is given by {tex}\phi_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}= \mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}=\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given that 
Initial kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by = K1​
Final kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by K2 = 2K1​
Wavelength of light changes from {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} to {tex}\lambda_2{/tex}
Let the threshold frequency is {tex}\nu_0{/tex}​ and work function is {tex}\phi_0{/tex}
Now, we know that:-
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + KE 
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K1 ...(i)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + K2 ...(ii)
K2 = 2K1 
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_2}{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + 2K1 ...(iii)
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{2hc}}{\lambda_1}{/tex} = {tex}2\phi_0{/tex} + 2K1 (eq (i) {tex}\times{/tex} 2)
{tex}\frac{2 \mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{1}}-\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{2}}=\phi_{0}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\phi_{0}=\mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} 
We know
work function is given by {tex}\phi_{0}=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}= \mathrm{hc}\left(\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}\right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_{0}}=\frac{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{/tex} 
{tex}\lambda_{0}=\frac{\lambda_{1} \lambda_{2}}{2 \lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}{/tex}

question_html:
Figure shows the stopping potential (V0) for the photo electron versus {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} graph, for two metals A and B, {tex}\lambda{/tex} being the wavelength of incident light.

  1. How is the value of Planck's constant determined from the graph?

question_text: Figure shows the stopping potential (V 0 ) for the photo electron versus {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} graph, for two metals A and B, {tex}\lambda{/tex} being the wavelength of incident light. How is the value of Planck's constant determined from the graph? If the distance between the light source and the surface of metal A is increased, how will the stopping potential for the electrons emitted from it be effected? Justify your answer.
explanation: Planck's constant can be determined from the gradient of the graph. Gradient = {tex}\frac{h c}{e}{/tex} h = Planck's constant c = Velocity of light in vacuum e = Charge of electron Gradient to be measured from graph. The values of c and e are known. Hence, h can be determined. If distance between source of light and surface of metal A is increased, the intensity of incident light decreases. But stopping potential does not depend on the intensity of the incident light. So, the stopping potential will not be affected.
solution: Planck's constant can be determined from the gradient of the graph. Gradient = {tex}\frac{h c}{e}{/tex} h = Planck's constant c = Velocity of light in vacuum e = Charge of electron Gradient to be measured from graph. The values of c and e are known. Hence, h can be determined. If distance between source of light and surface of metal A is increased, the intensity of incident light decreases. But stopping potential does not depend on the intensity of the incident light. So, the stopping potential will not be affected.
solution_html:
1. Planck's constant can be determined from the gradient of the graph.
    Gradient = {tex}\frac{h c}{e}{/tex}
    h = Planck's constant
    c = Velocity of light in vacuum
    e = Charge of electron
    Gradient to be measured from graph. The values of c and e are known. Hence, h can be determined.
  2. If distance between source of light and surface of metal A is increased, the intensity of incident light decreases. But stopping potential does not depend on the intensity of the incident light. So, the stopping potential will not be affected.

  1. The wave theory of light could not explain the existence of the threshold frequency.
  2. The photo electric current increases with increase of intensity of incident light.
question_text: In case of photo electric effect expeiment, explain the following facts, giving reasons. The wave theory of light could not explain the existence of the threshold frequency. The photo electric current increases with increase of intensity of incident light.
explanation: a) According to wave theory photoelectrons can be emitted using high intensity incident light of any frequency.Hence, there need not be any threshold frequency for any given photosensitive surface. b) Increase in intensity of light increases the number of incident photons. therefore, the photoelectric current increases.
solution: a) According to wave theory photoelectrons can be emitted using high intensity incident light of any frequency.Hence, there need not be any threshold frequency for any given photosensitive surface. b) Increase in intensity of light increases the number of incident photons. therefore, the photoelectric current increases.
solution_html:
a) According to wave theory photoelectrons can be emitted using high intensity incident light of any frequency.Hence, there need not be any threshold frequency for any given photosensitive surface.
b) Increase in intensity of light increases the number of incident photons. therefore, the photoelectric current increases.

question_html: Explain, how the process of emission of photoelectrons is different from the process of emission of {tex}\beta{/tex}-particles.
question_text: Explain, how the process of emission of photoelectrons is different from the process of emission of {tex}\beta{/tex} -particles.
explanation: Emission of photoelectron takes place due to the incident photons on the surface of metal whereas emission of {tex}\beta{/tex} particles takes place in radioactive nucleus spontaneously. Photoelectrons are not accompanied by emission of any other particle whereas {tex}\beta{/tex} particles accompanied by emission of followed by neutrino or antineutrino.hence, the process of emission of photoelectrons is different from the process of emission of {tex}\beta{/tex} -particles.

solution_html:
Emission of photoelectron takes place due to the incident photons on the surface of metal whereas emission of {tex}\beta{/tex} particles takes place in radioactive nucleus spontaneously.
Photoelectrons are not accompanied by emission of any other particle whereas {tex}\beta{/tex} particles accompanied by emission of followed by neutrino or antineutrino.hence, the process of emission of photoelectrons is different from the process of emission of {tex}\beta{/tex}-particles.

question_text: A photo-cell connected in an electrical circuit is placed at a distance d from a source of light. As a result current I flows in the circuit. What will be the current in the circuit when the distance is increased to 2d ?

options.explanation: The number of electrons emitted per second is inversely proportional to distance square. So when the distance becomes 2d the current becomes {tex}\frac{I}{4}{/tex} .

explanation: The number of electrons emitted per second is inversely proportional to distance square. So when the distance becomes 2d the current becomes {tex}\frac{I}{4}{/tex} .

question_html: Photons of frequencV are incident on the surface of two metals A and B of threshold frequencies 3{tex}\frac{v}{4}{/tex} and 2{tex}\frac{v}{3}{/tex}, respectively. Find the ratio of maximum kinetic energy of electrons emitted from A to that from B.
question_text: Photons of frequencV are incident on the surface of two metals A and B of threshold frequencies 3 {tex}\frac{v}{4}{/tex} and 2 {tex}\frac{v}{3}{/tex} , respectively. Find the ratio of maximum kinetic energy of electrons emitted from A to that from B.
explanation: By law of photoelectric emission, hv = hv 0 + (KE) max So, hv = {tex}\frac{h v}{2}{/tex} + (KE) max ...(1) hv = {tex}\frac{h v}{3}{/tex} + (KE) max ' ...(2) (KE) max = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h} v}{2}{/tex} = K A (KE) max ' = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} hv = K B So, {tex}\frac{K_A}{K_B}=\frac{\left(\frac{h v}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{3} h v\right)}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
solution: By law of photoelectric emission, hv = hv 0 + (KE) max So, hv = {tex}\frac{h v}{2}{/tex} + (KE) max ...(1) hv = {tex}\frac{h v}{3}{/tex} + (KE) max ' ...(2) (KE) max = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h} v}{2}{/tex} = K A (KE) max ' = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex} hv = K B So, {tex}\frac{K_A}{K_B}=\frac{\left(\frac{h v}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{3} h v\right)}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
By law of photoelectric emission,
hv = hv0 + (KE)max
So, hv = {tex}\frac{h v}{2}{/tex} + (KE)max ...(1)
hv = {tex}\frac{h v}{3}{/tex} + (KE)max' ...(2)
(KE)max = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h} v}{2}{/tex} = KA
(KE)max' = {tex}\frac{2}{3}{/tex}hv = KB
So, {tex}\frac{K_A}{K_B}=\frac{\left(\frac{h v}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{2}{3} h v\right)}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}

question_html:
1. In the photoelectric effect, do all the electrons that absorb a photon comes out as photoelectrons irrespective of their location? Explain.
  2. A source of light, of frequency greater than the threshold frequency, is placed at a distance d from the cathode of a photocell. The stopping potential is found to be V. If the distance of the light source is reduced to d/n (where n>1), explains the changes that are likely to be observed in the
    1. photoelectric current and
    2. stopping potential.
question_text: In the photoelectric effect, do all the electrons that absorb a photon comes out as photoelectrons irrespective of their location? Explain. A source of light, of frequency greater than the threshold frequency, is placed at a distance d from the cathode of a photocell. The stopping potential is found to be V. If the distance of the light source is reduced to d/n (where n>1), explains the changes that are likely to be observed in the photoelectric current and stopping potential.
explanation: No, it is not necessary that if the energy supplied to an electron is more than the work function, it will come out. A photo electron is produced only when the electron absorbs a single photon with energy greater than or equal to the work function. The electron after receiving energy may lose energy to the metal due to collisions with the atoms of the metal. Therefore, most electrons get scattered into the metal. Only a few electrons near the surface may come out of the surface of the metal for whom the incident energy is greater than the work function of the metal. On reducing the distance, intensity increases. Photoelectric current increases with an increase in intensity. Stopping potential is independent of intensity and therefore remains unchanged.
solution: No, it is not necessary that if the energy supplied to an electron is more than the work function, it will come out. A photo electron is produced only when the electron absorbs a single photon with energy greater than or equal to the work function. The electron after receiving energy may lose energy to the metal due to collisions with the atoms of the metal. Therefore, most electrons get scattered into the metal. Only a few electrons near the surface may come out of the surface of the metal for whom the incident energy is greater than the work function of the metal. On reducing the distance, intensity increases. Photoelectric current increases with an increase in intensity. Stopping potential is independent of intensity and therefore remains unchanged.
solution_html:
1. No, it is not necessary that if the energy supplied to an electron is more than the work function, it will come out. A photo electron is produced only when the electron absorbs a single photon with energy greater than or equal to the work function. The electron after receiving energy may lose energy to the metal due to collisions with the atoms of the metal. Therefore, most electrons get scattered into the metal. Only a few electrons near the surface may come out of the surface of the metal for whom the incident energy is greater than the work function of the metal.
  2. On reducing the distance, intensity increases. Photoelectric current increases with an increase in intensity. Stopping potential is independent of intensity and therefore remains unchanged.

question_text: A photon and a proton have the same de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Prove that the energy of the photon is ( {tex}\frac{2m \lambda c}{h}{/tex} ) times the kinetic energy of the proton.
explanation: Energy of photon E p = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex} For proton {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{mv}{/tex} mv = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} Kinetic energy of proton E k = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 E k = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} . {tex}\frac{h^2}{m \lambda^2}{/tex} Therefore, E p = ( {tex}\frac{2m \lambda c}{h}{/tex} )E k
solution: Energy of photon E p = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex} For proton {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{mv}{/tex} mv = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} Kinetic energy of proton E k = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 E k = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} . {tex}\frac{h^2}{m \lambda^2}{/tex} Therefore, E p = ( {tex}\frac{2m \lambda c}{h}{/tex} )E k
solution_html:
Energy of photon Ep = {tex}\frac{hc}{\lambda}{/tex}
For proton {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{mv}{/tex}
mv = {tex}\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
Kinetic energy of proton Ek = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2 
Ek = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}.{tex}\frac{h^2}{m \lambda^2}{/tex}
Therefore, Ep = ({tex}\frac{2m \lambda c}{h}{/tex})Ek

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html: Why is wave theory of electromagnetic radiation not able to explain photo electric effect? How does photon picture resolve this problem?
question_text: Why is wave theory of electromagnetic radiation not able to explain photo electric effect? How does photon picture resolve this problem?
explanation: Wave theory of light cannot explain the photoelectric effect because of the following reasons: Wave nature does not explain the instantaneous ejection of photoelectrons. It does not explain the threshold frequency for a metal surface Wave nature does not explain why kinetic energy of the emitted electrons is independent of the intensity of light and depends upon its frequency. In the photon picture, each electron absorbs one photon. If the frequency is high enough, then the energy of the photon (E = h {tex}\nu{/tex} ) is enough to eject the electron. By conservation of energy, h {tex}\nu{/tex} = KE max + W o where W 0 is the minimum energy required to free the electron. We can see that as the frequency increases, the maximum kinetic energy increases, in agreement with experimental results. Also, as intensity increases, the number of photons per unit area increase and so does the emitted photoelectrons; this results in increase in photocurrent. Thus, the wave theory of light fails to explain the photoelectric effect whereas the photon picture of light is able to explain this effect.
solution: Wave theory of light cannot explain the photoelectric effect because of the following reasons: Wave nature does not explain the instantaneous ejection of photoelectrons. It does not explain the threshold frequency for a metal surface Wave nature does not explain why kinetic energy of the emitted electrons is independent of the intensity of light and depends upon its frequency. In the photon picture, each electron absorbs one photon. If the frequency is high enough, then the energy of the photon (E = h {tex}\nu{/tex} ) is enough to eject the electron. By conservation of energy, h {tex}\nu{/tex} = KE max + W o where W 0 is the minimum energy required to free the electron. We can see that as the frequency increases, the maximum kinetic energy increases, in agreement with experimental results. Also, as intensity increases, the number of photons per unit area increase and so does the emitted photoelectrons; this results in increase in photocurrent. Thus, the wave theory of light fails to explain the photoelectric effect whereas the photon picture of light is able to explain this effect.
solution_html:
Wave theory of light cannot explain the photoelectric effect because of the following reasons:

  1. Wave nature does not explain the instantaneous ejection of photoelectrons.
  2. It does not explain the threshold frequency for a metal surface
  3. Wave nature does not explain why kinetic energy of the emitted electrons is independent of the intensity of light and depends upon its frequency.

In the photon picture, each electron absorbs one photon. If the frequency is high enough, then the energy of the photon (E = h{tex}\nu{/tex}) is enough to eject the electron. By conservation of energy,
h{tex}\nu{/tex} = KEmax + Wo
where W0 is the minimum energy required to free the electron. We can see that as the frequency increases, the maximum kinetic energy increases, in agreement with experimental results. Also, as intensity increases, the number of photons per unit area increase and so does the emitted photoelectrons; this results in increase in photocurrent.
Thus, the wave theory of light fails to explain the photoelectric effect whereas the photon picture of light is able to explain this effect.

question_html: Obtain the expression for the ratio of the de Broglie wavelengths associated with the electron orbiting in the second and third excited states of hydrogen atom.
question_text: Obtain the expression for the ratio of the de Broglie wavelengths associated with the electron orbiting in the second and third excited states of hydrogen atom.
explanation: 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} For second excited state (n = 3) r = 0.529(n) 2 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} = 0.529(3) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} (0.529)(3) 2 = 3 {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} For third excited state, n = 4 r = 0.529(4) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} (0.529)(4) 2 = 4 {tex}\lambda_3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 \lambda_2}{4 \lambda_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(3)^2}{(4)^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_3}{/tex} = 3 : 4
solution: 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r = n {tex}\lambda{/tex} For second excited state (n = 3) r = 0.529(n) 2 {tex}\overset oA{/tex} = 0.529(3) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} (0.529)(3) 2 = 3 {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} For third excited state, n = 4 r = 0.529(4) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} (0.529)(4) 2 = 4 {tex}\lambda_3{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 \lambda_2}{4 \lambda_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(3)^2}{(4)^2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_3}{/tex} = 3 : 4
solution_html:
2{tex}\pi{/tex}r = n{tex}\lambda{/tex}
For second excited state (n = 3)
r = 0.529(n)2 {tex}\overset oA{/tex}
= 0.529(3)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}(0.529)(3)2 = 3{tex}\lambda_2{/tex}
For third excited state, n = 4
r = 0.529(4)2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}(0.529)(4)2 = 4{tex}\lambda_3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{3 \lambda_2}{4 \lambda_3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{(3)^2}{(4)^2}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_3}{/tex} = 3 : 4

explanation: Purpose of Davisson Germer Experiment was to verify the wave nature of electron. It confirms the de Broglie relations for matter waves / Diffraction effect of electron beams from crystal de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda =\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mqV}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_p \mathrm{eV}_{\mathrm{p}}}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_\alpha \mathrm{eV}_\alpha}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{V} \alpha}=\frac{8}{1}{/tex}
solution: Purpose of Davisson Germer Experiment was to verify the wave nature of electron. It confirms the de Broglie relations for matter waves / Diffraction effect of electron beams from crystal de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda =\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mqV}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_p \mathrm{eV}_{\mathrm{p}}}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_\alpha \mathrm{eV}_\alpha}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{V} \alpha}=\frac{8}{1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Purpose of Davisson Germer Experiment was to verify the wave nature of electron. It confirms the de Broglie relations for matter waves / Diffraction effect of electron beams from crystal
  2. de Broglie wavelength
    {tex}\lambda =\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{mqV}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_p \mathrm{eV}_{\mathrm{p}}}}=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\sqrt{2 \mathrm{~m}_\alpha \mathrm{eV}_\alpha}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{V} \alpha}=\frac{8}{1}{/tex}

question_html:
When a given photosensitive material is irradiated with light of frequency v, the maximum speed of the emitted photoelectrons equals Vmax. The graph shown in the figure gives a plot of {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\max }^2{/tex} varying with frequency v.

Obtain an expression for

  1. Planck’s constant, and
  2. The work function of the given photosensitive material in terms of the parameters l, n and the mass m of the electron.
  3. How is threshold frequency determined from the plot?
question_text: When a given photosensitive material is irradiated with light of frequency v, the maximum speed of the emitted photoelectrons equals V max . The graph shown in the figure gives a plot of {tex}\mathrm{V}_{\max }^2{/tex} varying with frequency v. Obtain an expression for Planck’s constant, and The work function of the given photosensitive material in terms of the parameters l , n and the mass m of the electron. How is threshold frequency determined from the plot?
explanation: Einstein, Photoelectric equation {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\max }^2=\mathrm{h} v-\phi_0{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }=\left(\frac{2 h}{m}\right) v-\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}{/tex} Slope of the graph is {tex}\frac{2 h}{m}=\frac{l}{n}{/tex} Planck constant {tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{l m}{2 n}{/tex} Intercept on {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }{/tex} , axis, {tex}\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}=l{/tex} Work function {tex}\phi_0=\frac{m l}{2}{/tex} Threshold frequency is the intercept on {tex}\nu{/tex} axis i.e. v 0 = n
solution: Einstein, Photoelectric equation {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\max }^2=\mathrm{h} v-\phi_0{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }=\left(\frac{2 h}{m}\right) v-\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}{/tex} Slope of the graph is {tex}\frac{2 h}{m}=\frac{l}{n}{/tex} Planck constant {tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{l m}{2 n}{/tex} Intercept on {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }{/tex} , axis, {tex}\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}=l{/tex} Work function {tex}\phi_0=\frac{m l}{2}{/tex} Threshold frequency is the intercept on {tex}\nu{/tex} axis i.e. v 0 = n
solution_html:
1. Einstein, Photoelectric equation
    {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}_{\max }^2=\mathrm{h} v-\phi_0{/tex}
    {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }=\left(\frac{2 h}{m}\right) v-\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}{/tex}
    Slope of the graph is {tex}\frac{2 h}{m}=\frac{l}{n}{/tex}
    Planck constant {tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{l m}{2 n}{/tex}
  2. Intercept on {tex}\mathrm{V}^2{ }_{\max }{/tex}, axis, {tex}\frac{2 \phi_0}{m}=l{/tex}
    Work function {tex}\phi_0=\frac{m l}{2}{/tex}
  3. Threshold frequency is the intercept on {tex}\nu{/tex} axis i.e. v0 = n

explanation: Threshold frequency equals the minimum frequency of incident radiation (light) that can cause photoemission from a given photosensitive surface. Stopping potential: The minimum negative (retarding) potential, given to the anode (collector plate) for which the photocurrent stops or becomes zero.

question_html:
When light of frequency v1 is incident on a photosensitive surface, the stopping potential is V1. If the frequency of incident radiation becomes {tex}\frac{v_1}{2}{/tex}, the stopping potential changes to V2. Find out the expression for the threshold frequency for the surface in terms of V1 and V2.
If the frequency of incident radiation is doubled, will the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons also be doubled? Give reason.
question_text: When light of frequency v 1 is incident on a photosensitive surface, the stopping potential is V 1 . If the frequency of incident radiation becomes {tex}\frac{v_1}{2}{/tex} , the stopping potential changes to V 2 . Find out the expression for the threshold frequency for the surface in terms of V 1 and V 2 . If the frequency of incident radiation is doubled, will the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons also be doubled? Give reason.
explanation: By Einstein’s photoelectric equation. eV 1 = hv 1 - hv 0 and {tex}e V_2=\frac{h v_1}{2}-h v_0{/tex} 2eV 2 = hv 1 - 2hv 0 On solving the above equations {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e(V 1 - 2V 2 ) = hv 0 {tex}\Rightarrow V_0=\frac{e}{h}\left(V_1-2 V_2\right){/tex} If the frequency is doubled, maximum kinetic energy will not be doubled. K max = hv - hv 0 {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\text {max }}^1=2 h v-h v_0{/tex} = hv 0 + 2K max
solution: By Einstein’s photoelectric equation. eV 1 = hv 1 - hv 0 and {tex}e V_2=\frac{h v_1}{2}-h v_0{/tex} 2eV 2 = hv 1 - 2hv 0 On solving the above equations {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e(V 1 - 2V 2 ) = hv 0 {tex}\Rightarrow V_0=\frac{e}{h}\left(V_1-2 V_2\right){/tex} If the frequency is doubled, maximum kinetic energy will not be doubled. K max = hv - hv 0 {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\text {max }}^1=2 h v-h v_0{/tex} = hv 0 + 2K max
solution_html:
By Einstein’s photoelectric equation.
eV1 = hv1 - hv0
and
{tex}e V_2=\frac{h v_1}{2}-h v_0{/tex}
2eV2 = hv1 - 2hv0
On solving the above equations
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} e(V1 - 2V2) = hv0
{tex}\Rightarrow V_0=\frac{e}{h}\left(V_1-2 V_2\right){/tex}
If the frequency is doubled, maximum kinetic energy will not be doubled.
Kmax = hv - hv0
{tex}\mathrm{K}_{\text {max }}^1=2 h v-h v_0{/tex}
= hv0 + 2Kmax

question_html:
1. How does one explain the emission of electrons from a photosensitive surface with the help of Einstein’s photoelectric equation?
  2. Work function of aluminium is 4.2 eV. If two photons each of energy 2.5 eV are incident on its surface, will the emission of electrons take place? Justify your answer.
  3. The stopping potential in an experiment on photoelectric effect is 1.5 V. What is the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted? Calculate in Joules.
question_text: How does one explain the emission of electrons from a photosensitive surface with the help of Einstein’s photoelectric equation? Work function of aluminium is 4.2 eV. If two photons each of energy 2.5 eV are incident on its surface, will the emission of electrons take place? Justify your answer. The stopping potential in an experiment on photoelectric effect is 1.5 V. What is the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted? Calculate in Joules.
explanation: When a photon of the energy hv is absorbed by an electron in the photosensitive material, a part of the energy absorbed is used up in liberating it from the surface (the work function). The remaining energy appears as KE of the photoelectron. Emission of electron will not take place. Energy hv, of a single photon, is less than the work function {tex}\phi_{\circ}{/tex} Vo = 1.5 V k max = eV o = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J
solution: When a photon of the energy hv is absorbed by an electron in the photosensitive material, a part of the energy absorbed is used up in liberating it from the surface (the work function). The remaining energy appears as KE of the photoelectron. Emission of electron will not take place. Energy hv, of a single photon, is less than the work function {tex}\phi_{\circ}{/tex} Vo = 1.5 V k max = eV o = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J
solution_html:
1. When a photon of the energy hv is absorbed by an electron in the photosensitive material, a part of the energy absorbed is used up in liberating it from the surface (the work function). The remaining energy appears as KE of the photoelectron.
  2. Emission of electron will not take place.
    Energy hv, of a single photon, is less than the work function {tex}\phi_{\circ}{/tex}
  3. Vo = 1.5 V
    kmax = eVo = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J

question_html: Explain with the help of Einstein’s photo electric equation any two observed features in photoelectric effect which cannot be explained by wave theory.
question_text: Explain with the help of Einstein’s photo electric equation any two observed features in photoelectric effect which cannot be explained by wave theory.
solution_html:
k.Emax = h{tex}\nu{/tex}-w0/e{tex}\nu{/tex}0 = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - h{tex}\nu{/tex}0
Two features:

  1. Maximum energy is independent of intensity
  2. Maximum energy is directly proportional to frequency

Einstein's photoelectric equation is given by
{tex}h \nu=\phi+\frac{1}{2} m v^2{/tex}
The above equation implies that the kinetic energy of emitted electrons is independent of the intensity of light and a minimum threshold frequency is required for an electron to come out of the metal. Both of these are not explained by wave theory.

question_html:
Hydrogen atom in third excited state de-excites to the first excited state. Obtain the expressions for the frequency of radiation emitted in this process.
Also determine the ratio of the wavelengths of the emitted radiations when the atom de-excites from the third excited state to the second excited state and from the third excited state to the first excited state.
question_text: Hydrogen atom in third excited state de-excites to the first excited state. Obtain the expressions for the frequency of radiation emitted in this process. Also determine the ratio of the wavelengths of the emitted radiations when the atom de-excites from the third excited state to the second excited state and from the third excited state to the first excited state.
explanation: When de exited from third to first the frequency is {tex}\nu_{42}=R C\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=\frac{3}{16} R C{/tex} the wavelengths of the emitted radiations when the atom de-excites from the third excited state to the second excited state is {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{3^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=\frac{7}{144} R C{/tex} Wavelength from the third excited state to the first excited state {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=\frac{3}{16} R{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{22}{7}{/tex}
solution: When de exited from third to first the frequency is {tex}\nu_{42}=R C\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=\frac{3}{16} R C{/tex} the wavelengths of the emitted radiations when the atom de-excites from the third excited state to the second excited state is {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{3^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=\frac{7}{144} R C{/tex} Wavelength from the third excited state to the first excited state {tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=\frac{3}{16} R{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{22}{7}{/tex}
solution_html:
When de exited from third to first the frequency is

{tex}\nu_{42}=R C\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=\frac{3}{16} R C{/tex}
           
the wavelengths of the emitted radiations when the atom de-excites from the third excited state to the second excited state is 

{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_1}=R\left(\frac{1}{3^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=\frac{7}{144} R C{/tex}

Wavelength from the third excited state to the first excited state

{tex}\frac{1}{\lambda_2}=R\left(\frac{1}{2^2}-\frac{1}{4^2}\right)=\frac{3}{16} R{/tex}

{tex}\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\frac{22}{7}{/tex}

{tex}\text { But } r_n= n^2 r_0 {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \pi n^2 r_0= n \lambda_n {/tex}
and {tex} \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_3}= \frac{1}{3}{/tex}

question_text: If light of wavelength 412·5 nm is incident on each of the metals given below, which ones will show photoelectric emission and why? Metal Work Function Na 1.92 K 2.15 Ca 3.20 Mo 4.17
explanation: The energy of a photon of incident radiation is given by {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{\left(412.5 \times 10^{-9}\right) \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)} e V{/tex} {tex}\cong 3.01 e V{/tex} Hence only Na and K will show photoelectric emission.
solution: The energy of a photon of incident radiation is given by {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\therefore E=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{\left(412.5 \times 10^{-9}\right) \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)} e V{/tex} {tex}\cong 3.01 e V{/tex} Hence only Na and K will show photoelectric emission.
solution_html:
The energy of a photon of incident radiation is given by
{tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore E=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{\left(412.5 \times 10^{-9}\right) \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)} e V{/tex}
{tex}\cong 3.01 e V{/tex}
Hence only Na and K will show photoelectric emission.

question_html:
State Einstein's photoelectric equation explaining the symbols used.

Light of frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} is incident on a photosensitive surface. A graph of the square of the maximum speed of the electrons (V2max) vs. {tex}\nu{/tex} is obtained as shown in the figure. Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, obtain expressions for 

  1. Planck's constant,
  2. Work function of the given photosensitive material in terms of parameters l, n, and mass of the electron m.
question_text: State Einstein's photoelectric equation explaining the symbols used. Light of frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} is incident on a photosensitive surface. A graph of the square of the maximum speed of the electrons (V 2 max) vs. {tex}\nu{/tex} is obtained as shown in the figure. Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, obtain expressions for Planck's constant, Work function of the given photosensitive material in terms of parameters l, n, and mass of the electron m.
solution_html:
Einstein’s photoelectric equation is given by;-
h{tex}\nu{/tex} = W + {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} mv2max
{tex}\nu{/tex} = frequency of incident light
{tex}\nu_0{/tex}threshold frequency of photo sensitive material
W = work function of material 
{tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} mv2max = max. kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons
From Einstein’s photoelectric equation, we have
h{tex}\nu{/tex} = W +  {tex}\frac{1}{2} {/tex} mv2max 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v2max = {tex}\frac{2}{m}{/tex} (h{tex}\nu{/tex} - W)
{tex}=\left(\frac{2 h}{m}\right) v+\left(\frac{-2 W}{m}\right){/tex}
Slope of the given graph = {tex}\frac{l}{n}{/tex}
intercept on the y-axis = - l
{tex}\therefore \frac{2 h}{m}=\frac{l}{n}{/tex} or h = {tex}\frac{m l}{2 n}{/tex}
and -l = {tex}\frac{-2 W}{m}{/tex} or W = {tex}\frac{m l}{2}{/tex}

question_html: Find the frequency of light which ejects electrons from a metal surface, fully stopped by a retarding potential of 3.3 V. If photoelectric emission begins in this metal at a frequency of 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz, calculate the work function (in eV) for this metal.
question_text: Find the frequency of light which ejects electrons from a metal surface, fully stopped by a retarding potential of 3.3 V. If photoelectric emission begins in this metal at a frequency of 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz, calculate the work function (in eV) for this metal.
explanation: Finding the work function Finding the frequency of incident light We have W = hv 0 = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 J = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-20} \times 8}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 3.315 eV We have hv = W + eV = (3.315 + 3.3) eV\ v = {tex}\frac{6.615 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{6.63 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} Hz = 1.596 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz
solution: Finding the work function Finding the frequency of incident light We have W = hv 0 = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 J = {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-20} \times 8}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 3.315 eV We have hv = W + eV = (3.315 + 3.3) eV\ v = {tex}\frac{6.615 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{6.63 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} Hz = 1.596 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz
solution_html:
Finding the work function
Finding the frequency of incident light
We have
W = hv0
= 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex}10-34 {tex}\times{/tex} 8 {tex}\times{/tex}1014J
= {tex}\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-20} \times 8}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
= 3.315 eV
We have
hv = W + eV
= (3.315 + 3.3) eV\
v = {tex}\frac{6.615 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{6.63 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} Hz
= 1.596 {tex}\times{/tex}1015 Hz

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
1. What is photoelectric effect?
  2. Using the photon picture of light, show how Einstein's photoelectric equation can be established.
  3. What is the photo-electric effect? Write Einstein's photoelectric equation. Explain how it enables us to understand the
    1. linear dependence, of the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons, on the frequency of the incident radiation.
    2. existence of a threshold frequency for a given photoemitter.
    3. independence of the maximum energy of emitted photo-electrons from the intensity of incident light.
question_text: What is photoelectric effect? Using the photon picture of light, show how Einstein's photoelectric equation can be established. What is the photo-electric effect? Write Einstein's photoelectric equation. Explain how it enables us to understand the linear dependence, of the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electrons, on the frequency of the incident radiation. existence of a threshold frequency for a given photoemitter. independence of the maximum energy of emitted photo-electrons from the intensity of incident light.
solution_html:
1. Photo-electric effect is the phenomenon of emission of electrons from the surface of metals, when light radiations of suitable frequency fall on them.
  2. When a radiation of frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} is incident on a metal surface, it is absorbed in the form of discrete photons each of energy {tex}h\nu{/tex}. Photoelectric emission occurs because of single collision of a photon with a free electron.
    The energy of the photon is used to
    1. free the electron from the metal surface. It is equal to the work function W0 of the metal.
    2. provide kinetic energy to the emitted Electron.
      {tex}\therefore \quad h v=K_{\max }+W_{0}{/tex}
      or {tex}K_{\max }=\frac{1}{2} m v_{\max }^{2}=h v-W_{0}{/tex}
      This is Einstein's photoelectric equation.
    1. Clearly, above the threshold frequency {tex}\nu_0{/tex}, Kmax {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\nu{/tex} i.e., the maximum K.E. of the emitted electrons depends linearly on the frequency of incident radiation.
    2. When v < v0 , Kmax becomes negative. The kinetic energy becomes negative which has no physical meaning. Hence there is no photoelectric emission below the threshold frequency v0.
    3. It is obvious from the photo-electric equation that the maximum K.E. of photo-electrons does not depend on the intensity of incident light.

u^{2}=h 
u-W_{0}=h 
u-0= rac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}v=\sqrt{ rac{2 h c}{m \lambda}}{/tex} de Broglie wavelength of electrons, {tex}\lambda_{e}= rac{h}{m v}{/tex} {tex}= rac{h}{m} \sqrt{ rac{m \lambda}{2 h c}}=\sqrt{ rac{h \lambda}{2 m c}}{/tex}
solution_html:
The frequency of ultraviolet radiations is more while that of red light is less than the threshold frequency for a zinc surface, so ultraviolet radiations can cause the emission of electrons and red light cannot.
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, K.E. of a photoelectron is

question_html: How does the stopping potential applied to a photocell change, if the distance between the light source and the cathode of the cell is doubled?
question_text: How does the stopping potential applied to a photocell change, if the distance between the light source and the cathode of the cell is doubled?
explanation: Stopping potential remains unchanged. Reason: On doubling the distance between the light source and the cathode of the cell, the intensity of light incident on the photocell becomes one-fourth. As stopping potential does not depend on intensity, the stopping potential remains unchanged.
solution: Stopping potential remains unchanged. Reason: On doubling the distance between the light source and the cathode of the cell, the intensity of light incident on the photocell becomes one-fourth. As stopping potential does not depend on intensity, the stopping potential remains unchanged.

question_html: An {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle and a proton are accelerated through the same potential difference. Find the ratio of their de-Broglie wavelengths.
question_text: An {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle and a proton are accelerated through the same potential difference. Find the ratio of their de-Broglie wavelengths.
explanation: We have, de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m q V } }{/tex} Given, {tex}V _ { \alpha } = V _ { p } = V{/tex} (say) {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { \alpha } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 m _ { p } q _ { p } V } } { \sqrt { 2 m _ { \alpha } q _ { \alpha } V } } = \sqrt { \frac { m _ { p } q _ { p } } { m _ { \alpha } q _ {\alpha } } }{/tex} Now, {tex}m_\alpha=4m_p{/tex} and {tex}q_\alpha=2q_p{/tex} Thus, {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { \alpha } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { 4 \times 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
solution: We have, de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m q V } }{/tex} Given, {tex}V _ { \alpha } = V _ { p } = V{/tex} (say) {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { \alpha } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 m _ { p } q _ { p } V } } { \sqrt { 2 m _ { \alpha } q _ { \alpha } V } } = \sqrt { \frac { m _ { p } q _ { p } } { m _ { \alpha } q _ {\alpha } } }{/tex} Now, {tex}m_\alpha=4m_p{/tex} and {tex}q_\alpha=2q_p{/tex} Thus, {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { \alpha } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { 4 \times 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, de-Broglie wavelength,
{tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m q V } }{/tex}
Given, {tex}V _ { \alpha } = V _ { p } = V{/tex}(say)
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { \alpha } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \frac { \sqrt { 2 m _ { p } q _ { p } V } } { \sqrt { 2 m _ { \alpha } q _ { \alpha } V } } = \sqrt { \frac { m _ { p } q _ { p } } { m _ { \alpha } q _ {\alpha } } }{/tex}
Now, {tex}m_\alpha=4m_p{/tex} and {tex}q_\alpha=2q_p{/tex}
Thus,
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { \alpha } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { \frac { 1 } { 4 \times 2 } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 \sqrt { 2 } }{/tex}

question_html: An electron microscope uses electrons accelerated by a voltage of 50 kV. Determine the de Broglie wavelength associated with the electrons. If other factors (such as numerical aperture, etc.) are taken to be roughly the same, how does the resolving power of an electron microscope compare with that of an optical microscope which uses yellow light?
question_text: An electron microscope uses electrons accelerated by a voltage of 50 kV. Determine the de Broglie wavelength associated with the electrons. If other factors (such as numerical aperture, etc.) are taken to be roughly the same, how does the resolving power of an electron microscope compare with that of an optical microscope which uses yellow light?
explanation: Given V = 50 kV = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 volt K.E. of electron, E k = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 eV = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 J de-Broglie wave length of electron, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_{k}}}=\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 50 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-16}}}=5.5 \times 10^{-12}{/tex} m For yellow light wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m. Since resolving power (R.P.) is inversely proportional to wavelength, therefore R.P. of an electron microscope is about 10 5 times more than optical microscope.
solution: Given V = 50 kV = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 volt K.E. of electron, E k = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 eV = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -16 J de-Broglie wave length of electron, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_{k}}}=\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 50 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-16}}}=5.5 \times 10^{-12}{/tex} m For yellow light wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m. Since resolving power (R.P.) is inversely proportional to wavelength, therefore R.P. of an electron microscope is about 10 5 times more than optical microscope.
solution_html:
Given V = 50 kV = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 volt
K.E. of electron, Ek = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 eV = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J = 50 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-16 J
de-Broglie wave length of electron, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_{k}}}=\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)}{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 50 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-16}}}=5.5 \times 10^{-12}{/tex}m
For yellow light wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m.
Since resolving power (R.P.) is inversely proportional to wavelength, therefore R.P. of an electron microscope is about 105 times more than optical microscope.
