question_html: Write three characteristic features in photoelectric effect which cannot be explained on the basis of the wave theory of light, but can be explained only using Einstein’s equation.
question_text: Write three characteristic features in photoelectric effect which cannot be explained on the basis of the wave theory of light, but can be explained only using Einstein’s equation.
explanation: The three characteristic features which can't be explained by wave theory are : The kinetic energy of emitted electrons is found to be independent of the intensity of incident light. For a given metal there exists a certain minimum frequency of the incident radiation below which no emission takes place, this frequency is called the threshold frequency. Spontaneous emission of photo-electrons.
solution: The three characteristic features which can't be explained by wave theory are : The kinetic energy of emitted electrons is found to be independent of the intensity of incident light. For a given metal there exists a certain minimum frequency of the incident radiation below which no emission takes place, this frequency is called the threshold frequency. Spontaneous emission of photo-electrons.
solution_html:
The three characteristic features which can't be explained by wave theory are :

  1. The kinetic energy of emitted electrons is found to be independent of the intensity of incident light.
  2. For a given metal there exists a certain minimum frequency of the incident radiation below which no emission takes place, this frequency is called the threshold frequency.
  3. Spontaneous emission of photo-electrons.

question_html: It is easier to remove an electron from sodium than from copper. What metal has higher value of threshold frequency?
question_text: It is easier to remove an electron from sodium than from copper. What metal has higher value of threshold frequency?
explanation: The minimum energy required to remove an electron from the surface of metal without giving any kinetic energy is called work function. The work function of sodium is less than that of copper. Therefore, it is easier to remove an electron from sodium than from copper. As, work function, {tex}\phi_0 = h\nu_0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} threshold frequency, {tex}\nu_0= \phi_0 / h{/tex} It shows, the threshold frequency of copper is more than that of sodium.
solution: The minimum energy required to remove an electron from the surface of metal without giving any kinetic energy is called work function. The work function of sodium is less than that of copper. Therefore, it is easier to remove an electron from sodium than from copper. As, work function, {tex}\phi_0 = h\nu_0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} threshold frequency, {tex}\nu_0= \phi_0 / h{/tex} It shows, the threshold frequency of copper is more than that of sodium.
solution_html:
The minimum energy required to remove an electron from the surface of metal without giving any kinetic energy is called work function. The work function of sodium is less than that of copper. Therefore, it is easier to remove an electron from sodium than from copper.
As, work function, {tex}\phi_0 = h\nu_0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} threshold frequency, {tex}\nu_0= \phi_0 / h{/tex}
It shows, the threshold frequency of copper is more than that of sodium.

question_html:
The work function for the following metals is given: Na : 2.75 eV, K : 2.30 eV, M0 : 4.17 eV, Ni : 5.15 eV.

question_text: The work function for the following metals is given: Na : 2.75 eV, K : 2.30 eV, M 0 : 4.17 eV, Ni : 5.15 eV. Which of these metals will not give photoelectric emission for a radiation of wavelength {tex}3300\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} from a He-Cd laser placed 1m away from the photocell? What happens if the laser is brought nearer and placed 50 cm away?
explanation: Work function of Na is {tex}{\phi _{Na}} = 1.92eV = 1.92 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 3300\mathop A\limits^o = 3300 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex} {tex}E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda } = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{3300 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 3.75 eV It is observed that energy of incident radiation is less than Ni and Mo but larger than Na and K. So photoemission current take place from Na and K but not from Mo and Ni. Therefore, Mo and Ni will not give photoelectric emission. If the laser is brought closer, the intensity of radiation increases without any change in frequency. This therefore, will not affect the result for Ni and Mo. However, photoelectric current from Na and K will increase.
solution: Work function of Na is {tex}{\phi _{Na}} = 1.92eV = 1.92 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 3300\mathop A\limits^o = 3300 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex} {tex}E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda } = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{3300 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{/tex} = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J = 3.75 eV It is observed that energy of incident radiation is less than Ni and Mo but larger than Na and K. So photoemission current take place from Na and K but not from Mo and Ni. Therefore, Mo and Ni will not give photoelectric emission. If the laser is brought closer, the intensity of radiation increases without any change in frequency. This therefore, will not affect the result for Ni and Mo. However, photoelectric current from Na and K will increase.
solution_html:
Work function of Na is
{tex}{\phi _{Na}} = 1.92eV = 1.92 \times 1.6 \times {10^{ - 19}}J{/tex}
{tex}\lambda = 3300\mathop A\limits^o = 3300 \times {10^{ - 10}}m{/tex}
{tex}E = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda } = \frac{{6.6 \times {{10}^{ - 34}} \times 3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{3300 \times {{10}^{ - 10}}}}{/tex}
= 6{tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J = 3.75 eV
It is observed that energy of incident radiation is less than Ni and Mo but larger than Na and K. So photoemission current take place from Na and K but not from Mo and Ni. Therefore, Mo and Ni will not give photoelectric emission. If the laser is brought closer, the intensity of radiation increases without any change in frequency. This therefore, will not affect the result for Ni and Mo. However, photoelectric current from Na and K will increase.

question_text: Draw the graph showing the variation of de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} of a particle of charge q and mass m with the accelerating potential. An electron and proton have the same de Broglie wavelengths. Explain, which of the two has more kinetic energy.
explanation: Wavelength of a particle is given by {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} for an electron and proton q p = q e m p > m e Since wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} , and both particles have same de-Broglie wavelength, h & Kinetic energy is given by qV {tex}\therefore \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{e} \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}_{e}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K.E._{p}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m e (K.E.) e = m p (K.E.) p {tex}\because{/tex} m p > m e {tex}\therefore{/tex} K.E. of electron will be more than that of K.E. of proton
solution: Wavelength of a particle is given by {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} for an electron and proton q p = q e m p > m e Since wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} , and both particles have same de-Broglie wavelength, h & Kinetic energy is given by qV {tex}\therefore \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{e} \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}_{e}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K.E._{p}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} m e (K.E.) e = m p (K.E.) p {tex}\because{/tex} m p > m e {tex}\therefore{/tex} K.E. of electron will be more than that of K.E. of proton
solution_html:
1. Wavelength of a particle is given by
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex}
  2. for an electron and proton
    qp = qe
    mp > me
    Since wavelength, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex}, and both particles have same de-Broglie wavelength, h & Kinetic energy is given by qV 
    {tex}\therefore \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{e} \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}_{e}}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} K.E._{p}}}{/tex}

question_html:
In the study of a photoelectric effect, the graph between the stopping potential V and frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} of the incident radiation on two different metals P and Q is shown below.

  1. Which one of the two metals has higher threshold frequency?
  2. Determine the work function of the metal which has greater value.
question_text: In the study of a photoelectric effect, the graph between the stopping potential V and frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} of the incident radiation on two different metals P and Q is shown below. Which one of the two metals has higher threshold frequency? Determine the work function of the metal which has greater value.
solution_html:
1. Einstein's photoelectric equation is given by,
    h{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\phi{/tex} + eV
    {tex}V = \frac { h \nu } { e } - \frac { \phi } { e }{/tex}........(i)
    Eq. (1) represents a straight line given by line P and Q, {tex}\frac{\phi}{e}{/tex} represents negative intercept on the Y-axis. Since Q has greater negative intercept, it will have greater {tex}\phi{/tex} (work function) and hence higher threshold frequency.
  2. To know work function of Q, we put V = 0 in (i), 
    {tex}0 = \frac { h \nu } { e } - \frac { \phi } { e } \Rightarrow \phi = h \nu{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \phi = 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 6 \times 10 ^ { 14 } \mathrm { J }{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { 6.6 \times 6 \times 10 ^ { - 20 } } { 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } \mathrm { eV } = 25 \mathrm { eV }{/tex}

question_html:
1. State two important features of Einstein's photoelectric equation.
  2. Radiation of frequency 1015 Hz is incident on two photosensitive surfaces P and Q. There is no photoemission from surface P. Photoemission occurs from surface Q but photoelectrons have zero kinetic energy. Explain these observations and find the value of work function for surface Q.
question_text: State two important features of Einstein's photoelectric equation. Radiation of frequency 10 15 Hz is incident on two photosensitive surfaces P and Q. There is no photoemission from surface P. Photoemission occurs from surface Q but photoelectrons have zero kinetic energy. Explain these observations and find the value of work function for surface Q.
solution_html:
1. Einstein's photoelectric equation is
     eV0 = Kmax = h{tex}\nu{/tex}- {tex}\phi_0{/tex}
    Important features of this equation are given below
    1. Photoemission occurs when frequency of incident radiation is more than the threshold frequency,
      {tex}\nu{/tex}o = {tex}\frac { \phi _ { 0 } } { h }{/tex}
    2. Energy of emitted photoelectron is proportional to energy of incident photon.
    3. Photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process.
  2. The threshold frequency of surface P is greater than 1015 Hz, and that is the reason no photoemission takes place. 
    For surface Q, the threshold frequency is equal to 1015 Hz. So, photoemission takes place but photoelectrons have zero kinetic energy.
    If the kinetic energy of the electrons emitted from surface B has to be increased then, the wavelength of the incident radiation has to be decreased.
    Energy of incident photon is less than work function of P but just equal to that of Q.
    For Q, Work function,
    {tex}\phi _ { 0 } = \frac { h\nu } { e } ( e \mathrm { V } ){/tex}{tex}= \frac { 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 10 ^ { 15 } } { 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } = 4.1 \mathrm { eV }{/tex}

question_html:
1. How does one explain the emission of electrons from a photosensitive surface with the help of Einstein's photoelectric equation?
  2. The work function of the following metals is given: Na = 2.75 eV, K = 2.3 eV, Mo = 4.17 eV and Ni = 5.15 eV. Which of these metals will not cause photoelectric emission for radiation of wavelength 3300 A from a laser source placed 1 m away from these metals? What happens if the laser source is brought nearer and placed 50 cm away.
question_text: How does one explain the emission of electrons from a photosensitive surface with the help of Einstein's photoelectric equation? The work function of the following metals is given: Na = 2.75 eV, K = 2.3 eV, Mo = 4.17 eV and Ni = 5.15 eV. Which of these metals will not cause photoelectric emission for radiation of wavelength 3300 A from a laser source placed 1 m away from these metals? What happens if the laser source is brought nearer and placed 50 cm away.
explanation: Einstein’s Photoelectric equation is h {tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} + k max When a photon of energy 'h {tex}\nu{/tex} ' is incident on the metal, some part of this energy is utilized as work function to eject the electron and remaining energy appears as the kinetic energy of the emitted electron. {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{3.3 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 3.77 eV The work function of Mo and Ni is more than the energy of the incident photons, so photoelectric emission will not take place from these metals. Kinetic energy of photoelectrons will not change, only photoelectric current will change. The distance of the laser is of no consequence.
solution: Einstein’s Photoelectric equation is h {tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} + k max When a photon of energy 'h {tex}\nu{/tex} ' is incident on the metal, some part of this energy is utilized as work function to eject the electron and remaining energy appears as the kinetic energy of the emitted electron. {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{3.3 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 3.77 eV The work function of Mo and Ni is more than the energy of the incident photons, so photoelectric emission will not take place from these metals. Kinetic energy of photoelectrons will not change, only photoelectric current will change. The distance of the laser is of no consequence.
solution_html:
1. Einstein’s Photoelectric equation is
    h{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} + kmax
    When a photon of energy 'h{tex}\nu{/tex}' is incident on the metal, some part of this energy is utilized as work function to eject the electron and remaining energy appears as the kinetic energy of the emitted electron.
  2. {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{3.3 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex}
    = 3.77 eV
    The work function of Mo and Ni is more than the energy of the incident photons, so photoelectric emission will not take place from these metals.
    Kinetic energy of photoelectrons will not change, only photoelectric current will change. The distance of the laser is of no consequence.

question_html: Using photon picture of light, show how Einstein's photoelectric equation can be established. Write two features of photoelectric effect which cannot be explained by wave theory.
question_text: Using photon picture of light, show how Einstein's photoelectric equation can be established. Write two features of photoelectric effect which cannot be explained by wave theory.
explanation: In the photon picture, the energy of the light is assumed to be in the form of photons, each carrying an h {tex}\nu{/tex} . energy.Einstein assumed that photoelectric emission occurs because of a single collision of a photon with a free electron. The energy of the photon is used to Free the electrons from the metal. [For this, minimum energy, called the work function (= {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} ) is needed] and rest energy is given to electron is kinetic energy. Hence, (K.E.) max = hv - {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^{2} _{max} =h v-\phi_{0}\right){/tex} This is Einstein’s photoelectric equation Two features (which cannot be explained by wave theory): ’Instantaneous’ emission of photoelectrons Wave theory also fails to explain the existence of threshold energy. ’Maximum kinetic energy’ of the emitted photoelectrons, is independent of the intensity of incident light.
solution: In the photon picture, the energy of the light is assumed to be in the form of photons, each carrying an h {tex}\nu{/tex} . energy.Einstein assumed that photoelectric emission occurs because of a single collision of a photon with a free electron. The energy of the photon is used to Free the electrons from the metal. [For this, minimum energy, called the work function (= {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} ) is needed] and rest energy is given to electron is kinetic energy. Hence, (K.E.) max = hv - {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^{2} _{max} =h v-\phi_{0}\right){/tex} This is Einstein’s photoelectric equation Two features (which cannot be explained by wave theory): ’Instantaneous’ emission of photoelectrons Wave theory also fails to explain the existence of threshold energy. ’Maximum kinetic energy’ of the emitted photoelectrons, is independent of the intensity of incident light.
solution_html:
In the photon picture, the energy of the light is assumed to be in the form of photons, each carrying an  h{tex}\nu{/tex}. energy.Einstein assumed that photoelectric emission occurs because of a single collision of a photon with a        free electron.
The energy of the photon is used to Free the electrons from the metal. 
[For this, minimum energy, called the work function (= {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex}) is needed]  and rest energy is given to electron is  kinetic energy.  Hence,

           (K.E.)max = hv - {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex}
           or {tex}\left(\frac{1}{2} m v^{2} _{max} =h v-\phi_{0}\right){/tex}
     This is Einstein’s photoelectric equation

      Two features (which cannot be explained by wave theory):

  1. ’Instantaneous’ emission of photoelectrons
  2. Wave theory also fails to explain the existence of threshold energy.
  3. ’Maximum kinetic energy’ of the emitted photoelectrons, is independent of the intensity of incident light.

question_html: For a monochromatic radiation incident on a photosensitive surface, why do all photoelectrons not come out with the same energy? Give a reason for your answer.
question_text: For a monochromatic radiation incident on a photosensitive surface, why do all photoelectrons not come out with the same energy? Give a reason for your answer.
explanation: The different electrons belong to different energy levels in the conduction band. They need different energies to come out of the metal surface. For the same incident radiation, electrons knocked off from different energy levels come out with different energies.
solution: The different electrons belong to different energy levels in the conduction band. They need different energies to come out of the metal surface. For the same incident radiation, electrons knocked off from different energy levels come out with different energies.

When a photon of energy h{tex}\nu{/tex} is incident on a metal surface, a part of this energy equal to the work function W0 is used in removing the electron from the metal surface and the remaining energy appears as the kinetic energy of the emitted electron.

Maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of incident radiation.
solution_html:
1. The collision of a photon can cause emission of a photoelectron (if the frequency is above the threshold frequency). As intensity, if the frequency increases, number of photons increases. Hence, the current increases.
  2. We have, eV0= h({tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\nu_0{/tex})
    {tex}V_{0}=\frac{h}{e} \nu-\frac{h \nu_{0}}{e}{/tex}

    Graph of V0 vs.{tex}\nu{/tex} is a straight line and slope is a constant.
  3. Maximum K.E. for different surfaces is given by  = h({tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\nu_0{/tex})
    Hence, kinetic energy  depends on the frequency and not on the intensity of the incident radiation.

question_text: In the study of a photoelectric effect the graph between the stopping potential V and frequency of the incident radiation on two different metals P and Q is shown below. Which one of the two metals has higher threshold frequency? Determine the work function of the metal which has greater value. Find the maximum kinetic energy of electron emitted by light of frequency 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz for this metal.
explanation: Q has higher threshold frequency Work function of Q {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = hv {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 6 \times 10^{14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = 2.47 eV KE max = hv - hv 0 = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 (8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 - 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 ) = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 J = {tex}6.6\times 10^{-{34}} \times2 \times 10^{14} eV\over 1.6\times 10^{-19}{/tex} hence KE max = 0.83 eV
solution: Q has higher threshold frequency Work function of Q {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = hv {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 6 \times 10^{14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = 2.47 eV KE max = hv - hv 0 = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 (8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 - 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 ) = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 J = {tex}6.6\times 10^{-{34}} \times2 \times 10^{14} eV\over 1.6\times 10^{-19}{/tex} hence KE max = 0.83 eV
solution_html:
1. Q has higher threshold frequency
  2. Work function of Q {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = hv
    {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 6 \times 10^{14}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}{/tex}
    = 2.47 eV
  3. KEmax = hv - hv0
    = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34(8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 - 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014)
    = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014J
    = {tex}6.6\times 10^{-{34}} \times2 \times 10^{14} eV\over 1.6\times 10^{-19}{/tex}
    hence KEmax = 0.83 eV

explanation: According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, eV 0 = hv - W 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} eV 01 = hv 1 - W 0 and eV 02 = hv 2 - W 0 or e(V 02 - V 01 ) = h (v 2 - v 1 ) or V 02 - V 01 {tex}=\frac{h\left(v_{2}-v_{1}\right)}{e}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.4 \times 10^{-34} \times(8-4) \times 10^{15}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=16 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
solution: According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, eV 0 = hv - W 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} eV 01 = hv 1 - W 0 and eV 02 = hv 2 - W 0 or e(V 02 - V 01 ) = h (v 2 - v 1 ) or V 02 - V 01 {tex}=\frac{h\left(v_{2}-v_{1}\right)}{e}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.4 \times 10^{-34} \times(8-4) \times 10^{15}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}=16 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}

question_text: An {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle and a proton have the same de Broglie wavelength equal to 1 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex} . Explain with calculations, which of the two has more kinetic energy.
explanation: {tex}K=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow K \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex} [For same {tex}\lambda{/tex} ] {tex}\therefore \frac{K_{\alpha}}{K_{p}}=\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}=\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} or {tex}K_{p}=4 K_{\alpha}{/tex} K.E. of a proton is 4 times the K.E. of an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle.
solution: {tex}K=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow K \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex} [For same {tex}\lambda{/tex} ] {tex}\therefore \frac{K_{\alpha}}{K_{p}}=\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}=\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} or {tex}K_{p}=4 K_{\alpha}{/tex} K.E. of a proton is 4 times the K.E. of an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle.
solution_html:
{tex}K=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow K \propto \frac{1}{m}{/tex} [For same {tex}\lambda{/tex}]
{tex}\therefore \frac{K_{\alpha}}{K_{p}}=\frac{m_{p}}{m_{\alpha}}=\frac{m_{p}}{4 m_{p}}=\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
or {tex}K_{p}=4 K_{\alpha}{/tex}
K.E. of a proton is 4 times the K.E. of an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle.

question_text: Calculate the kinetic energy of an electron having de Broglie wavelength of {tex}1\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}}{/tex} .
explanation: From de Broglie hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad K=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1 \times 10^{-10}} \mathrm{~J}{/tex} {tex}=2.4 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~J}=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-17}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 150 V.
solution: From de Broglie hypothesis, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad K=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1 \times 10^{-10}} \mathrm{~J}{/tex} {tex}=2.4 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~J}=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-17}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 150 V.
solution_html:
From de Broglie hypothesis,
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad K=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 1 \times 10^{-10}} \mathrm{~J}{/tex}
{tex}=2.4 \times 10^{-17} \mathrm{~J}=\frac{2.4 \times 10^{-17}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 150 V.

question_html: Define the term 'work function' of a metal. The threshold frequency of a metal is f0. When the light of frequency 2 f0 is incident on the metal plate, the maximum velocity of electrons emitted is {tex} u_{1} {/tex}. When the frequency of the incident radiation is increased to 5 f0, the maximum velocity of electrons emitted is {tex} u_{2}{/tex}. Find the ratio of {tex} u_{1} {/tex} to {tex} u_{2} {/tex}.
explanation: The minimum amount of energy required to eject an electron from a metal surface (without imparting it any kinetic energy) is called work function of the metal. As f 0 is the threshold frequency, so W = hf 0 From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron is given by {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}=h \times 2 f_{0}-W=h \times 2 f_{0}-h f_{0}=h f_{0}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2}=h \times 5 f_{0}-h f_{0}=4 h f_{0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\frac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2}}=\frac{h f_{0}}{4 h f_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1 : 2
solution: The minimum amount of energy required to eject an electron from a metal surface (without imparting it any kinetic energy) is called work function of the metal. As f 0 is the threshold frequency, so W = hf 0 From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron is given by {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}=h \times 2 f_{0}-W=h \times 2 f_{0}-h f_{0}=h f_{0}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2}=h \times 5 f_{0}-h f_{0}=4 h f_{0}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\frac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2}}=\frac{h f_{0}}{4 h f_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1 : 2
solution_html:
The minimum amount of energy required to eject an electron from a metal surface (without imparting it any kinetic energy) is called work function of the metal. As f0 is the threshold frequency, so
W = hf0
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of emitted electron is given by
{tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}=h \times 2 f_{0}-W=h \times 2 f_{0}-h f_{0}=h f_{0}{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2}=h \times 5 f_{0}-h f_{0}=4 h f_{0}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{\frac{1}{2} m v_{1}^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} m v_{2}^{2}}=\frac{h f_{0}}{4 h f_{0}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 1 : 2

question_html: Write the basic features of the photon picture of electromagnetic radiation on which Einstein's photoelectric equation is based.
question_text: Write the basic features of the photon picture of electromagnetic radiation on which Einstein's photoelectric equation is based.
explanation: The basic features of the photon picture of e.m. radiation are as follows: Light is composed of discrete packets of energy called quanta or photons. Each photon carries an energy E ( {tex}=h\nu{/tex} ) and momentum p (= h / {tex}\lambda{/tex} ), which depend on the frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} of the incident radiation and not on its intensity. During the collision of a photon with an electron, the total energy of the photon gets absorbed by the electron. Photoelectric emission from the metal surface occurs due to the absorption of a photon by an electron.
solution: The basic features of the photon picture of e.m. radiation are as follows: Light is composed of discrete packets of energy called quanta or photons. Each photon carries an energy E ( {tex}=h\nu{/tex} ) and momentum p (= h / {tex}\lambda{/tex} ), which depend on the frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} of the incident radiation and not on its intensity. During the collision of a photon with an electron, the total energy of the photon gets absorbed by the electron. Photoelectric emission from the metal surface occurs due to the absorption of a photon by an electron.
solution_html:
The basic features of the photon picture of e.m. radiation are as follows:

  1. Light is composed of discrete packets of energy called quanta or photons.
  2. Each photon carries an energy E ({tex}=h\nu{/tex}) and momentum p (= h / {tex}\lambda{/tex}), which depend on the frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} of the incident radiation and not on its intensity.
  3. During the collision of a photon with an electron, the total energy of the photon gets absorbed by the electron.
  4. Photoelectric emission from the metal surface occurs due to the absorption of a photon by an electron.

State two important properties of the photon which are used to write Einstein's photoelectric equation. Define

  2. threshold frequency, using Einstein equation and drawing necessary plot between relevant quantities
question_text: State two important properties of the photon which are used to write Einstein's photoelectric equation. Define stopping potential and threshold frequency, using Einstein equation and drawing necessary plot between relevant quantities
explanation: Properties of Photon: For a radiation of frequency v, each photon has an energy, E = h {tex}\nu{/tex} , associated with it The energy of a photon is independent of the intensity of incident radiation. Stopping potential, V 0 , equals that value of the negative potential for which {tex}\left|e V_{0}\right|=K_{\max }{/tex} (Alternatively: The stopping potential (V 0 ) equals that (least) value of the (negative) plate potential that just stops the most energetic emitted photoelectrons from reaching the plate.) Threshold frequency ( {tex}\nu_0{/tex} ) equals that value of the frequency of incident radiation for which K max = 0 ( Or threshold frequency is the minimum value of the frequency of incident radiation for which photoelectrons can be just emitted from that surface or that maximum frequency of incident radiation below which no photoemission takes place.) The plot, between V 0 and v has the form shown :
solution: Properties of Photon: For a radiation of frequency v, each photon has an energy, E = h {tex}\nu{/tex} , associated with it The energy of a photon is independent of the intensity of incident radiation. Stopping potential, V 0 , equals that value of the negative potential for which {tex}\left|e V_{0}\right|=K_{\max }{/tex} (Alternatively: The stopping potential (V 0 ) equals that (least) value of the (negative) plate potential that just stops the most energetic emitted photoelectrons from reaching the plate.) Threshold frequency ( {tex}\nu_0{/tex} ) equals that value of the frequency of incident radiation for which K max = 0 ( Or threshold frequency is the minimum value of the frequency of incident radiation for which photoelectrons can be just emitted from that surface or that maximum frequency of incident radiation below which no photoemission takes place.) The plot, between V 0 and v has the form shown :
solution_html:
Properties of Photon:

  1. For a radiation of frequency v, each photon has an energy, E = h{tex}\nu{/tex}, associated with it
  2. The energy of a photon is independent of the intensity of incident radiation.
    1. Stopping potential, V0, equals that value of the negative potential for which
      {tex}\left|e V_{0}\right|=K_{\max }{/tex}
      (Alternatively: The stopping potential (V0) equals that (least) value of the (negative) plate potential that just stops the most energetic emitted photoelectrons from reaching the plate.)
    2. Threshold frequency ({tex}\nu_0{/tex}) equals that value of the frequency of incident radiation for which
      Kmax = 0

( Or threshold frequency is the minimum value of the frequency of incident radiation for which photoelectrons can be just emitted from that surface or that maximum frequency of incident radiation below which no photoemission takes place.)

question_html: In the wave picture of light, intensity of light is determined by square of the amplitude of wave. What determines the intensity of light in the photon picture of light?
question_text: In the wave picture of light, intensity of light is determined by square of the amplitude of wave. What determines the intensity of light in the photon picture of light?
explanation: The intensity of radiation of given wavelength represent the no. of energy quanta or photons incident per unit area per time. With each photon having the same energy for a given frequency, intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by {tex}I = \frac { \text { Energy of photons } } { \text { area } \times \text { time } } = \frac { n \times h \nu } { A \times t }{/tex} where n is the number of photons incident normally on crossing area A in time t.
solution: The intensity of radiation of given wavelength represent the no. of energy quanta or photons incident per unit area per time. With each photon having the same energy for a given frequency, intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by {tex}I = \frac { \text { Energy of photons } } { \text { area } \times \text { time } } = \frac { n \times h \nu } { A \times t }{/tex} where n is the number of photons incident normally on crossing area A in time t.
solution_html:
The intensity of radiation of given wavelength represent the no. of energy quanta or photons incident per unit area per time. With each photon having the same energy for a given frequency, intensity of light in the photon picture is determined by
{tex}I = \frac { \text { Energy of photons } } { \text { area } \times \text { time } } = \frac { n \times h \nu } { A \times t }{/tex}
where n is the number of photons incident normally on crossing area A in time t.

question_html: The wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}, of a photon and the de-Broglie wavelength of an electron have the same value. Show that energy of a photon is {tex}\frac { 2 { \lambda } { } m c } { h }{/tex} times the kinetic energy of electron, where m, c and h have their usual meaning.
question_text: The wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} , of a photon and the de-Broglie wavelength of an electron have the same value. Show that energy of a photon is {tex}\frac { 2 { \lambda } { } m c } { h }{/tex} times the kinetic energy of electron, where m, c and h have their usual meaning.
explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength of the electron is {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac h{mv}{/tex} K.E of electron, {tex}E _ { e } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m \left( \frac { h } { m \lambda } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .......(i) We know that energy of photon is E p = {tex}\frac {hc}\lambda{/tex} ........(ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get, {tex}\frac { E _ { e } } { E _ { p } } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { \lambda } { h c } \Rightarrow E _ { p } = \frac { 2 { \lambda } { } m c } { h } E _ { e }{/tex}
solution: The de-Broglie wavelength of the electron is {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac h{mv}{/tex} K.E of electron, {tex}E _ { e } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m \left( \frac { h } { m \lambda } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 } }{/tex} .......(i) We know that energy of photon is E p = {tex}\frac {hc}\lambda{/tex} ........(ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get, {tex}\frac { E _ { e } } { E _ { p } } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { \lambda } { h c } \Rightarrow E _ { p } = \frac { 2 { \lambda } { } m c } { h } E _ { e }{/tex}
solution_html:
The de-Broglie wavelength of the electron is {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac h{mv}{/tex}
K.E of electron,
{tex}E _ { e } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m \left( \frac { h } { m \lambda } \right) ^ { 2 } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 } }{/tex}.......(i)
We know that energy of photon is Ep = {tex}\frac {hc}\lambda{/tex} ........(ii)
On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get,
{tex}\frac { E _ { e } } { E _ { p } } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { \lambda } { h c } \Rightarrow E _ { p } = \frac { 2 { \lambda } { } m c } { h } E _ { e }{/tex}

question_html: Define the term "cut-off frequency" in photoelectric emission. The threshold frequency of a metal is {tex} u_0{/tex}. When the light of frequency 2{tex} u_o{/tex} is incident on the metal plate, the maximum velocity of photo-electron is v1. When the frequency of the incident radiation is increased to 5{tex} u_o{/tex}, the maximum velocity of photoelectrons is v2. Find the ratio of v1 : v2.
solution_html:
Cut-off frequency : The minimum value of the frequency of light below which the photoelectric emission stops completely, howsoever large may be the intensity of light.
Given, threshold frequency of metal is {tex}\nu_o{/tex} and frequency of light is 2{tex}\nu_o{/tex}.
Using Einstein's equation for the photoelectric effect, we can write
{tex}h ( 2\nu_o - \nu_o ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}h \nu_o = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}.....(i)
Similarly, for light having frequency 5{tex}\nu_o{/tex}, using Einstein's equation for the photoelectric effect, we can write
{tex}h ( 5 \nu - \nu_o ) = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}4h \nu_o = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v _ { 2 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}.....(ii)
Using Eqs. (i) and (ii), we find,
{tex}\mathrm { 1} / 4 \mathrm { } = v _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } / v _ { 2 } ^ { 2 } \Rightarrow v _ { 1 } / v _ { 2 } = \sqrt { 1 / 4 } \Rightarrow v _ { 1 } / v _ { 2 } = 1 / 2{/tex}

In which case, is the stopping potential more and why? Does the slope of the graph depend on the nature of the material used? Explain.
solution_html:
The graphs showing a variation of stopping potential with a frequency of incident radiations for two photosensitive materials A and B having threshold frequencies {tex}\nu{/tex}A > {tex}\nu{/tex}B is shown below:

  1. The stopping potential has a linear relation with the frequency of incident radiation. It increases linearly with the increase in the frequency of incident radiation. Therefore, the stopping potential of metal A is more than that of metal B as {tex}\nu{/tex}A > {tex}\nu{/tex}B.
  2. No, the slope of the graph does not depend on the nature of the material used. Since the slope of the graph tells us the value of h/e which is constant for both the materials.

Which of these will not give photoelectron emission from a radiation of wavelength 3300 {tex}\overset o A{/tex} from a laser beam? What happens if the source of laser beam is brought closer?
question_text: Plot a graph showing the variation of photoelectric current with the intensity of light. The work function for the following metals is given: Na : 2.75 eV and Mo : 4.175 eV. Which of these will not give photoelectron emission from a radiation of wavelength 3300 {tex}\overset o A{/tex} from a laser beam? What happens if the source of laser beam is brought closer?
explanation: The graph shows the variation of photoelectric current with the intensity of incident radiation is given below: Given that, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 3300 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m, {tex}\phi{/tex} Na = 2.75eV, {tex}\phi{/tex} Mo = 4.175eV Then the energy of the laser beam is {tex}E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } = \frac { 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } } { 3300 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } = 3.75 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} The work function of M o is more than the energy of the incident photons. So, photoelectric emission will not take place for M o metal. If the laser is brought closer, the intensity of incident radiation increases. This does not affect the result regarding M o metal, while photoelectric current will increase in proportion to intensity.
solution: The graph shows the variation of photoelectric current with the intensity of incident radiation is given below: Given that, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 3300 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -10 m, {tex}\phi{/tex} Na = 2.75eV, {tex}\phi{/tex} Mo = 4.175eV Then the energy of the laser beam is {tex}E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } = \frac { 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } } { 3300 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } = 3.75 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} The work function of M o is more than the energy of the incident photons. So, photoelectric emission will not take place for M o metal. If the laser is brought closer, the intensity of incident radiation increases. This does not affect the result regarding M o metal, while photoelectric current will increase in proportion to intensity.
solution_html:
The graph shows the variation of photoelectric current with the intensity of incident radiation is given below:

Given that, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 3300{tex}\times{/tex}10-10m, {tex}\phi{/tex}Na = 2.75eV, {tex}\phi{/tex}Mo = 4.175eV
Then the energy of the laser beam is
{tex}E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } = \frac { 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } } { 3300 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } = 3.75 \mathrm { eV }{/tex}
The work function of Mo is more than the energy of the incident photons. So, photoelectric emission will not take place for Mo metal.
If the laser is brought closer, the intensity of incident radiation increases. This does not affect the result regarding Mo metal, while photoelectric current will increase in proportion to intensity.

question_text: The work function (W), of a metal X, equals 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J. Calculate the number (N) of photons, of light of wavelength 26.52 nm, whose total energy equals W.
explanation: The work function of a metal is the minimum energy by it to eject an electron. It is given for metal X = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Energy of a photon of wavelength 26.52 nm = hv {tex}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} here h is planck constant {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{26.52 \times 10^{-9}}J{/tex} = 0.754 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 J If number of photons = n; for total energy 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Then, n {tex}\times{/tex} 0.754 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 Hence, {tex}n=\frac{3 \times 10^{-19}}{0.754 \times 10^{-17}}{/tex} = 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2
solution: The work function of a metal is the minimum energy by it to eject an electron. It is given for metal X = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Energy of a photon of wavelength 26.52 nm = hv {tex}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} here h is planck constant {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{26.52 \times 10^{-9}}J{/tex} = 0.754 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 J If number of photons = n; for total energy 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J Then, n {tex}\times{/tex} 0.754 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 Hence, {tex}n=\frac{3 \times 10^{-19}}{0.754 \times 10^{-17}}{/tex} = 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2
solution_html:
The work function of a metal is the minimum energy by it to eject an electron. It is given for metal
X = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
Energy of a photon of wavelength 26.52 nm = hv
{tex}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} here h is planck constant
{tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{26.52 \times 10^{-9}}J{/tex}
= 0.754 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 J
If number of photons = n; for total energy 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J
Then,
n {tex}\times{/tex} 0.754 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
Hence, {tex}n=\frac{3 \times 10^{-19}}{0.754 \times 10^{-17}}{/tex}
= 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2

question_html: A monochromatic light source of power 5 mW emits 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 photons per second. This light ejects photoelectrons from a metal surface. The stopping potential for this set up is 2 V. Calculate the work function of the metal.
question_text: A monochromatic light source of power 5 mW emits 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 photons per second. This light ejects photoelectrons from a metal surface. The stopping potential for this set up is 2 V. Calculate the work function of the metal.
explanation: Power of monochromatic light source = total energy released per second = number of photons emits per second {tex}\times{/tex} energy of one photon Number of photons emits per second = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Power of monochromatic light source = 5 mW Hence, energy of one photon, {tex}h \nu=\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{15}}{/tex} = 0.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 J {tex}=\frac{0.625 \times 10^{-18}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 3.9 eV K.E. max = V 0 eV = 2eV (V 0 = 2 Volts) where V 0 is stopping potential. K.E. = h {tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} 2 = 3.9 - {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = (3.9 - 2) eV {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = 1.9 eV
solution: Power of monochromatic light source = total energy released per second = number of photons emits per second {tex}\times{/tex} energy of one photon Number of photons emits per second = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Power of monochromatic light source = 5 mW Hence, energy of one photon, {tex}h \nu=\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{15}}{/tex} = 0.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -18 J {tex}=\frac{0.625 \times 10^{-18}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 3.9 eV K.E. max = V 0 eV = 2eV (V 0 = 2 Volts) where V 0 is stopping potential. K.E. = h {tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} 2 = 3.9 - {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = (3.9 - 2) eV {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = 1.9 eV
solution_html:
Power of monochromatic light source
= total energy released per second
= number of photons emits per second {tex}\times{/tex} energy of one photon
Number of photons emits per second = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015
Power of monochromatic light source = 5 mW
Hence, energy of one photon,
{tex}h \nu=\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{15}}{/tex}
= 0.625 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-18 J
{tex}=\frac{0.625 \times 10^{-18}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{/tex} = 3.9 eV
K.E.max = V0 eV  = 2eV (V0 = 2 Volts)   where V0 is stopping potential.
K.E. = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex}
2 = 3.9 - {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex}
{tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = (3.9 - 2) eV
{tex}\phi_{0}{/tex} = 1.9 eV

de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E_k}}{/tex}

1. What reasoning led de-Broglie to put forward the concept of matter wave?
  2. The wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}, of a photon, and de-Broglie wavelength associated with a particle of mass 'm' has the same value, say {tex}\lambda{/tex}. Show that the energy of photon is {tex}\frac{{2\lambda mc}}{h}{/tex} times the kinetic energy of the particle.
question_text: What reasoning led de-Broglie to put forward the concept of matter wave? The wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} , of a photon, and de-Broglie wavelength associated with a particle of mass 'm' has the same value, say {tex}\lambda{/tex} . Show that the energy of photon is {tex}\frac{{2\lambda mc}}{h}{/tex} times the kinetic energy of the particle.
explanation: de-Broglie put forward the bold hypothesis that moving particles of matter should display wave like properties under suitable conditions. He reasoned that nature is symmetrical and that the two basic physical entities, matter and energy must have symmetrical character. If radiation shows a dual nature, so should matter. Now, Kinetic Energy, {tex} K = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{2m}}{/tex} Also, {tex}p = \frac{h}{\lambda }{/tex} {tex}\therefore K = \frac{{{h^2}}}{{2m{\lambda ^2}}}{/tex} Also, E (photon) {tex} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{K}{E} = \frac{h}{{2mc\lambda }}{/tex} or {tex}E = \frac{{2mc\lambda }}{h} \cdot K{/tex}
solution: de-Broglie put forward the bold hypothesis that moving particles of matter should display wave like properties under suitable conditions. He reasoned that nature is symmetrical and that the two basic physical entities, matter and energy must have symmetrical character. If radiation shows a dual nature, so should matter. Now, Kinetic Energy, {tex} K = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{2m}}{/tex} Also, {tex}p = \frac{h}{\lambda }{/tex} {tex}\therefore K = \frac{{{h^2}}}{{2m{\lambda ^2}}}{/tex} Also, E (photon) {tex} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{K}{E} = \frac{h}{{2mc\lambda }}{/tex} or {tex}E = \frac{{2mc\lambda }}{h} \cdot K{/tex}
solution_html:
1. de-Broglie put forward the bold hypothesis that moving particles of matter should display wave like properties under suitable conditions. He reasoned that nature is symmetrical and that the two basic physical entities, matter and energy must have symmetrical character. If radiation shows a dual nature, so should matter.
  2. Now, Kinetic Energy, {tex} K = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{2m}}{/tex} 
    Also, {tex}p = \frac{h}{\lambda }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore K = \frac{{{h^2}}}{{2m{\lambda ^2}}}{/tex}
    Also, E (photon) {tex} = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{K}{E} = \frac{h}{{2mc\lambda }}{/tex}
    or {tex}E = \frac{{2mc\lambda }}{h} \cdot K{/tex}

question_html:
1. Write three observed features of photoelectric effect which cannot be explained by wave theory of light.
    Explain how Einstein's photoelectric equation is used to describe these features satisfactorily.
  2. The graphs, drawn here, are for the phenomenon of photoelectric effect.
    1. Identify which of the two characteristics (intensity/frequency) of incident light, is being kept constant in each case.
    2. Name the quantity, corresponding to the, mark, in each case.

    3. Justify the existence of a threshold frequency for a given photosensitive surface.
question_text: Write three observed features of photoelectric effect which cannot be explained by wave theory of light. Explain how Einstein's photoelectric equation is used to describe these features satisfactorily. The graphs, drawn here, are for the phenomenon of photoelectric effect. Identify which of the two characteristics (intensity/frequency) of incident light, is being kept constant in each case. Name the quantity, corresponding to the, mark, in each case. Justify the existence of a threshold frequency for a given photosensitive surface.
solution_html:
1. Three features, of photoelectric effect, which cannot be explained by the wave theory of light, are:
    1. Maximum kinetic energy of emitted electrons is independent of the intensity of incident light.
    2. There exists a threshold frequency for each photosensitive material.
    3. Photoelectric effect is instantaneous in nature. Einstein s photoelectric equation
      Kmax = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi_0{/tex}
    1. Graph 1: Intensity
      Graph 2: Frequency
    2. Graph 1: Saturation current
      Graph 2: Stopping potential
    3. For b given photo sensitive surface electrons need a minimum energy to be emitted, this is called work function of the surface W.
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} Photons energy hv should be greater/equal to the work function.
      {tex}\therefore{/tex} h{tex}\nu{/tex} {tex}\geq{/tex} W
      or {tex}\nu{/tex} {tex}\geq{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{W}}{h}{/tex}
      Minimum frequency for photo emission
      {tex}\nu{/tex}0 = {tex}\frac{W}{h}{/tex}

question_html:
A proton and a deuteron are accelerated through the same accelerating potential. Which one of the two has

  1. greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it and
  2. less momentum?
    Give reasons to justify your answer.
question_text: A proton and a deuteron are accelerated through the same accelerating potential. Which one of the two has greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it and less momentum? Give reasons to justify your answer.
explanation: de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m V _ { 0 } q } } \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m } }{/tex} [as V 0 and q are same, because proton and deuteron have been accelerated by same potential and have same charge]. Since, mass of proton is less as compared to a deuteron. So, proton will have higher value of de-Broglie wavelength. de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda = h / p \Rightarrow p = h / \lambda{/tex} As, {tex}\lambda _ { d } < \lambda _ { p }{/tex} So, {tex}p _ { d } > p _ { p }{/tex} Thus, proton will have less momentum.
solution: de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m V _ { 0 } q } } \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m } }{/tex} [as V 0 and q are same, because proton and deuteron have been accelerated by same potential and have same charge]. Since, mass of proton is less as compared to a deuteron. So, proton will have higher value of de-Broglie wavelength. de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda = h / p \Rightarrow p = h / \lambda{/tex} As, {tex}\lambda _ { d } < \lambda _ { p }{/tex} So, {tex}p _ { d } > p _ { p }{/tex} Thus, proton will have less momentum.
solution_html:
1. de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m V _ { 0 } q } } \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m } }{/tex} [as V0 and q are same, because proton and deuteron have been accelerated by same potential and have same charge].
    Since, mass of proton is less as compared to a deuteron. So, proton will have higher value of de-Broglie wavelength.
  2. de-Broglie wavelength is given by
    {tex}\lambda = h / p \Rightarrow p = h / \lambda{/tex}
    As, {tex}\lambda _ { d } < \lambda _ { p }{/tex}
    So, {tex}p _ { d } > p _ { p }{/tex}
    Thus, proton will have less momentum.

question_html:
Write Einstein's photoelectric equation and mention which important features in photoelectric effect can be explained with the help of this equation.
The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons gets doubled when the wavelength of light incident on the surface changes {tex}\lambda{/tex}1 from to {tex}\lambda{/tex}2. Derive the expressions for the threshold wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex}0 and work function for the metal surface.
question_text: Write Einstein's photoelectric equation and mention which important features in photoelectric effect can be explained with the help of this equation. The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons gets doubled when the wavelength of light incident on the surface changes {tex}\lambda{/tex} 1 from to {tex}\lambda{/tex} 2 . Derive the expressions for the threshold wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} 0 and work function for the metal surface.
solution_html:
Einstein's photoelectric equation
{tex}K _ { \max } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v ^ { 2 } = h \nu - \phi _ { 0 } = h \nu - h \nu _ { 0 }{/tex}........(i)
Important features are
a) The photoelectric effect will occur when the incident frequency is greater or equal to the threshold frequency for a given metal i.e {tex}\nu{/tex} ≥ {tex}\nu{/tex}0​.
b) When the incident frequency is greater than the threshold frequency, the maximum kinetic energy is proportional to {tex}\nu-\nu_0{/tex}.
c) The number of photon incident per unit time per unit area increases with the increase of intensity of incident light. This implies photo current is directly proportional to the intensity of radiation.
From Eq. (i),
{tex}K _ { \max } = \frac { h c } { \lambda _ { 1 } } - \phi _ { 0 }{/tex}
According to question,
{tex}K _ { \max } = \frac { h c } { \lambda _ { 1 } } - \phi _ { 0 }{/tex}........(i)
{tex}2 K _ { \max } = \frac { h c } { \lambda _ { 2 } } - \phi _ { 0 }{/tex}........(ii)
From Eqs. (ii) and (iii),
{tex}2 \left( \frac { h c } { \lambda _ { 1 } } - \phi _ { 0 } \right) = \frac { h c } { \lambda _ { 2 } } - \phi _ { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}\phi _ { 0 } = \frac { 2 h c } { \lambda _ { 1 } } - \frac { h c } { \lambda _ { 2 } } = h c \left( \frac { 2 } { \lambda _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
Also, {tex}\phi _ { 0 } = \frac { h c } { \lambda _ { 0 } }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { h c } { \lambda _ { 0 } } = h c \left( \frac { 2 } { \lambda _ { 1 } } - \frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { 2 } } \right){/tex}
or {tex}\frac { 1 } { \lambda _ { 0 } } = \frac { 2 \lambda _ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 1 } } { \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\lambda _ { 0 } = \frac { \lambda _ { 1 } \lambda _ { 2 } } { 2 \lambda _ { 2 } - \lambda _ { 1 } }{/tex}

question_html:
A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle have the same de Broglie wavelength. Determine the ratio of

  1. their accelerating potentials
  2. and  their speeds.
question_text: A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle have the same de Broglie wavelength. Determine the ratio of their accelerating potentials and their speeds.
explanation: de-broglie wavelength of the particle is given by, {tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mqV}}{/tex} where, V is the accelerating potential and v is the speed of the particle. Given that the de-broglie wavelength is the same for both proton and {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. Charge on {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, q {tex}\alpha{/tex} is 2 times charge on proton, q p Mass of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is 4 times mass of proton, m p According to the given question, {tex}\lambda_\alpha = \lambda_p{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}q_{\alpha}V_{\alpha}}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_pq_pV_p}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow m_{\alpha}q_{\alpha}V_{\alpha} = m_pq_pV_p{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{V_p}{V_{\alpha}} = \frac{m_{\alpha}q_{\alpha}}{m_pq_p} = \frac{4m_p}{m_p} \times \frac{2q_p}{q_p} = \frac{8}{1}{/tex} 8:1 this is the required ratio of the accelerating potential. Also, {tex}\lambda_a = \lambda_p{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{h}{m_{\alpha}v_{\alpha}} = \frac{h}{m_pv_p}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{v_p}{v_{\alpha}} = \frac{m_{\alpha}}{m_p} = \frac{4m_p}{m_p}= \frac{4}{1}{/tex} 4:1 this is the required ratio of the speed of proton to speed of alpha-particle.
solution: de-broglie wavelength of the particle is given by, {tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mqV}}{/tex} where, V is the accelerating potential and v is the speed of the particle. Given that the de-broglie wavelength is the same for both proton and {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle. Charge on {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, q {tex}\alpha{/tex} is 2 times charge on proton, q p Mass of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle is 4 times mass of proton, m p According to the given question, {tex}\lambda_\alpha = \lambda_p{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}q_{\alpha}V_{\alpha}}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_pq_pV_p}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow m_{\alpha}q_{\alpha}V_{\alpha} = m_pq_pV_p{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{V_p}{V_{\alpha}} = \frac{m_{\alpha}q_{\alpha}}{m_pq_p} = \frac{4m_p}{m_p} \times \frac{2q_p}{q_p} = \frac{8}{1}{/tex} 8:1 this is the required ratio of the accelerating potential. Also, {tex}\lambda_a = \lambda_p{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{h}{m_{\alpha}v_{\alpha}} = \frac{h}{m_pv_p}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{v_p}{v_{\alpha}} = \frac{m_{\alpha}}{m_p} = \frac{4m_p}{m_p}= \frac{4}{1}{/tex} 4:1 this is the required ratio of the speed of proton to speed of alpha-particle.
solution_html:
1. de-broglie wavelength of the particle is given by,
    {tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{p} = \frac{h}{mv} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2mqV}}{/tex}
    where, V is the accelerating potential and v is the speed of the particle.
    Given that the de-broglie wavelength is the same for both proton and {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle.
    Charge on {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle, q{tex}\alpha{/tex} is 2 times charge on proton, qp
    Mass of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle is 4 times mass of proton, mp
    According to the given question,
    {tex}\lambda_\alpha = \lambda_p{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_{\alpha}q_{\alpha}V_{\alpha}}} = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2m_pq_pV_p}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow m_{\alpha}q_{\alpha}V_{\alpha} = m_pq_pV_p{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{V_p}{V_{\alpha}} = \frac{m_{\alpha}q_{\alpha}}{m_pq_p} = \frac{4m_p}{m_p} \times \frac{2q_p}{q_p} = \frac{8}{1}{/tex}
    8:1 this is the required ratio of the accelerating potential.
  2. Also,
    {tex}\lambda_a = \lambda_p{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{h}{m_{\alpha}v_{\alpha}} = \frac{h}{m_pv_p}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{v_p}{v_{\alpha}} = \frac{m_{\alpha}}{m_p} = \frac{4m_p}{m_p}= \frac{4}{1}{/tex}
    4:1 this is the required ratio of the speed of proton to speed of alpha-particle.

question_html:
1. A deutron and an alpha particle are accelerated with the same accelerating potential. Which one of the two has:
    1. greater value of de-Broglie wavelength, associated with it, and
    2. less kinetic energy? Explain.
  2. A proton and a deuteron are accelerated through the same accelerating potential. Which one of the two has:
    1. greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it, and
    2. less momentum? Give reasons to justify your answer.
question_text: A deutron and an alpha particle are accelerated with the same accelerating potential. Which one of the two has: greater value of de-Broglie wavelength, associated with it, and less kinetic energy? Explain. A proton and a deuteron are accelerated through the same accelerating potential. Which one of the two has: greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it, and less momentum? Give reasons to justify your answer.
explanation: Using conservation of energy, change in electrostatic energy equals the kinetic energy gained. E = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = qV p = mv = {tex}\sqrt{2 m qV}{/tex} De-brogile wavelength is given by: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} Now, Charge on deuteron = 1e, Charge on alpha particle = 2e and m α = 2 m d For same accelerating potential V, {tex}\frac{\lambda_{d}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\frac{\sqrt{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{m_{d} q_{d}}}{/tex} = 2 Hence, wavelength of deuteron is more than the wavelength of the alpha particle. Kinetic energy, E = qV For same accelerating potential V, {tex}\frac{E_{d}}{E_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{\alpha}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, kinetic energy of deuteron is less than the kinetic energy of alpha particle. From de-Broglie equation, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex} As, {tex}p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} and K = qV {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m q V } }{/tex} ........(i) {tex}\lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m q } }{/tex} Ratio of wavelengths of electron and proton, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } \right) }{/tex} Ratio of mass of proton and electron, {tex}\frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } = 1836{/tex} (constant) {tex}\frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } = 1{/tex} (Both electron and proton have same charge) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { 1836 \times 1 }{/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { e } \approx 42.8 \lambda _ { p }{/tex} nearly Electron have a greater wavelength associated with it than that of proton. {tex}\because \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { p }{/tex} (de-Broglie equation) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \frac { h } { \lambda } \Rightarrow p \propto \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } }{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 } \Rightarrow \quad \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 }{/tex} Momentum of proton is nearly 42.8 times to that of the momentum of the electron. Thus, the electron will have less momentum.
solution: Using conservation of energy, change in electrostatic energy equals the kinetic energy gained. E = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = qV p = mv = {tex}\sqrt{2 m qV}{/tex} De-brogile wavelength is given by: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} Now, Charge on deuteron = 1e, Charge on alpha particle = 2e and m α = 2 m d For same accelerating potential V, {tex}\frac{\lambda_{d}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\frac{\sqrt{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{m_{d} q_{d}}}{/tex} = 2 Hence, wavelength of deuteron is more than the wavelength of the alpha particle. Kinetic energy, E = qV For same accelerating potential V, {tex}\frac{E_{d}}{E_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{\alpha}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, kinetic energy of deuteron is less than the kinetic energy of alpha particle. From de-Broglie equation, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex} As, {tex}p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} and K = qV {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m q V } }{/tex} ........(i) {tex}\lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m q } }{/tex} Ratio of wavelengths of electron and proton, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } \right) }{/tex} Ratio of mass of proton and electron, {tex}\frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } = 1836{/tex} (constant) {tex}\frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } = 1{/tex} (Both electron and proton have same charge) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { 1836 \times 1 }{/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { e } \approx 42.8 \lambda _ { p }{/tex} nearly Electron have a greater wavelength associated with it than that of proton. {tex}\because \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { p }{/tex} (de-Broglie equation) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \frac { h } { \lambda } \Rightarrow p \propto \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } }{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 } \Rightarrow \quad \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 }{/tex} Momentum of proton is nearly 42.8 times to that of the momentum of the electron. Thus, the electron will have less momentum.
solution_html:
1. Using conservation of energy, change in electrostatic energy equals the kinetic energy gained.
    E = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = qV
    p = mv = {tex}\sqrt{2 m qV}{/tex}
    1. De-brogile wavelength is given by: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
      {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex}
      Now, Charge on deuteron = 1e, Charge on alpha particle = 2e and mα = 2 md
      For same accelerating potential V,
      {tex}\frac{\lambda_{d}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\frac{\sqrt{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{m_{d} q_{d}}}{/tex} = 2
      Hence, wavelength of deuteron is more than the wavelength of the alpha particle.
    2. Kinetic energy, E = qV
      For same accelerating potential V,
      {tex}\frac{E_{d}}{E_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{\alpha}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
      Hence, kinetic energy of deuteron is less than the kinetic energy of alpha particle.
    1. From de-Broglie equation,
      {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex}
      As, {tex}p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} and K = qV
      {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m q V } }{/tex}........(i)
      {tex}\lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m q } }{/tex}
      Ratio of wavelengths of electron and proton,
      {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } \right) }{/tex}
      Ratio of mass of proton and electron,
      {tex}\frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } = 1836{/tex} (constant)
      {tex}\frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } = 1{/tex} (Both electron and proton have same charge)
      {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { 1836 \times 1 }{/tex}
      {tex}\lambda _ { e } \approx 42.8 \lambda _ { p }{/tex} nearly
      Electron have a greater wavelength associated with it than that of proton.
    2. {tex}\because \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { p }{/tex} (de-Broglie equation)
      {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \frac { h } { \lambda } \Rightarrow p \propto \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } }{/tex}
      Now,
      {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 } \Rightarrow \quad \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 }{/tex}
      Momentum of proton is nearly 42.8 times to that of the momentum of the electron. Thus, the electron will have less momentum.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
1. Write the important properties of photons which are used to establish Einstein's photoelectric equation.
  2. Use this equation to explain the concept of (a) threshold frequency and (b) stopping potential
question_text: Write the important properties of photons which are used to establish Einstein's photoelectric equation. Use this equation to explain the concept of (a) threshold frequency and (b) stopping potential
solution_html:
1. Important properties of photons are-
    1.  Photons are small packets of energy.
    2. Each photon has energy E (= h{tex}\nu{/tex} = hc/{tex}\lambda{/tex}) and momentum p(= E/c), where c is the speed of light, h is Planck's constant, {tex}\nu{/tex} and {tex}\lambda{/tex} are frequency and wavelength of radiation respectively.
  2. Since Einstein's photoelectric equation is given by {tex}\mathrm { KE } _ { \mathrm { max } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } m v _ { \max } ^ { 2 } = h \nu - \phi _ { 0 }{/tex}
    1. For a given material, there exists a certain minimum frequency of the incident radiation below, which no emission of photoelectron takes place. This frequency is called threshold frequency. Above threshold frequency, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photo electron or equivalent stopping potential is independent of the intensity of the incident light but depends only upon the frequency of the incident light.
    2. If the collecting plate in the photoelectric apparatus is made at a high negative potential, then most of the high energetic electrons get repelled back along the same path and the photoelectric current in the circuit becomes zero called cut-off or stopping potential.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
1. Describe briefly three experimentally observed features in the phenomenon of photoelectric effect.
  2. Discuss briefly how wave theory of light cannot explain these features.
question_text: Describe briefly three experimentally observed features in the phenomenon of photoelectric effect. Discuss briefly how wave theory of light cannot explain these features.
solution_html:
1. The three experimentally observed features in the phenomenon of photoelectric effect are -
    a) Threshold frequency: The photoelectric effect will occur when the incident frequency is greater or equal to the threshold frequency for a given metal i.e {tex}\nu{/tex} ≥ {tex}\nu{/tex}0​.
    b) The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectron: When the incident frequency is greater than the threshold frequency, the maximum kinetic energy is proportional to {tex}\nu-\nu_0{/tex}.
    c) No time lag: When energy of incident photon is greater than the work function, the photoelectron is immediately ejected. Thus, there is no time lag between the incidence of light and emission of photoelectron.
  2. We can not explain these by using wave theory of light because there are following reasons-
    a) The instantaneous ejection of photoelectrons.
    b) The existence of threshold frequency for a metal surface.
    c) The kinetic energy of photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of light and depends on its frequency.

question_html: Describe briefly the experimentally observed features in the phenomenon of photoelectric effect.
question_text: Describe briefly the experimentally observed features in the phenomenon of photoelectric effect.
explanation: For every metal there is a certain minimum frequency (threshold frequency) below which no photoelectrons are emitted, however high is the intensity of incident radiation. Photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of incident radiation. The photoelectric current becomes zero at a certain value of negative potential (stopping potential) applied at the anode. The value of stopping potential increases with the increase in the frequency of incident radiation. The maximum K.E. of photoelectrons is directly proportional to the frequency of incident radiation. The maximum K.E. of photoelectron is independent of the incident radiation. The photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process.
solution: For every metal there is a certain minimum frequency (threshold frequency) below which no photoelectrons are emitted, however high is the intensity of incident radiation. Photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of incident radiation. The photoelectric current becomes zero at a certain value of negative potential (stopping potential) applied at the anode. The value of stopping potential increases with the increase in the frequency of incident radiation. The maximum K.E. of photoelectrons is directly proportional to the frequency of incident radiation. The maximum K.E. of photoelectron is independent of the incident radiation. The photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process.
solution_html:
1. For every metal there is a certain minimum frequency (threshold frequency) below which no photoelectrons are emitted, however high is the intensity of incident radiation.
  2. Photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of incident radiation.
  3. The photoelectric current becomes zero at a certain value of negative potential (stopping potential) applied at the anode.
  4. The value of stopping potential increases with the increase in the frequency of incident radiation.
  5. The maximum K.E. of photoelectrons is directly proportional to the frequency of incident radiation.
  6. The maximum K.E. of photoelectron is independent of the incident radiation.
  7. The photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
A beam of monochromatic radiation is incident on a photosensitive surface. Answer the following questions giving reasons.

  1. Do the emitted photoelectrons have the same kinetic energy?
  2. Does the kinetic energy of the emitted electrons depend on the intensity of incident radiation?
  3. On what factors does the number of emitted photoelectrons depend?
question_text: A beam of monochromatic radiation is incident on a photosensitive surface. Answer the following questions giving reasons. Do the emitted photoelectrons have the same kinetic energy? Does the kinetic energy of the emitted electrons depend on the intensity of incident radiation? On what factors does the number of emitted photoelectrons depend?
explanation: Yes, all emitted photoelectrons have same kinetic energy as the kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons depends upon frequency of the incident radiation for a given photosensitive surface. No, the kinetic energy of the emitted electrons does not depend on the intensity of incident radiation. If the intensity of the incident light radiation is increased, then the number of incident photons falling per second on the metal surface also increases but the energy of each photon remains the same as the frequency of the radiation is not changing. The maximum kinetic energy of a photoelectron depends only on the frequency of incident radiation and not on the intensity of incident radiation. The number of emitted photoelectrons depends only on intensity of incident light.
solution: Yes, all emitted photoelectrons have same kinetic energy as the kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons depends upon frequency of the incident radiation for a given photosensitive surface. No, the kinetic energy of the emitted electrons does not depend on the intensity of incident radiation. If the intensity of the incident light radiation is increased, then the number of incident photons falling per second on the metal surface also increases but the energy of each photon remains the same as the frequency of the radiation is not changing. The maximum kinetic energy of a photoelectron depends only on the frequency of incident radiation and not on the intensity of incident radiation. The number of emitted photoelectrons depends only on intensity of incident light.
solution_html:
1. Yes, all emitted photoelectrons have same kinetic energy as the kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons depends upon frequency of the incident radiation for a given photosensitive surface.
  2. No, the kinetic energy of the emitted electrons does not depend on the intensity of incident radiation. If the intensity of the incident light radiation is increased, then the number of incident photons falling per second on the metal surface also increases but the energy of each photon remains the same as the frequency of the radiation is not changing. The maximum kinetic energy of a photoelectron depends only on the frequency of incident radiation and not on the intensity of incident radiation.
  3. The number of emitted photoelectrons depends only on intensity of incident light.
