question_html:
1. Write three observed features of photoelectric effect which cannot be explained by wave theory of light. Explain how Einstein's photoelectric equation is used to describe these features satisfactorily
  2. Figure shows a plot of stopping potential (V0) with frequency (v) of incident radiation for two photosensitive materials M1 and M2. Explain
    1. Why the slope of both the lines is same?
    2. For which material emitted electrons have greater kinetic energy for the same frequency of incident radiation?
question_text: Write three observed features of photoelectric effect which cannot be explained by wave theory of light. Explain how Einstein's photoelectric equation is used to describe these features satisfactorily Figure shows a plot of stopping potential (V 0 ) with frequency (v) of incident radiation for two photosensitive materials M 1 and M 2 . Explain Why the slope of both the lines is same? For which material emitted electrons have greater kinetic energy for the same frequency of incident radiation?
explanation: The three characteristic features which can't be explained by wave theory are: Kinetic energy of emitted electrons are found to be independent of intensity of incident light. Below a certain frequency (threshold) there is no photoemission. Spontaneous emission of photo-electrons. We have drawn a graph between stopping potential (V 0 ) and frequency (V). In that graph we have drawn two slopes equally M 1 and M 2 . The slope stopping potential (V 0 ) versus frequency (V) is equal to (h/e) the universal constant, so the slope is same for both lines. Thus it doesn’t depend on the material. The threshold frequency (V 0 ) is lesser for the slope M 1 , so that the kinetic energy is greater for M 1 in same frequency (V). Thus the kinetic energy is, K.E = hV - hV 0 . Emitted electrons are greater energy so, {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = hV 0 which has lower energy material M 1 . Thus the given two conditions are satisfied in the slope potential difference and frequency.
solution: The three characteristic features which can't be explained by wave theory are: Kinetic energy of emitted electrons are found to be independent of intensity of incident light. Below a certain frequency (threshold) there is no photoemission. Spontaneous emission of photo-electrons. We have drawn a graph between stopping potential (V 0 ) and frequency (V). In that graph we have drawn two slopes equally M 1 and M 2 . The slope stopping potential (V 0 ) versus frequency (V) is equal to (h/e) the universal constant, so the slope is same for both lines. Thus it doesn’t depend on the material. The threshold frequency (V 0 ) is lesser for the slope M 1 , so that the kinetic energy is greater for M 1 in same frequency (V). Thus the kinetic energy is, K.E = hV - hV 0 . Emitted electrons are greater energy so, {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = hV 0 which has lower energy material M 1 . Thus the given two conditions are satisfied in the slope potential difference and frequency.
solution_html:
1. The three characteristic features which can't be explained by wave theory are:
    1. Kinetic energy of emitted electrons are found to be independent of intensity of incident light.
    2. Below a certain frequency (threshold) there is no photoemission.
    3. Spontaneous emission of photo-electrons.
    1. We have drawn a graph between stopping potential (V0) and frequency (V). In that graph we have drawn two slopes equally M1 and M2. The slope stopping potential (V0) versus frequency (V) is equal to (h/e) the universal constant, so the slope is same for both lines. Thus it doesn’t depend on the material.
    2. The threshold frequency (V0) is lesser for the slope M1, so that the kinetic energy is greater for M1 in same frequency (V). Thus the kinetic energy is, K.E = hV - hV0. Emitted electrons are greater energy so, {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = hV0 which has lower energy material M1. Thus the given two conditions are satisfied in the slope potential difference and frequency.

question_html: A proton and an alpha particle are accelerated through the same potential. Which one of the two has (i) greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it, and (ii) less kinetic energy? Justify your answer.
question_text: A proton and an alpha particle are accelerated through the same potential. Which one of the two has (i) greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it, and (ii) less kinetic energy? Justify your answer.
explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with same potential V is {tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{p}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt m }}{/tex} As proton's mass is less than the mass of alpha particle thus, {tex}{\lambda _{proton}} > {\lambda _{alpha}}{/tex} As, {tex}K.E. = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow K.E.\propto \frac{1}{\lambda }{/tex} Since, {tex}{\lambda _{proton}} > {\lambda _{alpha}}{/tex} Thus, kinetic energy of proton will be lesser than that of alpha particle.
solution: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with same potential V is {tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{p}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \lambda \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt m }}{/tex} As proton's mass is less than the mass of alpha particle thus, {tex}{\lambda _{proton}} > {\lambda _{alpha}}{/tex} As, {tex}K.E. = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow K.E.\propto \frac{1}{\lambda }{/tex} Since, {tex}{\lambda _{proton}} > {\lambda _{alpha}}{/tex} Thus, kinetic energy of proton will be lesser than that of alpha particle.
solution_html:
1. The de-Broglie wavelength associated with same potential V is
    {tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{p}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda = \frac{h}{mv}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \lambda \propto \frac{1}{{\sqrt m }}{/tex}
    As proton's mass is less than the mass of alpha particle thus,
    {tex}{\lambda _{proton}} > {\lambda _{alpha}}{/tex}
     
  2. As, {tex}K.E. = \frac{{hc}}{\lambda }{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow K.E.\propto \frac{1}{\lambda }{/tex}
    Since, {tex}{\lambda _{proton}} > {\lambda _{alpha}}{/tex}
    Thus, kinetic energy of proton will be lesser than that of alpha particle.

question_html:
Two monochromatic radiations of frequencies {tex}\nu_1{/tex} and {tex}\nu_2{/tex} ({tex}\nu_1{/tex} > {tex}\nu_2{/tex} ) and having the same intensity are, in turn, incident on a photosensitive surface to cause photoelectric emission. Explain, giving reason, in which case

  1. more number of electrons will be emitted and
  2. the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons will be more.
solution_html:
1. Intensity of incident radiation is given by , I = nh{tex} u{/tex},
    For same intensity of two monochromatic radiations of frequencies {tex} u{/tex}1 and {tex} u{/tex}2,
    n1h{tex} u{/tex}1 = n2h{tex} u{/tex}2
    As, {tex} u{/tex}1 > {tex} u{/tex}2
    Thus, n2 > n1
    Therefore, the number of electrons emitted for monochromatic radiation of frequency {tex} u{/tex}2, will be more than that for the  radiation of frequency {tex} u{/tex}1.
  2.  As we know that , h{tex} u{/tex} = {tex}\phi_0{/tex} + KEmax ({tex}\phi_0{/tex} = work function of metal)
    Thus, Kmax increases with {tex} u{/tex}
    Thus, maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectron will be more for monochromatic light of frequency {tex} u{/tex}1 (as {tex} u{/tex}1 > {tex} u{/tex}2).

question_text: An electron is revolving around the nucleus with a constant speed of 2.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 8 m/s. Find the de-Broglie wavelength associated with it.
explanation: Given, v = 2.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 8 m/s, m = 9.1 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -31 kg, h = 6.63 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -34 Js de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac h{mv}{/tex} Substituting all values we get, {tex}\lambda = \frac { 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 2.2 \times 10 ^ { 8 } }{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 3.31 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
solution: Given, v = 2.2 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 8 m/s, m = 9.1 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -31 kg, h = 6.63 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -34 Js de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac h{mv}{/tex} Substituting all values we get, {tex}\lambda = \frac { 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 2.2 \times 10 ^ { 8 } }{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 3.31 \times 10 ^ { - 12 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}

de-Broglie wavelength is given by
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac h{mv}{/tex}

question_html: X-rays fall on a photosensitive surface to cause photoelectric emission. Assuming that the work-function of the surface can be neglected, find the relation between the de-Broglie wavelength ({tex}\lambda{/tex}) of the electrons emitted to the energy (E) of the incident photons. Draw the graph for {tex}\lambda{/tex} as function of E.
question_text: X-rays fall on a photosensitive surface to cause photoelectric emission. Assuming that the work-function of the surface can be neglected, find the relation between the de-Broglie wavelength ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} ) of the electrons emitted to the energy (E) of the incident photons. Draw the graph for {tex}\lambda{/tex} as function of E .
explanation: According to the Einstein and photoelectric equation, KE max = h {tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi{/tex} = E - {tex}\phi{/tex} Thus, E = KE max + {tex}\phi{/tex} Given {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 So, E = K max E = {tex}\frac{p^2}{2m}{/tex} Thus, p = {tex}\sqrt { 2 m E }{/tex} de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac hp{/tex} ..............(i) here h is the Planck constant Substituting the value of p, we get {tex}\lambda = \frac{h }{ \sqrt { 2 m E }}{/tex} The graph between wavelength and Energy is shown in the figure below.
solution: According to the Einstein and photoelectric equation, KE max = h {tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi{/tex} = E - {tex}\phi{/tex} Thus, E = KE max + {tex}\phi{/tex} Given {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0 So, E = K max E = {tex}\frac{p^2}{2m}{/tex} Thus, p = {tex}\sqrt { 2 m E }{/tex} de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac hp{/tex} ..............(i) here h is the Planck constant Substituting the value of p, we get {tex}\lambda = \frac{h }{ \sqrt { 2 m E }}{/tex} The graph between wavelength and Energy is shown in the figure below.
solution_html:
According to the Einstein and photoelectric equation,
KEmax = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi{/tex} = E - {tex}\phi{/tex}
Thus, E = KEmax + {tex}\phi{/tex}
Given {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0
So, E = Kmax
E = {tex}\frac{p^2}{2m}{/tex}
Thus, p = {tex}\sqrt { 2 m E }{/tex}
de-Broglie wavelength is given by
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac hp{/tex}  ..............(i) here h is the Planck constant 
Substituting the value of p, we get
{tex}\lambda = \frac{h }{ \sqrt { 2 m E }}{/tex}
The graph between wavelength and Energy is shown in the figure below.

question_html: A deuteron and an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle are accelerated with the same accelerating potential. Which one of the two has (i) greater value of de-Broglie wavelength, associated with it and (ii) less kinetic energy?
question_text: A deuteron and an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle are accelerated with the same accelerating potential. Which one of the two has (i) greater value of de-Broglie wavelength, associated with it and (ii) less kinetic energy?
explanation: Using conservation of energy, change in electrostatic energy equals the kinetic energy gained. E = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = qV p = mv = {tex}\sqrt{2 m qV}{/tex} De-brogile wavelength is given by: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} Now, Charge on deuteron = 1e, Charge on alpha particle = 2e, and m α = 2 m d For same accelerating potential V, {tex}\frac{\lambda_{d}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\frac{\sqrt{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{m_{d} q_{d}}}{/tex} = 2 Hence, wavelength of deuteron is more than the wavelength of the alpha particle. Kinetic energy, E = qV For same accelerating potential V, {tex}\frac{E_{d}}{E_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{\alpha}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, kinetic energy of deuteron is less than the kinetic energy of alpha particle.
solution: Using conservation of energy, change in electrostatic energy equals the kinetic energy gained. E = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = qV p = mv = {tex}\sqrt{2 m qV}{/tex} De-brogile wavelength is given by: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} Now, Charge on deuteron = 1e, Charge on alpha particle = 2e, and m α = 2 m d For same accelerating potential V, {tex}\frac{\lambda_{d}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\frac{\sqrt{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{m_{d} q_{d}}}{/tex} = 2 Hence, wavelength of deuteron is more than the wavelength of the alpha particle. Kinetic energy, E = qV For same accelerating potential V, {tex}\frac{E_{d}}{E_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{\alpha}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, kinetic energy of deuteron is less than the kinetic energy of alpha particle.
solution_html:
Using conservation of energy, change in electrostatic energy equals the kinetic energy gained.
E = {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}{/tex} = qV
p = mv = {tex}\sqrt{2 m qV}{/tex}

  1. De-brogile wavelength is given by: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex}
    Now, Charge on deuteron = 1e, Charge on alpha particle = 2e, 
    and mα = 2 md
    For same accelerating potential V,
    {tex}\frac{\lambda_{d}}{\lambda_{\alpha}}=\frac{\sqrt{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}}{\sqrt{m_{d} q_{d}}}{/tex} = 2
    Hence, wavelength of deuteron is more than the wavelength of the alpha particle.
  2. Kinetic energy, E = qV
    For same accelerating potential V,
    {tex}\frac{E_{d}}{E_{\alpha}}=\frac{q_{d}}{q_{\alpha}}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    Hence, kinetic energy of deuteron is less than the kinetic energy of alpha particle.

question_html: In the photoelectric effect, why should the photoelectric current increase as the intensity of the monochromatic radiation incident on a photosensitive surface is increased? Explain.
question_text: In the photoelectric effect, why should the photoelectric current increase as the intensity of the monochromatic radiation incident on a photosensitive surface is increased? Explain.
explanation: When the intensity of radiation incident on the surface increases, the number of photons per unit area unit time increases. Hence, the photo electrons ejected will be large, which in turn, will contribute to the increase in photoelectric current.
solution: When the intensity of radiation incident on the surface increases, the number of photons per unit area unit time increases. Hence, the photo electrons ejected will be large, which in turn, will contribute to the increase in photoelectric current.

question_text: Monochromatic light of frequency 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz is produced by a laser. The power emitted is 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 W. Estimate the number of photons emitted per second on an average by the source.
solution_html:
Here, frequency of light, {tex}\nu{/tex} = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz
Power emitted, P = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 W
If n is the number of photons emitted per second, then
P = nh{tex}\nu{/tex}
n = {tex}\frac{P}{h \nu}=\frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{6 \cdot 62 \times 10^{-34} \times 6 \cdot 0 \times 10^{14}}{/tex}
= 5.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015 s-1

The graph showing the variation of stopping potential with the frequency of incident radiation for two different material having work functions {tex}\omega_1{/tex} and {tex}\omega_2{/tex} is as shown in fig.

From Einstein's photoelectric equation, we have 
h{tex}\nu{/tex} = h{tex}\nu{/tex}0 + {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}m{tex}v^2_{max}{/tex}

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
1. Why photoelectric effect cannot be explained on the basis of wave nature of light? Give reasons.
  2. Write the basic features of photon picture of electromagnetic radiation on which Einstein's photoelectric equation is based.
question_text: Why photoelectric effect cannot be explained on the basis of wave nature of light? Give reasons. Write the basic features of photon picture of electromagnetic radiation on which Einstein's photoelectric equation is based.
solution_html:
1. The photoelectric effect cannot be explained on the basis of wave nature of light because wave nature of radiation cannot explain the following:
    1. The instantaneous ejection of photoelectrons.
    2. The existence of threshold frequency for a metal surface.
    3. The fact that kinetic energy of the emitted electrons is independent of the intensity of light and depends upon its frequency.
  2. Photon picture of electromagnetic radiation on which Einstein's photoelectric equation is based on particle nature of light Its basic features are given as below:
    1. In interaction of radiation with matter, radiation behaves as if it is made up of particles called photons.
    2. Each photon has energy E (= h{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac { h c } { \lambda }{/tex} ) and momentum p(= E/c).
    3. All photons of light of a particular frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} or wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} have the same energy E (= h{tex}\nu{/tex} = {tex}\frac { h c } { \lambda }{/tex} ) and momentum p (= E/c) whatever the intensity radiation may be.
    4. By increasing the intensity of light of given wavelength, there is only an increase in the number of photons per second crossing a given area with each photon having the same energy. Thus, photon energy is independent of the intensity of radiation.

question_html: Write Einstein's photoelectric equation and point out any two characteristic properties of photons on which this equation is based. Briefly explain three observed features which can be explained by this equation.
question_text: Write Einstein's photoelectric equation and point out any two characteristic properties of photons on which this equation is based. Briefly explain three observed features which can be explained by this equation.
solution_html:
Einstein's photoelectric equation is
eV0 = Kmax = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi_0{/tex}
Important features of this equation are

  1. Photoemission occurs when frequency of incident radiation is more than the threshold frequency,
    {tex}\nu{/tex}o = {tex}\frac{\phi_0}{h}{/tex}
  2. Energy of emitted photoelectron is proportional to energy of incident photon.

Three salient features observed in photoelectric effect and their explanation on the basis of Einstein's photoelectric equation is given as below:

  1. Threshold frequency: For KEmax {tex}≥{/tex} 0,
     ⇒ {tex}\nu{/tex} {tex}≥{/tex} {tex}\nu{/tex}0 
    i.e. the phenomenon of photoelectric effect takes place when incident frequency is greater or equal to a minimum frequency (threshold frequency) {tex}\nu{/tex}0 fixed for given metal.
  2. KEmax of photoelectron: When incident frequency is greater than threshold frequency, then KEmax of photo-electron is directly proportional to ({tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\nu{/tex}0) as
    KEmax = h({tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\nu{/tex}0)
    ⇒ KEmax {tex}\propto{/tex} ({tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\nu{/tex}0)
  3. Effect of intensity of incident light: The number of photons incident per unit time per unit area increases with the increase of intensity of incident light. More number of photons facilitates ejection of more number of photo-electrons from metal surface leads to further increase of photocurrent till its saturation value is reached.

question_html: A monochromatic source emitting light of wavelength 600 nm has a power output of 66 W. Calculate the number of photons emitted by this source in 2 minutes.
question_text: A monochromatic source emitting light of wavelength 600 nm has a power output of 66 W. Calculate the number of photons emitted by this source in 2 minutes.
explanation: The energy of one photon {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6 \times 10^{-7}}{/tex} = 3.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of photons emitted by the source in 1 s, n = {tex}\frac{P}{E}{/tex} , or n = {tex}\frac{66}{3.3 \times 10^{-19}}=2 \times 10^{20}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total number of photons emitted by source in 2 minutes N = n {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 120 = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 photons
solution: The energy of one photon {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6 \times 10^{-7}}{/tex} = 3.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 J {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of photons emitted by the source in 1 s, n = {tex}\frac{P}{E}{/tex} , or n = {tex}\frac{66}{3.3 \times 10^{-19}}=2 \times 10^{20}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Total number of photons emitted by source in 2 minutes N = n {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 120 = 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 22 photons
solution_html:
The energy of one photon 
{tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}E=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6 \times 10^{-7}}{/tex} = 3.3 {tex}\times{/tex}10-19 J
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of photons emitted by the source in 1 s,
n = {tex}\frac{P}{E}{/tex}, or n = {tex}\frac{66}{3.3 \times 10^{-19}}=2 \times 10^{20}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total number of photons emitted by source in 2 minutes
N = n {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 60
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1020 {tex}\times{/tex} 120
= 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1022 photons

State clearly how this graph can be used to determine the (i) Planck's constant and (ii) work function of the material?
solution_html:
The two characteristic features observed in photoelectric effect which support the photon pictures of electromagnetic radiation are as follows:

  1. All photons of light of a particular frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} or wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} have the same energy.
    E (= h{tex}\nu{/tex} {tex}= \frac { h c } { \lambda }{/tex}) and momentum, p ( = E/c)
  2. Photons are electrically neutral and not deflected by electric and magnetic fields.

  1. Planck constant is given by the slope of the curve,
    i.e. Slope of graph = {tex}\frac{h}{e}{/tex}
  2. Work function is the minimum energy required by the electron to escape out of the metal surface thus,
    {tex}\phi = h \nu _ { 0 }{/tex}
    Here, {tex}\nu{/tex}0 is the threshold frequency.

question_text: Define the terms (i) cut-off voltage and (ii) threshold frequency in relation to the phenomenon of photoelectric effect. Using Einstein's photoelectric equation show how the cut-off voltage and threshold frequency for a given photosensitive material can be determined with the help of a suitable plot/graph.
solution_html:
1. Cut-off voltage: For a particular frequency of incident radiation, the minimum negative (retarding) potential V0 for which the photocurrent stops or becomes zero is called the cut-off or stopping potential.
  2. Threshold frequency: For a given material, there exists a certain minimum frequency of the incident radiation below which no emission of photo- electrons take place. This frequency is called the threshold frequency.

The variation of cut-off potential with frequency of incident radiation is shown as below.

As per Einstein's photoelectric equation,
eV0 = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - h{tex}\nu{/tex}0
V0 = {tex}\frac { h } { e } \left( \nu - \nu _ { 0 } \right){/tex}
Hence the intercept, on the Y - axis, gives {tex}\nu{/tex}0. One can read V0, for any {tex}\nu{/tex}, from the graph.

solution_html:
Kinetic energy of photoelectrons emitted from the surface of a photosensitive material,
KEmax = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi{/tex}o
A graph is shown between the frequency of incident radiation ({tex}\nu{/tex}) and the maximum kinetic energy of the electrons emitted from the surface of a photosensitive material.

  1. So the graph between Kmax and {tex}\nu{/tex} is a straight line as shown in Figure. From this graph, the Planck constant can be calculated by the slope of the current. 
    Slope of Kmax−{tex}\nu{/tex} graph = {tex}\frac{\triangle K_{max}}{\triangle\nu}{/tex} = h
     
  2. Work function is the minimum energy required to eject the photo-electron from the metal surface,
    {tex}\phi{/tex}o = h{tex}\nu{/tex}0 , where {tex}\nu{/tex}0 = Threshold frequency
    Now, Intercept on the negative Kmax axis = {tex}\phi{/tex}0
    Thus, Intercept on the negative Kmax axis gives the value of work function.

Write Einstein's photoelectric equation. Explain the terms

  2. stopping potential.
question_text: Write Einstein's photoelectric equation. Explain the terms threshold frequency and stopping potential.
solution_html:
1. Einstein's photoelectric equation, 
    K.E. of photoelectron = Incident energy of photons - Work function
    or, K.E. = h{tex}\nu{/tex}- {tex}\phi_{0}{/tex}
    or, K.E. = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - h{tex}\nu_0{/tex}
    where {tex}\nu_0{/tex} is called threshold frequency.
    Threshold Frequency: For a given metal, there exists a certain minimum frequency of the incident radiation below which no emission of photoelectrons takes place. This frequency is called the threshold frequency . Or Threshold frequency is the minimum frequency of the incident light which can cause the ejection of electrons without giving them additional energy.  It is denoted by {tex}\nu _0{/tex}.
  2. Stopping Potential: It is that minimum negative potential given to anode in a photocell for which the photoelectric current becomes zero. It is denoted by {tex}V_0{/tex} It is independent of the intensity of the incident light.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html: What is the photo-electric effect? State the laws of photo-electric emission.
question_text: What is the photo-electric effect? State the laws of photo-electric emission.
explanation: Photoelectric effect- When the radiation such as {tex}\lambda{/tex} -rays, X-rays, UV and even visible light fall on metals, electrons are emitted. This, phenomenon of emission of electrons is known as photoelectric effect. Laws of Photoelectric Emission: The experimental observations on the photoelectric effect may be summarized as follows, which are known as the fundamental laws of photoelectric emission. For a given photo-sensitive material, there is a minimum frequency called the threshold frequency, below which the emission of photoelectrons stops completely, however great the intensity may be. For a given photosensitive material, the photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of the incident radiation, provided the frequency is greater than the threshold frequency. The photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process i.e., there is no time lag, between the incidence of radiation and the emission of photoelectrons. The maximum kinetic energy of the photo electrons is directly proportional to the frequency of incident radiation, but is independent of its intensity.
solution: Photoelectric effect- When the radiation such as {tex}\lambda{/tex} -rays, X-rays, UV and even visible light fall on metals, electrons are emitted. This, phenomenon of emission of electrons is known as photoelectric effect. Laws of Photoelectric Emission: The experimental observations on the photoelectric effect may be summarized as follows, which are known as the fundamental laws of photoelectric emission. For a given photo-sensitive material, there is a minimum frequency called the threshold frequency, below which the emission of photoelectrons stops completely, however great the intensity may be. For a given photosensitive material, the photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of the incident radiation, provided the frequency is greater than the threshold frequency. The photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process i.e., there is no time lag, between the incidence of radiation and the emission of photoelectrons. The maximum kinetic energy of the photo electrons is directly proportional to the frequency of incident radiation, but is independent of its intensity.
solution_html:
Photoelectric effect- When the radiation such as {tex}\lambda{/tex}-rays, X-rays, UV and even visible light fall on metals, electrons are emitted. This, phenomenon of emission of electrons is known as photoelectric effect.

Laws of Photoelectric Emission:

The experimental observations on the photoelectric effect may be summarized as follows, which are known as the fundamental laws of photoelectric emission.

  1. For a given photo-sensitive material, there is a minimum frequency called the threshold frequency, below which the emission of photoelectrons stops completely, however great the intensity may be.
  2. For a given photosensitive material, the photoelectric current is directly proportional to the intensity of the incident radiation, provided the frequency is greater than the threshold frequency.
  3. The photoelectric emission is an instantaneous process i.e., there is no time lag, between the incidence of radiation and the emission of photoelectrons.
  4. The maximum kinetic energy of the photo electrons is directly proportional to the frequency of incident radiation, but is independent of its intensity.

question_html: Write the relationship of de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} associated with a particle of mass m in terms of its kinetic energy K.
question_text: Write the relationship of de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} associated with a particle of mass m in terms of its kinetic energy K .
explanation: Relation between Kinetic energy and linear momentum is, {tex}K = \frac { p ^ { 2 } } { 2 m }{/tex} where p = momentum, K = kinetic energy, m = mass So, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} de-Broglie wavelength, where, p = {tex} \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m E } } \quad [ as\;K = E ]{/tex}
solution: Relation between Kinetic energy and linear momentum is, {tex}K = \frac { p ^ { 2 } } { 2 m }{/tex} where p = momentum, K = kinetic energy, m = mass So, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} de-Broglie wavelength, where, p = {tex} \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m E } } \quad [ as\;K = E ]{/tex}
solution_html:
Relation between Kinetic energy and linear momentum is, {tex}K = \frac { p ^ { 2 } } { 2 m }{/tex}
where p = momentum, K = kinetic energy, m = mass
So,
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex}
de-Broglie wavelength, where, p ={tex} \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m E } } \quad [ as\;K = E ]{/tex}

question_text: For a given photosensitive material and with a source of constant frequency of incident radiation, how does the photocurrent vary with the intensity of incident light?
explanation: The intensity of light mainly depends on the number of photons for the given frequency of incident radiation. So the photoelectric current increases with the intensity of incident light.
solution: The intensity of light mainly depends on the number of photons for the given frequency of incident radiation. So the photoelectric current increases with the intensity of incident light.

question_html: A particle is moving three times as fast as an electron. The ratio of the de Broglie wavelength of the particle to that of the electron is 8. 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-4. Calculate the particle's mass and identify the particle.
question_text: A particle is moving three times as fast as an electron. The ratio of the de Broglie wavelength of the particle to that of the electron is 8. 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -4 . Calculate the particle's mass and identify the particle.
explanation: Given: v 1 = 3v e According to the de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { m v }{/tex} So, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { 1 } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { m _ { e } v _ { e } } { m _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } } = 8.3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad m _ { 1 } = \frac { m _ { e } v _ { e } } { 8.3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \left( 3 v _ { e } \right) } \quad \left( \because v _ { 1 } = 3 v _ { e } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } } { 8.3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times 3 } = 0.36 \times 10 ^ { - 27 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex} Thus, the particle may be proton or neutron.
solution: Given: v 1 = 3v e According to the de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { m v }{/tex} So, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { 1 } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { m _ { e } v _ { e } } { m _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } } = 8.3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad m _ { 1 } = \frac { m _ { e } v _ { e } } { 8.3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \left( 3 v _ { e } \right) } \quad \left( \because v _ { 1 } = 3 v _ { e } \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac { 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } } { 8.3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times 3 } = 0.36 \times 10 ^ { - 27 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex} Thus, the particle may be proton or neutron.
solution_html:
Given: v1 = 3ve
According to the de-Broglie wavelength,
{tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { m v }{/tex}
So,
{tex}\frac { \lambda _ { 1 } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { m _ { e } v _ { e } } { m _ { 1 } v _ { 1 } } = 8.3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad m _ { 1 } = \frac { m _ { e } v _ { e } } { 8.3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \left( 3 v _ { e } \right) } \quad \left( \because v _ { 1 } = 3 v _ { e } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } } { 8.3 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \times 3 } = 0.36 \times 10 ^ { - 27 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex}
Thus, the particle may be proton or neutron.

question_html: Light of wavelength 2000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} falls on a metal surface of work function 4.2 eV. What is the kinetic energy (in eV) of the fastest electrons emitted from the surface?
question_text: Light of wavelength 2000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} falls on a metal surface of work function 4.2 eV. What is the kinetic energy (in eV) of the fastest electrons emitted from the surface?
explanation: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} = 2000 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -10 m {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = 4.2 eV, h = 6.63 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -34 Js Now, {tex} K.E. = \frac { h c } { \lambda } - \phi _ { 0 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \left( 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \right) \times \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) } { \left( 2000 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \right) } \times \frac { 1 } { 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } \mathrm { eV } - 4.2 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} = (6.2 - 4.2) eV = 2.0 eV
solution: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} = 2000 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -10 m {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = 4.2 eV, h = 6.63 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -34 Js Now, {tex} K.E. = \frac { h c } { \lambda } - \phi _ { 0 }{/tex} {tex}= \frac { \left( 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \right) \times \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) } { \left( 2000 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \right) } \times \frac { 1 } { 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } \mathrm { eV } - 4.2 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} = (6.2 - 4.2) eV = 2.0 eV
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2000 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} = 2000 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-10 m
{tex}\phi_0{/tex} = 4.2 eV, h = 6.63 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-34 Js
Now,
{tex} K.E. = \frac { h c } { \lambda } - \phi _ { 0 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \left( 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \right) \times \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) } { \left( 2000 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \right) } \times \frac { 1 } { 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } \mathrm { eV } - 4.2 \mathrm { eV }{/tex}
= (6.2 - 4.2) eV = 2.0 eV

question_html:
1. Ultraviolet light of wavelength 2271 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} from a 100 W mercury source is incident on a photocell made of molybdenum metal. If the stopping potential is 1.3 V, estimate the work function of the metal.
  2. How would the photocell respond to high intensity (105 W/m2) red light of wavelength 6328 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} produced by a He-Ne laser?
question_text: Ultraviolet light of wavelength 2271 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} from a 100 W mercury source is incident on a photocell made of molybdenum metal. If the stopping potential is 1.3 V, estimate the work function of the metal. How would the photocell respond to high intensity (10 5 W/m 2 ) red light of wavelength 6328 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} produced by a He-Ne laser?
solution_html:
1. Einstein's photoelectric equation is given by 
    KEmax= h{tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi_o{/tex}.......(i)
    But, KEmax = eV0
    Here, h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34J-s, V0  = 1.3 V
    {tex}\lambda = 2271 \mathop A\limits^o = 2271 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
    {tex}\nu{/tex} {tex} = \frac { c } { \lambda } = \frac { 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } } { 2271 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } } = 1.32 \times 10 ^ { 15 } \mathrm { Hz }{/tex}
    eVo = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 {tex}\times{/tex}1.3 = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19J
    Work function, {tex}\phi_0{/tex} = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - eV0
    = (6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34) {tex}\times{/tex} (1.32 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015) - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19
    {tex}= \frac { 6.76 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } { 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } \mathrm { eV }{/tex} = 4.22 eV
  2. {tex}\lambda = 6328 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
    KEmax = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi{/tex}......(i)
    Here, h{tex}\nu{/tex} {tex} = \frac { h c } { \lambda } = 3.14 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \mathrm { J } = 1.96 \mathrm { eV }{/tex}
    But, {tex}\phi = 4.22 \text { eV i.e. } {/tex}h{tex}\nu{/tex} {tex}< \phi{/tex}
    Therefore, KEmax < 0 [From Eq(i)]
    which is not possible. Hence, the photocell will not respond to the red light produced by the laser.

question_html:
1. Determine the de-Broglie wavelength of a proton whose kinetic energy is equal to the rest mass energy of an electron. Mass of a proton 1836 times that of electron.
  2. In which region of electromagnetic spectrum does this wavelength lie?
question_text: Determine the de-Broglie wavelength of a proton whose kinetic energy is equal to the rest mass energy of an electron. Mass of a proton 1836 times that of electron. In which region of electromagnetic spectrum does this wavelength lie?
explanation: de-Broglie matter wave equation is given by {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex} where, m = mass of proton K = kinetic energy of proton. According to the question, kinetic energy of proton, K = m e c 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m \left( m _ { e } c ^ { 2 } \right) } }{/tex} {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 } c \sqrt { m m _ { e } } } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 c } \times \left( m _ { e } \right) \sqrt { 1836 } }{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} m p = 1836 m e ) {tex}\lambda = \frac { 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 1.414 \times \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 42.8 }{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 4 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \;\mathrm { m }{/tex} This region of electromagnetic spectrum is X-ray.
solution: de-Broglie matter wave equation is given by {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex} where, m = mass of proton K = kinetic energy of proton. According to the question, kinetic energy of proton, K = m e c 2 {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m \left( m _ { e } c ^ { 2 } \right) } }{/tex} {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 } c \sqrt { m m _ { e } } } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 c } \times \left( m _ { e } \right) \sqrt { 1836 } }{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} m p = 1836 m e ) {tex}\lambda = \frac { 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 1.414 \times \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 42.8 }{/tex} {tex}\lambda = 4 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \;\mathrm { m }{/tex} This region of electromagnetic spectrum is X-ray.
solution_html:
1. de-Broglie matter wave equation is given by
    {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex}
    where, m = mass of proton
    K = kinetic energy of proton.
    According to the question, kinetic energy of proton, K = mec2
    {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m \left( m _ { e } c ^ { 2 } \right) } }{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 } c \sqrt { m m _ { e } } } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 c } \times \left( m _ { e } \right) \sqrt { 1836 } }{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} mp = 1836 me)
    {tex}\lambda = \frac { 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 1.414 \times \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 42.8 }{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda = 4 \times 10 ^ { - 14 } \;\mathrm { m }{/tex}
  2. This region of electromagnetic spectrum is X-ray.

question_text: A particle is moving three times as fast as an electron. The ratio of the de-Broglie wavelength of the particle to that of the electron is 1.813 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -4 . Calculate the particle's mass and identify the particle. An electron and a proton have the same kinetic energy. Which of the two will have larger de-Broglie wavelength? Give reason.
explanation: According to the question , Given, {tex}v _ { \text { particle } } = 3 v _ { \text {electron } }{/tex} .......(i) {tex}\lambda _ { \text { particle } } = 1.813 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \lambda _ { \text { electron } }{/tex} As, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { m v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { m _ { \text { particle } } } { m _ { \text { electron } } } = \frac { \lambda _ { \text { electron } } \times v _ { \text { electron } } } { \lambda _ { \text { particle } } \times v _ { \text { particle } } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad m _ { \text { particle } } = 1839 \mathrm { m } _ { \text {electron } }{/tex} {tex}m _ { \text { particle } } = 1839 \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 }{/tex} {tex}= 1.673 \times 10 ^ { - 27 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex} Particle is either a proton or a neutron. Now, {tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} Therefore, Kinetic Energy of electron is more than that of proton or neutron.
solution: According to the question , Given, {tex}v _ { \text { particle } } = 3 v _ { \text {electron } }{/tex} .......(i) {tex}\lambda _ { \text { particle } } = 1.813 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \lambda _ { \text { electron } }{/tex} As, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { m v }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { m _ { \text { particle } } } { m _ { \text { electron } } } = \frac { \lambda _ { \text { electron } } \times v _ { \text { electron } } } { \lambda _ { \text { particle } } \times v _ { \text { particle } } }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad m _ { \text { particle } } = 1839 \mathrm { m } _ { \text {electron } }{/tex} {tex}m _ { \text { particle } } = 1839 \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 }{/tex} {tex}= 1.673 \times 10 ^ { - 27 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex} Particle is either a proton or a neutron. Now, {tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} Therefore, Kinetic Energy of electron is more than that of proton or neutron.
solution_html:
According to the question ,
Given, {tex}v _ { \text { particle } } = 3 v _ { \text {electron } }{/tex} .......(i)
{tex}\lambda _ { \text { particle } } = 1.813 \times 10 ^ { - 4 } \lambda _ { \text { electron } }{/tex}

  1. As, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { m v }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac { m _ { \text { particle } } } { m _ { \text { electron } } } = \frac { \lambda _ { \text { electron } } \times v _ { \text { electron } } } { \lambda _ { \text { particle } } \times v _ { \text { particle } } }{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad m _ { \text { particle } } = 1839 \mathrm { m } _ { \text {electron } }{/tex}
    {tex}m _ { \text { particle } } = 1839 \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 }{/tex}{tex}= 1.673 \times 10 ^ { - 27 } \mathrm { kg }{/tex}
    Particle is either a proton or a neutron.
  2. Now, {tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex}
    Therefore, Kinetic Energy of electron is more than that of proton or neutron.

An electron and a photon each have a wavelength 1nm. Find

  1. their momenta
  2. the energy of the photon
  3. the kinetic energy of electron
question_text: An electron and a photon each have a wavelength 1nm. Find their momenta the energy of the photon the kinetic energy of electron
explanation: For electron and photon, momentum, {tex}p = \frac { h } { \lambda } = \frac { 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 10 ^ { - 9 } } = 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 25 }\; m{/tex} We know that, energy of photon is given by , {tex}E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } = \frac { \left( 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \right) \times \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) } { 10 ^ { - 9 } \times \left( 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \right) } = 1243 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} As, kinetic energy is given by , {tex}\begin{aligned} E & = \frac { p ^ { 2 } } { 2 m } = \frac { \left( 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 25 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \times 9 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } = 1.52 \mathrm { ev } \end{aligned}{/tex}
solution: For electron and photon, momentum, {tex}p = \frac { h } { \lambda } = \frac { 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 10 ^ { - 9 } } = 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 25 }\; m{/tex} We know that, energy of photon is given by , {tex}E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } = \frac { \left( 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \right) \times \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) } { 10 ^ { - 9 } \times \left( 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \right) } = 1243 \mathrm { eV }{/tex} As, kinetic energy is given by , {tex}\begin{aligned} E & = \frac { p ^ { 2 } } { 2 m } = \frac { \left( 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 25 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \times 9 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } = 1.52 \mathrm { ev } \end{aligned}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. For electron and photon,
    momentum, {tex}p = \frac { h } { \lambda } = \frac { 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 10 ^ { - 9 } } = 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 25 }\; m{/tex}
  2. We know that, energy of photon is given by ,
    {tex}E = \frac { h c } { \lambda } = \frac { \left( 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } \right) \times \left( 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \right) } { 10 ^ { - 9 } \times \left( 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \right) } = 1243 \mathrm { eV }{/tex}
  3. As, kinetic energy is given by , {tex}\begin{aligned} E & = \frac { p ^ { 2 } } { 2 m } = \frac { \left( 6.63 \times 10 ^ { - 25 } \right) ^ { 2 } } { 2 \times 9 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } } = 1.52 \mathrm { ev } \end{aligned}{/tex}

question_text: An electron and a photon each have a wavelength of 2 nm. Find their momenta, the energy of the photon and the kinetic energy of the electron.
explanation: Here, wavelength of the electron and the photon, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2 nm = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m Momentum of the electron and the photon, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{6 \cdot 62 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 3.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 kg ms -1 Energy of the photon, {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6 \cdot 62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 9.93 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 J Kinetic energy of the electron, {tex}E_{k}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{\left(3 \cdot 31 \times 10^{-25}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 9 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-31}}{/tex} = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -20 J
solution: Here, wavelength of the electron and the photon, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2 nm = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m Momentum of the electron and the photon, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{6 \cdot 62 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 3.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 kg ms -1 Energy of the photon, {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6 \cdot 62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 9.93 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -17 J Kinetic energy of the electron, {tex}E_{k}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{\left(3 \cdot 31 \times 10^{-25}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 9 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-31}}{/tex} = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -20 J
solution_html:
Here, wavelength of the electron and the photon,
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 2 nm = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m

  1. Momentum of the electron and the photon,
    {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}=\frac{6 \cdot 62 \times 10^{-34}}{2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 3.31 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-25 kg ms-1 
  2. Energy of the photon,
    {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6 \cdot 62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{-9}}{/tex} = 9.93 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-17 J
  3. Kinetic energy of the electron,
    {tex}E_{k}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{\left(3 \cdot 31 \times 10^{-25}\right)^{2}}{2 \times 9 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-31}}{/tex} = 6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-20 J

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html: Write Einstein's photoelectric equation. State clearly the three salient features observed in photoelectric effect which can be explained on the basis of above equation.
question_text: Write Einstein's photoelectric equation. State clearly the three salient features observed in photoelectric effect which can be explained on the basis of above equation.
solution_html:
Einstein's photoelectric equation is given as,
(K.E.)max = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - h{tex}\nu{/tex}0.
The three salient features which can be explained on the basis this equation are as follows:

  1. If {tex}\nu{/tex}0 < {tex}\nu{/tex}, K.E.max is negative since K.E. can never be negative, {tex}\nu{/tex}0 can never be less than {tex}\nu{/tex} during photoelectric emission. This brings forward the concept of threshold frequency.
  2. There is a linear dependency of K.Emax and {tex}\nu{/tex}.
  3. K.Emax is independent of the intensity of incident radiation.

question_html:
​​​​​​Find the ratio of de-Broglie wavelengths associated with  protons, accelerated through a potential of 128 V and

{tex}\alpha{/tex}-particles, accelerated through a potential of 64 V.
question_text: ​​​​​​Find the ratio of de-Broglie wavelengths associated with protons, accelerated through a potential of 128 V and {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particles, accelerated through a potential of 64 V.
explanation: de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 m K } = h / \sqrt { 2 m q V }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \propto 1 / \sqrt { m q V }{/tex} Here, {tex}\frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } = 4 , \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } = 2{/tex} , {tex}\frac { V _ { a } } { V _ { p } } = \frac { 64 } { 128 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = \sqrt { 4 \times 2 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } } = 2 \text { or } \lambda _ { p } : \lambda _ { \alpha } = 2 : 1{/tex} Hence the ratio of their wavelength is 2:1
solution: de-Broglie wavelength is given by {tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 m K } = h / \sqrt { 2 m q V }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \propto 1 / \sqrt { m q V }{/tex} Here, {tex}\frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } = 4 , \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } = 2{/tex} , {tex}\frac { V _ { a } } { V _ { p } } = \frac { 64 } { 128 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = \sqrt { 4 \times 2 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } } = 2 \text { or } \lambda _ { p } : \lambda _ { \alpha } = 2 : 1{/tex} Hence the ratio of their wavelength is 2:1
solution_html:
de-Broglie wavelength is given by
{tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 m K } = h / \sqrt { 2 m q V }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \propto 1 / \sqrt { m q V }{/tex}
Here, {tex}\frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } = 4 , \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } = 2{/tex},
{tex}\frac { V _ { a } } { V _ { p } } = \frac { 64 } { 128 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = \sqrt { 4 \times 2 \times \frac { 1 } { 2 } } = 2 \text { or } \lambda _ { p } : \lambda _ { \alpha } = 2 : 1{/tex}

Hence the ratio of their wavelength is 2:1

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html: Define the terms threshold frequency and stopping potential in the study of photoelectric emission. Explain briefly the reasons why wave theory of light is not able to explain the observed features in photoelectric effect?
question_text: Define the terms threshold frequency and stopping potential in the study of photoelectric emission. Explain briefly the reasons why wave theory of light is not able to explain the observed features in photoelectric effect?
explanation: The minimum frequency below which there is no occurrence of photoelectric effect is called the cut-off frequency or threshold frequency. The stopping potential is defined as the potential necessary to stop any electron (or, in other words, to stop even the electron with the most kinetic energy) from reaching the other side. The wave theory of light is not able to explain the observed features of photoelectric current because of following reasons: The greater energy incident per unit time per unit area increases with the increase of intensity which should facilitate liberation of photoelectron of greater kinetic energy which is in contradiction of observed feature of photoelectric effect. Wave theory states that energy carried by wave is independent of frequency of light wave and hence wave of high intensity and low frequency should stimulate photoelectric emission but practically, it does not happen.
solution: The minimum frequency below which there is no occurrence of photoelectric effect is called the cut-off frequency or threshold frequency. The stopping potential is defined as the potential necessary to stop any electron (or, in other words, to stop even the electron with the most kinetic energy) from reaching the other side. The wave theory of light is not able to explain the observed features of photoelectric current because of following reasons: The greater energy incident per unit time per unit area increases with the increase of intensity which should facilitate liberation of photoelectron of greater kinetic energy which is in contradiction of observed feature of photoelectric effect. Wave theory states that energy carried by wave is independent of frequency of light wave and hence wave of high intensity and low frequency should stimulate photoelectric emission but practically, it does not happen.
solution_html:
The minimum frequency below which there is no occurrence of photoelectric effect is called the cut-off frequency or threshold frequency.
The stopping potential is defined as the potential necessary to stop any electron (or, in other words, to stop even the electron with the most kinetic energy) from reaching the other side.
The wave theory of light is not able to explain the observed features of photoelectric current because of following reasons:

  1. The greater energy incident per unit time per unit area increases with the increase of intensity which should facilitate liberation of photoelectron of greater kinetic energy which is in contradiction of observed feature of photoelectric effect.
  2. Wave theory states that energy carried by wave is independent of frequency of light wave and hence wave of high intensity and low frequency should stimulate photoelectric emission but practically, it does not happen.

As, KE max = h {tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} [Einstein's photoelectric current] The KE max of violet radiation will be more.

solution_html:
The intensities for both the monochromatic radiation are same but their frequencies are different. Thus,

  2. As, KEmax = h{tex}\nu{/tex} - {tex}\phi_0{/tex} [Einstein's photoelectric current]
    The KEmax of violet radiation will be more.

question_html:
Sketch the graphs showing the variation of stopping potential with frequency of incident radiations for two photosensitive materials A and B having threshold frequencies {tex}{\nu _0}' > {\nu _0}{/tex} respectively:

  1. Which of the two metals, A or B has higher work function?
  2. What information do you get from the slope of the graphs?
  3. What does the value of intercept of graph 'A' on the potential axis represent?
question_text: Sketch the graphs showing the variation of stopping potential with frequency of incident radiations for two photosensitive materials A and B having threshold frequencies {tex}{\nu _0}' > {\nu _0}{/tex} respectively: Which of the two metals, A or B has higher work function? What information do you get from the slope of the graphs? What does the value of intercept of graph 'A' on the potential axis represent?
solution_html:
1. Since work function, {tex}\phi = h\nu {/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {\phi _0}' = h{\nu _0}'{/tex}
    Metal 'A' has higher work function as {tex}{\nu _0}' > {\nu _0}{/tex}
  2. We know that,
    Kmax = {tex}h\nu-\phi_o{/tex} = eVo
    Dividing the whole equation by e, we get,
    {tex}\frac{h\nu}e-\frac{\phi_o}e=V_o{/tex}
    From the above equation, the slope of the graph is {tex}\frac he{/tex} (on comparing with the straight line equation). Thus, we see that the slope is independent of the nature of the photoelectric material.
  3. Intercept of graph A on the potential axis = {tex} - \frac{{{\phi _0}'}}{e}{/tex}
    In this way, the work function can be determined.

question_html: Crystal diffraction experiments can be performed either by using electrons accelerated through appropriate voltage or by using X-rays. If the wavelength of these probes (electrons or X-rays) is 1{tex} \mathop A\limits^o{/tex}, estimate which of the two has greater energy.
question_text: Crystal diffraction experiments can be performed either by using electrons accelerated through appropriate voltage or by using X-rays. If the wavelength of these probes (electrons or X-rays) is 1 {tex} \mathop A\limits^o{/tex} , estimate which of the two has greater energy.
explanation: de-Broglie matter wave equation states that {tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 e m V } = h / \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = h ^ { 2 } / 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 }{/tex} .........(i) For X-ray of same wavelength = 1 {tex}\mathop A\limits ^o{/tex} {tex}E ^ { \prime } = h \nu = \frac { h c } { \lambda }{/tex} ........(ii) {tex}\frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { \lambda } { h c } = \frac { h } { 2 m c \lambda }{/tex} where, h = 6.6 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -34 J-s, c = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 8 m/s, m = 9.1 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -31 kg, {tex}\lambda = 1 \dot { A } = 1 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 2 \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \times 1 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } = 0.012 \Rightarrow \frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } < 1{/tex} Thus, K < E' i.e. Energy possess by X-ray is more than electron.
solution: de-Broglie matter wave equation states that {tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 e m V } = h / \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = h ^ { 2 } / 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 }{/tex} .........(i) For X-ray of same wavelength = 1 {tex}\mathop A\limits ^o{/tex} {tex}E ^ { \prime } = h \nu = \frac { h c } { \lambda }{/tex} ........(ii) {tex}\frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { \lambda } { h c } = \frac { h } { 2 m c \lambda }{/tex} where, h = 6.6 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -34 J-s, c = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 8 m/s, m = 9.1 {tex} \times {/tex} 10 -31 kg, {tex}\lambda = 1 \dot { A } = 1 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \mathrm { m }{/tex} {tex}\frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 2 \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \times 1 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } }{/tex} {tex}\frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } = 0.012 \Rightarrow \frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } < 1{/tex} Thus, K < E' i.e. Energy possess by X-ray is more than electron.
solution_html:
de-Broglie matter wave equation states that
{tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 e m V } = h / \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad K = h ^ { 2 } / 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 }{/tex}.........(i)
For X-ray of same wavelength = 1{tex}\mathop A\limits ^o{/tex}
{tex}E ^ { \prime } = h \nu = \frac { h c } { \lambda }{/tex}........(ii)
{tex}\frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } = \frac { h ^ { 2 } } { 2 m \lambda ^ { 2 } } \times \frac { \lambda } { h c } = \frac { h } { 2 m c \lambda }{/tex}
where, h = 6.6 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-34 J-s, c = 3 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 m/s, m = 9.1 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-31kg, {tex}\lambda = 1 \dot { A } = 1 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } \mathrm { m }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } = \frac { 6.6 \times 10 ^ { - 34 } } { 2 \times 9.1 \times 10 ^ { - 31 } \times 3 \times 10 ^ { 8 } \times 1 \times 10 ^ { - 10 } }{/tex}
{tex}\frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } = 0.012 \Rightarrow \frac { K } { E ^ { \prime } } < 1{/tex}
Thus, K < E'
i.e. Energy possess by X-ray is more than electron.

question_text: Calculate the ratio of the accelerating potential required to accelerate a proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle to have the same de-Broglie wavelength associated with them.
explanation: de-Broglie matter wave equation for accelerating charged particle is given by {tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 m q V }{/tex} where, h = Planck's constant m = mass of charged particle q = charge of charged particle V = potential difference Ratio of wavelengths of proton and {tex}\alpha {/tex} -particle {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } \right) \left( \frac { V _ { \alpha } } { V _ { p } } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = 1 , \frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } = 4 , \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } = 2 , \frac { V _ { \alpha } } { V _ { p } } = ?{/tex} 1 = {tex}\sqrt { 4 \times 2 \times \left( V _ { \alpha } / V _ { p } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = 8 \times V _ { \alpha } / V _ { p } \Rightarrow V _ { p } / V _ { \alpha } = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V _ { p } : V _ { \alpha } = 8 : 1{/tex}
solution: de-Broglie matter wave equation for accelerating charged particle is given by {tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 m q V }{/tex} where, h = Planck's constant m = mass of charged particle q = charge of charged particle V = potential difference Ratio of wavelengths of proton and {tex}\alpha {/tex} -particle {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } \right) \left( \frac { V _ { \alpha } } { V _ { p } } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = 1 , \frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } = 4 , \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } = 2 , \frac { V _ { \alpha } } { V _ { p } } = ?{/tex} 1 = {tex}\sqrt { 4 \times 2 \times \left( V _ { \alpha } / V _ { p } \right) }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = 8 \times V _ { \alpha } / V _ { p } \Rightarrow V _ { p } / V _ { \alpha } = 8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad V _ { p } : V _ { \alpha } = 8 : 1{/tex}
solution_html:
de-Broglie matter wave equation for accelerating charged particle is given by
{tex}\lambda = h / \sqrt { 2 m q V }{/tex}
where, h = Planck's constant
m = mass of charged particle
q = charge of charged particle
V = potential difference
Ratio of wavelengths of proton and {tex}\alpha {/tex}-particle
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } \right) \left( \frac { V _ { \alpha } } { V _ { p } } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}\because \quad \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = 1 , \frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } = 4 , \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } = 2 , \frac { V _ { \alpha } } { V _ { p } } = ?{/tex}
1 = {tex}\sqrt { 4 \times 2 \times \left( V _ { \alpha } / V _ { p } \right) }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1 = 8 \times V _ { \alpha } / V _ { p } \Rightarrow V _ { p } / V _ { \alpha } = 8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad V _ { p } : V _ { \alpha } = 8 : 1{/tex}

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html: A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle are accelerated through the same potential. Which of the two has (i) greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it and (ii) less kinetic energy? Justify your answer.
question_text: A proton and an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle are accelerated through the same potential. Which of the two has (i) greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it and (ii) less kinetic energy? Justify your answer.
explanation: From de-Broglie matter wave equation, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p }{/tex} But, {tex}p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} and K = qV {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m q } }{/tex} Using de-Broglie wavelength formula, we already know that proton and alpha particle are accelerated through the same potential. So, their velocities are same. Ratio of wavelengths of proton and {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = \sqrt { \frac { m _ { \alpha } q _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } q _ { p } } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } \right) }{/tex} But, {tex}\frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } = 4 , \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } = 2{/tex} {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { a } } = \sqrt { ( 4 ) \times 2 } = 2 \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda _ { p } : \lambda _ { \alpha } = 2 \sqrt { 2 } : 1{/tex} Proton have greater de-Broglie wavelength associated with it. Kinetic energy, K = qV {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { K _ { p } } { K _ { a } } = \left( \frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { \alpha } } \right){/tex} (for same accelerating voltage) {tex}\frac { K _ { p } } { K _ { \alpha } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow K _ { p } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } K _ { \alpha }{/tex} Thus, proton has less kinetic energy.
solution: From de-Broglie matter wave equation, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p }{/tex} But, {tex}p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} and K = qV {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m q } }{/tex} Using de-Broglie wavelength formula, we already know that proton and alpha particle are accelerated through the same potential. So, their velocities are same. Ratio of wavelengths of proton and {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = \sqrt { \frac { m _ { \alpha } q _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } q _ { p } } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } \right) }{/tex} But, {tex}\frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } = 4 , \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } = 2{/tex} {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { a } } = \sqrt { ( 4 ) \times 2 } = 2 \sqrt { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda _ { p } : \lambda _ { \alpha } = 2 \sqrt { 2 } : 1{/tex} Proton have greater de-Broglie wavelength associated with it. Kinetic energy, K = qV {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { K _ { p } } { K _ { a } } = \left( \frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { \alpha } } \right){/tex} (for same accelerating voltage) {tex}\frac { K _ { p } } { K _ { \alpha } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow K _ { p } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } K _ { \alpha }{/tex} Thus, proton has less kinetic energy.
solution_html:
From de-Broglie matter wave equation,
{tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p }{/tex}
But, {tex}p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} and K = qV
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m q } }{/tex}

  1. Using de-Broglie wavelength formula, we already know that proton and alpha particle are accelerated through the same potential. So, their velocities are same.
    Ratio of wavelengths of proton and {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle,
    {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { \alpha } } = \sqrt { \frac { m _ { \alpha } q _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } q _ { p } } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } \right) }{/tex}
    But, 
    {tex}\frac { m _ { \alpha } } { m _ { p } } = 4 , \frac { q _ { \alpha } } { q _ { p } } = 2{/tex}
    {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { a } } = \sqrt { ( 4 ) \times 2 } = 2 \sqrt { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda _ { p } : \lambda _ { \alpha } = 2 \sqrt { 2 } : 1{/tex}
    Proton have greater de-Broglie wavelength associated with it.

  2. Kinetic energy, K = qV
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { K _ { p } } { K _ { a } } = \left( \frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { \alpha } } \right){/tex}(for same accelerating voltage)
    {tex}\frac { K _ { p } } { K _ { \alpha } } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \Rightarrow K _ { p } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } K _ { \alpha }{/tex}
    Thus, proton has less kinetic energy.

Using the principle of energy conservation, write the equation relating the energy of incident photon, threshold frequency and the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons.
solution_html:
The graph showing the variation of stopping potential with the frequency is shown as below:

where, {tex}\phi{/tex} = work function of photosensitive material, e = electronic charge
From Einstein's photoelectric equation,
(KE)max = h{tex} u{/tex} - h{tex} u{/tex}0 = h({tex} u{/tex} - {tex} u{/tex}0)
where {tex} u{/tex} = frequency of incident radiation, {tex} u{/tex}0 = threshold frequency.
From the equation, if {tex} u{/tex} < {tex} u{/tex}0, then (KE)max is negative, which is not possible. For a given material, there exists a certain minimum frequency of the incident radiation below which no emission of photoelectrons takes place, this is called threshold frequency, i.e. photoelectric effect will take place only when {tex} u{/tex} > {tex} u{/tex}0 .

question_html: An electron and a proton are accelerated through the same potential. Which one of the two has (i) greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it and (ii) less momentum? Justify your answer.
question_text: An electron and a proton are accelerated through the same potential. Which one of the two has (i) greater value of de-Broglie wavelength associated with it and (ii) less momentum? Justify your answer.
explanation: From de-Broglie equation, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex} As, {tex}p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} and K = qV {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m q V } }{/tex} ........(i) {tex}\lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m q } }{/tex} Ratio of wavelengths of electron and proton, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } \right) }{/tex} Ratio of mass of proton and electron, {tex}\frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } = 1836{/tex} (constant) {tex}\frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } = 1{/tex} (Both electron and proton have same charge) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { 1836 \times 1 }{/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { e } \approx 42.8 \lambda _ { p }{/tex} nearly Electron have greater wavelength associated with it than that of proton. {tex}\because \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { p }{/tex} (de-Broglie equation) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \frac { h } { \lambda } \Rightarrow p \propto \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } }{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 } \Rightarrow \quad \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 }{/tex} Momentum of proton is nearly 42.8 times to that of momentum of electron. Thus, electron will have less momentum.
solution: From de-Broglie equation, {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex} As, {tex}p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} and K = qV {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m q V } }{/tex} ........(i) {tex}\lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m q } }{/tex} Ratio of wavelengths of electron and proton, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } \right) }{/tex} Ratio of mass of proton and electron, {tex}\frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } = 1836{/tex} (constant) {tex}\frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } = 1{/tex} (Both electron and proton have same charge) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { 1836 \times 1 }{/tex} {tex}\lambda _ { e } \approx 42.8 \lambda _ { p }{/tex} nearly Electron have greater wavelength associated with it than that of proton. {tex}\because \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { p }{/tex} (de-Broglie equation) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \frac { h } { \lambda } \Rightarrow p \propto \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } }{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 } \Rightarrow \quad \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 }{/tex} Momentum of proton is nearly 42.8 times to that of momentum of electron. Thus, electron will have less momentum.
solution_html:
1. From de-Broglie equation,
    {tex}\lambda = \frac { h } { p } = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m K } }{/tex}
    As, {tex}p = \sqrt { 2 m K }{/tex} and K = qV
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { \sqrt { 2 m q V } }{/tex}........(i)
    {tex}\lambda \propto \frac { 1 } { \sqrt { m q } }{/tex}
    Ratio of wavelengths of electron and proton,
    {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { \left( \frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } \right) \left( \frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } \right) }{/tex}
    Ratio of mass of proton and electron,
    {tex}\frac { m _ { p } } { m _ { e } } = 1836{/tex} (constant)
    {tex}\frac { q _ { p } } { q _ { e } } = 1{/tex} (Both electron and proton have same charge)
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { \lambda _ { e } } { \lambda _ { p } } = \sqrt { 1836 \times 1 }{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda _ { e } \approx 42.8 \lambda _ { p }{/tex} nearly
    Electron have greater wavelength associated with it than that of proton.
  2. {tex}\because \quad \lambda = \frac { h } { p }{/tex} (de-Broglie equation)
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad p = \frac { h } { \lambda } \Rightarrow p \propto \frac { 1 } { \lambda } \Rightarrow \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } }{/tex}
    Now,
    {tex}\frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 } \Rightarrow \quad \frac { p _ { e } } { p _ { p } } = \frac { \lambda _ { p } } { \lambda _ { e } } = \frac { 1 } { 42.8 }{/tex}
    Momentum of proton is nearly 42.8 times to that of momentum of electron. Thus, electron will have less momentum.

question_html: Calculate the ratio of the accelerating potential required to accelerate a deuteron and an alpha particle to have the same de-Broglie wavelength associated with them. Given, mass of deutron = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg and mass of alpha particle = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg
question_text: Calculate the ratio of the accelerating potential required to accelerate a deuteron and an alpha particle to have the same de-Broglie wavelength associated with them. Given, mass of deutron = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg and mass of alpha particle = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg
explanation: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with a deutron having charge e and accelerated through a potential difference V is given by {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}{/tex} .....(i) Suppose that an alpha particle possesses the same de-Broglie wavelength, when accelerated through a potential difference V'. If e' and m' are the values of charge and mass of the alpha particle, then {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m^{\prime} e^{\prime} V^{\prime}}}{/tex} .....(ii) From the equations (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m^{\prime} e^{\prime} V^{\prime}}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{V}{V^{\prime}}=\frac{m^{\prime} e^{\prime}}{m e}=\frac{m^{\prime}(2 e)}{m e}=\frac{2 m^{\prime}}{m}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} e' = 2e) Here, m = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg and m' = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{V}{V^{\prime}}=\frac{2 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-27}}{3 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-27}}{/tex} = 4
solution: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with a deutron having charge e and accelerated through a potential difference V is given by {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}{/tex} .....(i) Suppose that an alpha particle possesses the same de-Broglie wavelength, when accelerated through a potential difference V'. If e' and m' are the values of charge and mass of the alpha particle, then {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m^{\prime} e^{\prime} V^{\prime}}}{/tex} .....(ii) From the equations (i) and (ii), we have {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m^{\prime} e^{\prime} V^{\prime}}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{V}{V^{\prime}}=\frac{m^{\prime} e^{\prime}}{m e}=\frac{m^{\prime}(2 e)}{m e}=\frac{2 m^{\prime}}{m}{/tex} ( {tex}\because{/tex} e' = 2e) Here, m = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg and m' = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{V}{V^{\prime}}=\frac{2 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-27}}{3 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-27}}{/tex} = 4
solution_html:
The de-Broglie wavelength associated with a deutron having charge e and accelerated through a potential difference V is given by
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}{/tex} .....(i)
Suppose that an alpha particle possesses the same de-Broglie wavelength, when accelerated through a potential difference V'. If e' and m' are the values of charge and mass of the alpha particle, then
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m^{\prime} e^{\prime} V^{\prime}}}{/tex} .....(ii)
From the equations (i) and (ii), we have
{tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m e V}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m^{\prime} e^{\prime} V^{\prime}}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{V}{V^{\prime}}=\frac{m^{\prime} e^{\prime}}{m e}=\frac{m^{\prime}(2 e)}{m e}=\frac{2 m^{\prime}}{m}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} e' = 2e)
Here, m = 3.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg and m' = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{V}{V^{\prime}}=\frac{2 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-27}}{3 \cdot 2 \times 10^{-27}}{/tex} = 4
