question_html:
An electron and a photon each have de-Broglie wavelength of 1.00 nm.

  1. Write the ratio of their linear momenta,
  2. Compare the energy of the photon with the kinetic energy of the electron.
question_text: An electron and a photon each have de-Broglie wavelength of 1.00 nm. Write the ratio of their linear momenta, Compare the energy of the photon with the kinetic energy of the electron.
explanation: As both, electron and photon have the same wavelength, so they have the same momentum also. {tex}\therefore{/tex} p e : p p = 1 : 1 de-Broglie wavelength of electron, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The momentum of an electron, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} K.E. of an electron {tex}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex} The energy of X-ray photon is totally kinetic. Energy of X-ray photon {tex}=h \nu =\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text { Energy of photon }}{\text { K.E. of electron }}=\frac{2 m \lambda c}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^{-9} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9110}{11}{/tex} = 9110 : 11.
solution: As both, electron and photon have the same wavelength, so they have the same momentum also. {tex}\therefore{/tex} p e : p p = 1 : 1 de-Broglie wavelength of electron, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The momentum of an electron, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex} K.E. of an electron {tex}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex} The energy of X-ray photon is totally kinetic. Energy of X-ray photon {tex}=h \nu =\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\text { Energy of photon }}{\text { K.E. of electron }}=\frac{2 m \lambda c}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^{-9} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{9110}{11}{/tex} = 9110 : 11.
solution_html:
1. As both, electron and photon have the same wavelength, so they have the same momentum also.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} pe : pp = 1 : 1
  2. de-Broglie wavelength of electron, {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{p}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The momentum of an electron, {tex}p=\frac{h}{\lambda}{/tex}
    K.E. of an electron {tex}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex}
    The energy of X-ray photon is totally kinetic.
    Energy of X-ray photon {tex}=h \nu =\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{\text { Energy of photon }}{\text { K.E. of electron }}=\frac{2 m \lambda c}{h}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^{-9} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{9110}{11}{/tex} = 9110 : 11.

question_html:
1. Draw a graph showing variation of photoelectric current (I) with anode potential (V) for different intensities of incident radiation. Name the characteristic of the incident radiation that is kept constant in this experiment.

question_text: Draw a graph showing variation of photoelectric current (I) with anode potential (V) for different intensities of incident radiation. Name the characteristic of the incident radiation that is kept constant in this experiment. If the potential difference used to accelerate electrons is doubled, by what factor does the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electrons change?
explanation: The frequency of the incident radiation was kept constant from the de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\nu}}{/tex} If potential difference V is doubled, the de-Broglie wavelength is decreased to {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} times.
solution: The frequency of the incident radiation was kept constant from the de-Broglie wavelength, {tex}\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\nu}}{/tex} If potential difference V is doubled, the de-Broglie wavelength is decreased to {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} times.
solution_html:
1. The frequency of the incident radiation was kept constant
  2. from the de-Broglie wavelength,
    {tex}\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{\nu}}{/tex}
    If potential difference V is doubled, the de-Broglie wavelength is decreased to {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} times.

question_html:
Calculate the ratio of the accelerating potentials required to accelerate (i) a proton and (ii) an {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle to have the same de-Broglie wavelength associated with them.
[Given : Mass of proton = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg ; Mass of {tex}\alpha{/tex}-particle = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg]
question_text: Calculate the ratio of the accelerating potentials required to accelerate (i) a proton and (ii) an {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle to have the same de-Broglie wavelength associated with them. [Given : Mass of proton = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg ; Mass of {tex}\alpha{/tex} -particle = 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg]
explanation: If a particle is accelerated through a potential difference V, then {tex}q V=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} or {tex}p=\sqrt{2 m q V}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} As {tex}\lambda_{p}=\lambda_{\alpha}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} q_{p} V_{p}}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha} V_{\alpha}}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{V_{p}}{V_{\alpha}}=\frac{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}{m_{p} q_{p}}=\frac{4 m_{p} \cdot 2 e}{m_{p} \cdot e}{/tex} = 8 : 1.
solution: If a particle is accelerated through a potential difference V, then {tex}q V=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} or {tex}p=\sqrt{2 m q V}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex} As {tex}\lambda_{p}=\lambda_{\alpha}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} q_{p} V_{p}}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha} V_{\alpha}}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{V_{p}}{V_{\alpha}}=\frac{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}{m_{p} q_{p}}=\frac{4 m_{p} \cdot 2 e}{m_{p} \cdot e}{/tex} = 8 : 1.
solution_html:
If a particle is accelerated through a potential difference V, then
{tex}q V=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}{/tex} or {tex}p=\sqrt{2 m q V}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m q V}}{/tex}
As {tex}\lambda_{p}=\lambda_{\alpha}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{p} q_{p} V_{p}}}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha} V_{\alpha}}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{V_{p}}{V_{\alpha}}=\frac{m_{\alpha} q_{\alpha}}{m_{p} q_{p}}=\frac{4 m_{p} \cdot 2 e}{m_{p} \cdot e}{/tex} = 8 : 1.

question_html: Why are de-Broglie waves associated with a moving football not visible?
question_text: Why are de-Broglie waves associated with a moving football not visible?
explanation: Due to the large mass of a football, the de-Broglie wavelength ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h}{m\nu}{/tex} ) associated with a moving football is very small, quite beyond measurement. Hence its wave nature is not visible.
solution: Due to the large mass of a football, the de-Broglie wavelength ( {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac {h}{m\nu}{/tex} ) associated with a moving football is very small, quite beyond measurement. Hence its wave nature is not visible.

question_html: An electron and alpha particle have the same deBroglie wavelength associated with them. How are their kinetic energies related to each other?
question_text: An electron and alpha particle have the same deBroglie wavelength associated with them. How are their kinetic energies related to each other?
explanation: Kinetic energy, {tex}K.E. = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}m\frac{{{p^2}}}{{{m^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \left( {\because p = mv} \right)​​​​​​​{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow K.E. = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{2m}}{/tex} Now, {tex}{\lambda _e} = {\lambda _\alpha }{/tex} {tex}\frac{h}{{{p_e}}} = \frac{h}{{{p_\alpha }}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow p_e^2 = p_\alpha ^2 {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{K.{E_e}}}{{K.{E_\alpha }}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}\frac{{p_e^2}}{{2{m_e}}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}\frac{{p_\alpha ^2}}{{2m_\alpha }}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{K.{E_e}}}{{K.{E_\alpha }}} = \frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_e}}} \times \frac{{p_e^2}}{{{p_\alpha }}} = \frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_e}}}{/tex}
solution: Kinetic energy, {tex}K.E. = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}m\frac{{{p^2}}}{{{m^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \left( {\because p = mv} \right)​​​​​​​{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow K.E. = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{2m}}{/tex} Now, {tex}{\lambda _e} = {\lambda _\alpha }{/tex} {tex}\frac{h}{{{p_e}}} = \frac{h}{{{p_\alpha }}}{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow p_e^2 = p_\alpha ^2 {/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{K.{E_e}}}{{K.{E_\alpha }}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}\frac{{p_e^2}}{{2{m_e}}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}\frac{{p_\alpha ^2}}{{2m_\alpha }}}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{K.{E_e}}}{{K.{E_\alpha }}} = \frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_e}}} \times \frac{{p_e^2}}{{{p_\alpha }}} = \frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_e}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
Kinetic energy, {tex}K.E. = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{2}m\frac{{{p^2}}}{{{m^2}}}{/tex} {tex} \left( {\because p = mv} \right)​​​​​​​{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow K.E. = \frac{{{p^2}}}{{2m}}{/tex}
Now, {tex}{\lambda _e} = {\lambda _\alpha }{/tex}
{tex}\frac{h}{{{p_e}}} = \frac{h}{{{p_\alpha }}}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow p_e^2 = p_\alpha ^2 {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{{K.{E_e}}}{{K.{E_\alpha }}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}\frac{{p_e^2}}{{2{m_e}}}}}{{\frac{1}{2}\frac{{p_\alpha ^2}}{{2m_\alpha }}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{{K.{E_e}}}{{K.{E_\alpha }}} = \frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_e}}} \times \frac{{p_e^2}}{{{p_\alpha }}} = \frac{{{m_\alpha }}}{{{m_e}}}{/tex}

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html: An electron and alpha particle have the same kinetic energy. How are their de-Broglie wavelength associated with them related?
question_text: An electron and alpha particle have the same kinetic energy. How are their de-Broglie wavelength associated with them related?
explanation: If {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy K, then {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m \mathrm{K}}}{/tex} or {tex}K=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{K}_{e}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{e} \lambda_{e}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\alpha}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{\alpha} \lambda_{\alpha}^{2}}{/tex} Since K e = {tex}K_{\alpha}{/tex} , we have {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{e} \lambda_{e}^{2}}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{\alpha} \lambda_{\alpha}^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}m_{e} \lambda_{e}^{2}=m_{\alpha} \lambda_{\alpha}^{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{a}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{\alpha}}{m_{e}}}{/tex}
solution: If {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy K, then {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m \mathrm{K}}}{/tex} or {tex}K=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{K}_{e}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{e} \lambda_{e}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\alpha}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{\alpha} \lambda_{\alpha}^{2}}{/tex} Since K e = {tex}K_{\alpha}{/tex} , we have {tex}\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{e} \lambda_{e}^{2}}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{\alpha} \lambda_{\alpha}^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}m_{e} \lambda_{e}^{2}=m_{\alpha} \lambda_{\alpha}^{2}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{a}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{\alpha}}{m_{e}}}{/tex}
solution_html:
If {tex}\lambda{/tex} is the de-Broglie wavelength of a particle having kinetic energy K, then
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m \mathrm{K}}}{/tex}
or {tex}K=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m \lambda^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{K}_{e}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{e} \lambda_{e}^{2}}{/tex}
and {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\alpha}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{\alpha} \lambda_{\alpha}^{2}}{/tex}
Since Ke = {tex}K_{\alpha}{/tex}, we have
{tex}\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{e} \lambda_{e}^{2}}=\frac{h^{2}}{2 m_{\alpha} \lambda_{\alpha}^{2}}{/tex}
or {tex}m_{e} \lambda_{e}^{2}=m_{\alpha} \lambda_{\alpha}^{2}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{\lambda_{e}}{\lambda_{a}}=\sqrt{\frac{m_{\alpha}}{m_{e}}}{/tex}

question_html:
An electromagnetic wave of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident on a photosensitive surface of negligible work function. If the photo-electrons emitted from this surface have de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} prove that
{tex}\lambda=\frac{2 m c}{h} \lambda_{1}^{2}{/tex}
question_text: An electromagnetic wave of wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} is incident on a photosensitive surface of negligible work function. If the photo-electrons emitted from this surface have de-Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} prove that {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 m c}{h} \lambda_{1}^{2}{/tex}
explanation: Since the photosensitive surface is of negligible work function, K.E. of emitted electron = energy of the incident photon i.e. {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=h v{/tex} or {tex}\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}p=\sqrt{\frac{2 m h c}{\lambda}}{/tex} Therefore, de-Broglie wavelength of the emitted photo-electrons, {tex}\lambda_{1}=\frac{h}{p}=h \times \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2 m h c}}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 m c}{h} \lambda_{1}^{2}{/tex}
solution: Since the photosensitive surface is of negligible work function, K.E. of emitted electron = energy of the incident photon i.e. {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=h v{/tex} or {tex}\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex} or {tex}p=\sqrt{\frac{2 m h c}{\lambda}}{/tex} Therefore, de-Broglie wavelength of the emitted photo-electrons, {tex}\lambda_{1}=\frac{h}{p}=h \times \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2 m h c}}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 m c}{h} \lambda_{1}^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Since the photosensitive surface is of negligible work function,
K.E. of emitted electron = energy of the incident photon
i.e. {tex}\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=h v{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{p^{2}}{2 m}=\frac{h c}{\lambda}{/tex}
or {tex}p=\sqrt{\frac{2 m h c}{\lambda}}{/tex}
Therefore, de-Broglie wavelength of the emitted photo-electrons,
{tex}\lambda_{1}=\frac{h}{p}=h \times \sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2 m h c}}{/tex}
or {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 m c}{h} \lambda_{1}^{2}{/tex}

question_html: For what kinetic energy of a proton, will the associated de Broglie wavelength be 16.5 nm? Mass of proton = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27kg, h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34Js.
question_text: For what kinetic energy of a proton, will the associated de Broglie wavelength be 16.5 nm? Mass of proton = 1.675 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg, h = 6.63 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -34 Js.
explanation: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 16.5 nm = 16.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m m = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg Using de-Broglie equation, we have {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{m v} \Rightarrow {/tex} 16.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times v}{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{16.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{26.4 \times 10^{-36}}{/tex} = 0.251 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 = 25.1 m/s Hence, kinetic energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times(25.1)^2{/tex} = 504.008 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 = 5.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 J.
solution: {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 16.5 nm = 16.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 m m = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg Using de-Broglie equation, we have {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{m v} \Rightarrow {/tex} 16.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -9 = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times v}{/tex} v = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{16.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{26.4 \times 10^{-36}}{/tex} = 0.251 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 2 = 25.1 m/s Hence, kinetic energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times(25.1)^2{/tex} = 504.008 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 = 5.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -25 J.
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = 16.5 nm = 16.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 m
m = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg
Using de-Broglie equation, we have
{tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{m v} \Rightarrow {/tex} 16.5 {tex}\times{/tex}10-9 = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times v}{/tex}
v = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{16.5 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{26.4 \times 10^{-36}}{/tex}
= 0.251 {tex}\times{/tex} 102 = 25.1 m/s
Hence, kinetic energy = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-27} \times(25.1)^2{/tex}
= 504.008 {tex}\times{/tex}10-27 = 5.04 {tex}\times{/tex}10-25 J.

question_html: Write Einstein's photoelectric equation in terms of the stopping potential and the threshold frequency for a given photosensitive material. Draw a plot showing the variation of stopping potential vs. the frequency of incident radiation.
question_text: Write Einstein's photoelectric equation in terms of the stopping potential and the threshold frequency for a given photosensitive material. Draw a plot showing the variation of stopping potential vs. the frequency of incident radiation.
explanation: Einstein's photoelectric equation, K.E. of photo electron = incident energy of photons - Work function or K.E. = hv - W 0 or K.E. = hv - hv 0 where v 0 is called threshold frequency. Threshold Frequency: For a given metal, there exists a certain minimum frequency of the incident radiation below which no emission of photo electrons takes place. This frequency is called threshold frequency. Stopping Potential: It is that minimum negative potential given to anode in a photocell for which the photoelectric current becomes zero. It is denoted by V 0 . It is independent of the intensity of the incident light.
solution: Einstein's photoelectric equation, K.E. of photo electron = incident energy of photons - Work function or K.E. = hv - W 0 or K.E. = hv - hv 0 where v 0 is called threshold frequency. Threshold Frequency: For a given metal, there exists a certain minimum frequency of the incident radiation below which no emission of photo electrons takes place. This frequency is called threshold frequency. Stopping Potential: It is that minimum negative potential given to anode in a photocell for which the photoelectric current becomes zero. It is denoted by V 0 . It is independent of the intensity of the incident light.
solution_html:
Einstein's photoelectric equation,
K.E. of photo electron = incident energy of photons - Work function
or K.E. = hv - W0
or K.E. = hv - hv0
where v0 is called threshold frequency.

  1. Threshold Frequency: For a given metal, there exists a certain minimum frequency of the incident radiation below which no emission of photo electrons takes place. This frequency is called threshold frequency.
  2. Stopping Potential: It is that minimum negative potential given to anode in a photocell for which the photoelectric current becomes zero. It is denoted by V0. It is independent of the intensity of the incident light.

chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
Figure shows the variation of stopping potential V0 with the frequency {tex}\nu {/tex} of the incident radiation for two photosensitive metals P and Q.

  1. Explain which metal has a smaller threshold wavelengths.
  2. Explain, giving reason, which metals emits photo electrons having smaller kinetic energy, for the same wavelength of incident radiation.
  3. If the distance between the light source and metal P is doubled, how will the stopping potential change?
question_text: Figure shows the variation of stopping potential V 0 with the frequency {tex}\nu {/tex} of the incident radiation for two photosensitive metals P and Q. Explain which metal has a smaller threshold wavelengths. Explain, giving reason, which metals emits photo electrons having smaller kinetic energy, for the same wavelength of incident radiation. If the distance between the light source and metal P is doubled, how will the stopping potential change?
solution_html:
1. Suppose the frequency of incident radiations of metal Q and P be {tex}{\nu _0}{/tex} and {tex}{\nu _0}'{/tex} respectively.
    {tex}\therefore {\nu _0} > {\nu _0}'{/tex}
    {tex}\because {\nu _0} = \frac{c}{{{\lambda _0}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \frac{c}{{{\lambda _0}}} > \frac{c}{{{\lambda _0}'}}{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow {\lambda _0} < {\lambda _0}'{/tex}
    Therefore, metal 'Q' has smaller wavelength.
  2. As we know,
    {tex}E = h{\nu _0}{/tex}
    {tex} \Rightarrow E\propto {\nu _0}{/tex}
    Hence, metal 'P' has smaller kinetic energy.
  3. Stopping potential remains unaffected because the value of stopping potential for a given metal surface does not depend on the intensity of the incident radiation. It depends on the frequency of incident radiation.

question_html: An electron, an alpha-particle and a proton have the same kinetic energy. Which one of these particles has the largest de-Broglie wavelength?
question_text: An electron, an alpha-particle and a proton have the same kinetic energy. Which one of these particles has the largest de-Broglie wavelength?
explanation: de-broglie wavelength is given by, {tex}\lambda=\frac h{\sqrt{2mE_k}}{/tex} where m is mass of particle and E k is the kinetic energy. Thus, {tex}\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}{/tex} Now, mass of electron = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg, mass of alpha particle = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg, mass of proton = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg Since electron is the lightest particle, so it will have the maximum wavelength.
solution: de-broglie wavelength is given by, {tex}\lambda=\frac h{\sqrt{2mE_k}}{/tex} where m is mass of particle and E k is the kinetic energy. Thus, {tex}\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}{/tex} Now, mass of electron = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -31 kg, mass of alpha particle = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg, mass of proton = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -27 kg Since electron is the lightest particle, so it will have the maximum wavelength.
solution_html:
de-broglie wavelength is given by,
{tex}\lambda=\frac h{\sqrt{2mE_k}}{/tex} where m is mass of particle and Ek is the kinetic energy.
Thus, {tex}\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}{/tex}
Now, mass of electron = 9.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-31 kg, mass of alpha particle = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg, mass of proton = 1.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-27 kg
Since electron is the lightest particle, so it will have the maximum wavelength.

question_html:
Define the following terms for a given photosensitive surface:

  1. Threshold wavelength
  2. Work function.
question_text: Define the following terms for a given photosensitive surface: Threshold wavelength Work function.
explanation: The maximum wavelength of incident light for a metal surface, above which the incident light cannot eject electrons, is called threshold wavelength for that metal surface. The minimum amount of energy required to eject an electron from a surface (without imparting any kinetic energy) is called work function of the surface.
solution: The maximum wavelength of incident light for a metal surface, above which the incident light cannot eject electrons, is called threshold wavelength for that metal surface. The minimum amount of energy required to eject an electron from a surface (without imparting any kinetic energy) is called work function of the surface.
solution_html:
1. The maximum wavelength of incident light for a metal surface, above which the incident light cannot eject electrons, is called threshold wavelength for that metal surface.
  2. The minimum amount of energy required to eject an electron from a surface (without imparting any kinetic energy) is called work function of the surface.

question_text: When light of frequency 2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz, falls on a photosensitive surface, the retarding potential needed to completely stop the emitted photoelectrons, is found to be 6.8 V. What is the work function (in eV) of the given photosensitive surface?
explanation: W 0 = hv - eV 0 {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.4 \times 10^{15}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}-6.8 \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = 9.9 - 6.8 = 3.1 eV
solution: W 0 = hv - eV 0 {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.4 \times 10^{15}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}-6.8 \mathrm{eV}{/tex} = 9.9 - 6.8 = 3.1 eV

question_text: The work function, for a given photosensitive surface equals 2.5 eV. When light of frequency, {tex}\nu{/tex} , falls on this surface, the emitted photoelectrons are completely stopped by applying a retarding potential of 4.1 V. What is the value of {tex}\nu{/tex} ?
explanation: h {tex}\nu{/tex} = eV 0 + W 0 = 4.1eV + 2.5eV = 6.6eV {tex}\nu=\frac{6.6 \mathrm{eV}}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{/tex} = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz
solution: h {tex}\nu{/tex} = eV 0 + W 0 = 4.1eV + 2.5eV = 6.6eV {tex}\nu=\frac{6.6 \mathrm{eV}}{h}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}}{/tex} = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 Hz

K.E.= {tex}\frac12{/tex} mv 2 max= h (v−v 0 ) ...(i) Laws of Photoelectric emission on basis of Einstein's Photoelectric equation: From (i), we note that if v < v 0 , max K.E. is -ve which is impossible. question_html: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with an electron accelerated through a potential difference V is {tex}\lambda{/tex}. What will be its wavelength when the accelerating potential is increased to 4 V?
question_text: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with an electron accelerated through a potential difference V is {tex}\lambda{/tex} . What will be its wavelength when the accelerating potential is increased to 4 V?
explanation: de-Broglie wavelength is given as, {tex}\lambda=\frac h{\sqrt{2meV}}{/tex} Given: {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = X, V 1 = V, V 2 = 4V So, {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}=\sqrt{\frac{V_1}{V_2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{V_1}{V_2}}{/tex} = X {tex}\sqrt{\frac{V}{4V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac X2{/tex} Thus, the de-broglie wavelength becomes half when the accelerating potential is increased to 4V.
solution: de-Broglie wavelength is given as, {tex}\lambda=\frac h{\sqrt{2meV}}{/tex} Given: {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = X, V 1 = V, V 2 = 4V So, {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}=\sqrt{\frac{V_1}{V_2}}{/tex} {tex}\lambda_2{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{V_1}{V_2}}{/tex} = X {tex}\sqrt{\frac{V}{4V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac X2{/tex} Thus, the de-broglie wavelength becomes half when the accelerating potential is increased to 4V.
solution_html:
de-Broglie wavelength is given as,
{tex}\lambda=\frac h{\sqrt{2meV}}{/tex}
Given: {tex}\lambda_1{/tex} = X, V1 = V, V2 = 4V
So, {tex}\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}=\sqrt{\frac{V_1}{V_2}}{/tex}
{tex}\lambda_2{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_1{/tex}{tex}\sqrt{\frac{V_1}{V_2}}{/tex} = X{tex}\sqrt{\frac{V}{4V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac X2{/tex}
Thus, the de-broglie wavelength becomes half when the accelerating potential is increased to 4V.

question_html:
A source of light of frequency greater than threshold frequency is placed at a distance of 1 m from the cathode of a photocell. The stopping potential is found to be V. If the distance of the light source from the cathode is reduced, explain giving reasons, what change will you observe in the

  1. photoelectric current and
  2. stopping potential?
question_text: A source of light of frequency greater than threshold frequency is placed at a distance of 1 m from the cathode of a photocell. The stopping potential is found to be V. If the distance of the light source from the cathode is reduced, explain giving reasons, what change will you observe in the photoelectric current and stopping potential?
explanation: On decreasing the distance of the source of light from the photocell, the intensity of light will increase. As the photocurrent is directly proportional to the intensity of light, the photocurrent will increase. There will be no effect on the stopping potential.
solution: On decreasing the distance of the source of light from the photocell, the intensity of light will increase. As the photocurrent is directly proportional to the intensity of light, the photocurrent will increase. There will be no effect on the stopping potential.
solution_html:
1. On decreasing the distance of the source of light from the photocell, the intensity of light will increase. As the photocurrent is directly proportional to the intensity of light, the photocurrent will increase.
  2. There will be no effect on the stopping potential.

explanation: From the value of the intercept on the potential axis, we can determine the work function W 0 as follows: As eV 0 = {tex}h\nu{/tex} - W 0 or {tex}V_{0}=\left(\frac{h}{e}\right) v-\frac{W_{0}}{e}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Intercept on the potential axis {tex}=-\frac{W_{0}}{e}{/tex} Work function, W 0 = e {tex}\times{/tex} Magnitude of intercept on the potential axis. If the distance of the light source from the cathode is reduced, then the photoelectric current increases because the intensity of incident radiation increases, and the stopping potential V 0 remains the same because it is independent of the intensity.

If the distance of the light source from the cathode is reduced, then the photoelectric current increases because the intensity of incident radiation increases, and the stopping potential V 0 remains the same because it is independent of the intensity.

As eV0 = {tex}h\nu{/tex} - W0
or {tex}V_{0}=\left(\frac{h}{e}\right) v-\frac{W_{0}}{e}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Intercept on the potential axis {tex}=-\frac{W_{0}}{e}{/tex}
Work function,
W0 = e {tex}\times{/tex} Magnitude of intercept on the potential axis.
If the distance of the light source from the cathode is reduced, then

  1. the photoelectric current increases because the intensity of incident radiation increases, and
  2. the stopping potential V0 remains the same because it is independent of the intensity.

question_html: Two metals A and B have work functions 2 eV and 4 eV respectively. Which metal has a lower threshold wavelength for the photoelectric effect?
question_text: Two metals A and B have work functions 2 eV and 4 eV respectively. Which metal has a lower threshold wavelength for the photoelectric effect?
explanation: We know, {tex}W=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{0} \propto \frac{1}{W}{/tex} It follows that for the metal B, which has greater value of work function i.e. 4 eV, the threshold wavelength will be lower.
solution: We know, {tex}W=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} or {tex}\lambda_{0} \propto \frac{1}{W}{/tex} It follows that for the metal B, which has greater value of work function i.e. 4 eV, the threshold wavelength will be lower.
solution_html:
We know, {tex}W=\frac{h c}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex}
or {tex}\lambda_{0} \propto \frac{1}{W}{/tex}
It follows that for the metal B, which has greater value of work function i.e. 4 eV, the threshold wavelength will be lower.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html:
Electrons are emitted from a photosensitive surface when it is illuminated by green light but electron emission does not take place by yellow light. Will the electrons are emitted when the surface is illuminated by

  1. red light and
  2. blue light?
question_text: Electrons are emitted from a photosensitive surface when it is illuminated by green light but electron emission does not take place by yellow light. Will the electrons are emitted when the surface is illuminated by red light and blue light?
explanation: As the green light ejects electrons from the surface, the wavelength of green light is less than the threshold wavelength for the surface. Further, as the yellow light cannot eject photo-electrons, the wavelength of yellow light is greater than the threshold wavelength. The wavelength of red light is greater than that of yellow light, while the wavelength of blue light is less than that of green light. Hence, red light cannot cause photoelectric emission, while photoelectric emission will take place for blue light.
solution: As the green light ejects electrons from the surface, the wavelength of green light is less than the threshold wavelength for the surface. Further, as the yellow light cannot eject photo-electrons, the wavelength of yellow light is greater than the threshold wavelength. The wavelength of red light is greater than that of yellow light, while the wavelength of blue light is less than that of green light. Hence, red light cannot cause photoelectric emission, while photoelectric emission will take place for blue light.
solution_html: As the green light ejects electrons from the surface, the wavelength of green light is less than the threshold wavelength for the surface. Further, as the yellow light cannot eject photo-electrons, the wavelength of yellow light is greater than the threshold wavelength. The wavelength of red light is greater than that of yellow light, while the wavelength of blue light is less than that of green light. Hence, red light cannot cause photoelectric emission, while photoelectric emission will take place for blue light.

explanation: The minimum energy required to free an electron from the metallic surface is called the work function of that surface. It is represented by {tex}\phi{/tex} . {tex}\phi=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} where {tex}\lambda_0{/tex} = threshold frequency.
solution: The minimum energy required to free an electron from the metallic surface is called the work function of that surface. It is represented by {tex}\phi{/tex} . {tex}\phi=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex} where {tex}\lambda_0{/tex} = threshold frequency.

The minimum energy required to free an electron from the metallic surface is called the work function of that surface. It is represented by {tex}\phi{/tex}.
{tex}\phi=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda_{0}}{/tex}
where {tex}\lambda_0{/tex} = threshold frequency.

question_html: Define photoelectric work function. How is it related to threshold frequency?
question_text: Define photoelectric work function. How is it related to threshold frequency?
explanation: The minimum amount of radiant energy needed to pull an electron (without imparting it any kinetic energy) from a metallic surface is called work function of the metal. The relation between work function W 0 and threshold frequency {tex}\nu_0{/tex} is W 0 = h {tex}\nu_0{/tex}
solution: The minimum amount of radiant energy needed to pull an electron (without imparting it any kinetic energy) from a metallic surface is called work function of the metal. The relation between work function W 0 and threshold frequency {tex}\nu_0{/tex} is W 0 = h {tex}\nu_0{/tex}
solution_html: The minimum amount of radiant energy needed to pull an electron (without imparting it any kinetic energy) from a metallic surface is called work function of the metal. The relation between work function W0 and threshold frequency {tex}\nu_0{/tex} is W0 = h{tex}\nu_0{/tex}

question_html:
Obtain Einstein's photo-electric equation. Explain how it enables us to understand the

  1. independence of maximum energy of the emitted photo-electrons from the intensity of incident light
  2. linear dependence of the maximum energy of the emitted electrons on the frequency of the incident radiation, and
  3. the existence of a threshold frequency for a given photoemitter.
question_text: Obtain Einstein's photo-electric equation. Explain how it enables us to understand the independence of maximum energy of the emitted photo-electrons from the intensity of incident light linear dependence of the maximum energy of the emitted electrons on the frequency of the incident radiation, and the existence of a threshold frequency for a given photoemitter.
explanation: Incident energy = Work functions + Kinetic energy ...(i) E = {tex}\phi{/tex} + K {tex}\therefore{/tex} K = E - {tex}\phi{/tex} But, E = hv ...(ii) {tex}\phi{/tex} = hv 0 Using in Eqn. (ii) ...(iii) K = h(v - v 0 ) ...(vi) But, K = eVs = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 ...(v) {tex}\therefore{/tex} eVs = h(v - v 0 ) ...(vi) and, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = h(v - v 0 ) ...(vii) But v = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} ...(viii) and v 0 = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} It's because intensity is directly proportional to number of photon in light ray and in photoelectric effect one photon can only release one electron and so more intense light means more photon hence more elections will ejected from the metal surface and hence increasing the photo current. it means, above threshold frequencyv 0 , maximum K.E. is directly proportional to v, i.e.,(K.E.) max depends linearly on the frequency of the incident radiation. When v < v 0 , (K.E.) max becomes negative. The negative K.E. has no physical meaning hence there is no photoelectric emission below the threshold frequency v 0 .
solution: Incident energy = Work functions + Kinetic energy ...(i) E = {tex}\phi{/tex} + K {tex}\therefore{/tex} K = E - {tex}\phi{/tex} But, E = hv ...(ii) {tex}\phi{/tex} = hv 0 Using in Eqn. (ii) ...(iii) K = h(v - v 0 ) ...(vi) But, K = eVs = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 ...(v) {tex}\therefore{/tex} eVs = h(v - v 0 ) ...(vi) and, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} mv 2 = h(v - v 0 ) ...(vii) But v = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} ...(viii) and v 0 = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} It's because intensity is directly proportional to number of photon in light ray and in photoelectric effect one photon can only release one electron and so more intense light means more photon hence more elections will ejected from the metal surface and hence increasing the photo current. it means, above threshold frequencyv 0 , maximum K.E. is directly proportional to v, i.e.,(K.E.) max depends linearly on the frequency of the incident radiation. When v < v 0 , (K.E.) max becomes negative. The negative K.E. has no physical meaning hence there is no photoelectric emission below the threshold frequency v 0 .
solution_html:
Incident energy = Work functions + Kinetic energy ...(i)
E = {tex}\phi{/tex} + K
{tex}\therefore{/tex} K = E - {tex}\phi{/tex}
But, E = hv ...(ii)
{tex}\phi{/tex} = hv0
Using in Eqn. (ii) ...(iii)
K = h(v - v0) ...(vi)
But, K = eVs = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2 ...(v)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} eVs = h(v - v0) ...(vi)
and, {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}mv2 = h(v - v0) ...(vii)
But v = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex} ...(viii)
and v0 = {tex}\frac{c}{\lambda}{/tex}

  1. It's because intensity is directly proportional to number of photon in light ray and in photoelectric effect one photon can only release one electron and so more intense light means more photon hence more elections will ejected from the metal surface and hence increasing the photo current.
  2. it means, above threshold frequencyv0, maximum K.E. is directly proportional to v, i.e.,(K.E.) max depends linearly on the frequency of the incident radiation.
  3. When v < v0, (K.E.) max becomes negative. The negative K.E. has no physical meaning hence there is no photoelectric emission below the threshold frequency v0.

question_html:
Radiation of frequency 1015 Hz is incident on three photo-sensitive surfaces A, B and C.
Following observations are recorded:
Surface A: No photo-emission occurs.
Surface B: Photo-emission occurs but the photoelectrons have zero kinetic energy.
Surface C: Photo-emission occurs and photoelectrons have some K.E.
Based on Einstein's photo-electric equation, explain the three observations.
question_text: Radiation of frequency 10 15 Hz is incident on three photo-sensitive surfaces A, B and C. Following observations are recorded: Surface A: No photo-emission occurs. Surface B: Photo-emission occurs but the photoelectrons have zero kinetic energy. Surface C: Photo-emission occurs and photoelectrons have some K.E. Based on Einstein's photo-electric equation, explain the three observations.
explanation: From the observations made (parts A and B) on the basis of Einstein's photoelectric equation, we draw the following conclusions: For surface A, the threshold frequency is more than 10 15 Hz, hence no photo-emission is possible. For surface B, the threshold frequency is equal to the frequency of given radiation. Thus, photoemission takes place but kinetic energy of photo-electrons is zero. For surface C, the threshold frequency is less than 10 15 Hz. So photo-emission occurs and photoelectrons have some kinetic energy.
solution: From the observations made (parts A and B) on the basis of Einstein's photoelectric equation, we draw the following conclusions: For surface A, the threshold frequency is more than 10 15 Hz, hence no photo-emission is possible. For surface B, the threshold frequency is equal to the frequency of given radiation. Thus, photoemission takes place but kinetic energy of photo-electrons is zero. For surface C, the threshold frequency is less than 10 15 Hz. So photo-emission occurs and photoelectrons have some kinetic energy.
solution_html:
From the observations made (parts A and B) on the basis of Einstein's photoelectric equation, we draw the following conclusions:

  1. For surface A, the threshold frequency is more than 1015 Hz, hence no photo-emission is possible.
  2. For surface B, the threshold frequency is equal to the frequency of given radiation. Thus, photoemission takes place but kinetic energy of photo-electrons is zero.
  3. For surface C, the threshold frequency is less than 1015 Hz. So photo-emission occurs and photoelectrons have some kinetic energy.

As the energy of a photon of orange light is less than the work function of sodium (2.3 eV), so sodium does not show photoelectric emission with orange light.

subject: Physics
chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
question_html: What is photo-electric effect? Why it cannot be explained on the basis of wave nature of light?
question_text: What is photo-electric effect? Why it cannot be explained on the basis of wave nature of light?
explanation: Photoelectric effect: It is the emission of electrons from the surface of a material when electromagnetic radiation, such as light is incident on a material. Explanation: Wave nature of light does not explain about following concepts of the photoelectric effect because: Immediate ejection of electrons from the metal surface. Existence of threshold frequency for the metal surface. Kinetic energy of photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of the incident light and depends on the frequency of the incident light.
solution: Photoelectric effect: It is the emission of electrons from the surface of a material when electromagnetic radiation, such as light is incident on a material. Explanation: Wave nature of light does not explain about following concepts of the photoelectric effect because: Immediate ejection of electrons from the metal surface. Existence of threshold frequency for the metal surface. Kinetic energy of photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of the incident light and depends on the frequency of the incident light.
solution_html:
Photoelectric effect:

  1. It is the emission of electrons from the surface of a material when electromagnetic radiation, such as light is incident on a material.

Explanation: Wave nature of light does not explain about following concepts of the photoelectric effect because:

  1. Immediate ejection of electrons from the metal surface.
  2. Existence of threshold frequency for the metal surface.
  3. Kinetic energy of photoelectrons is independent of the intensity of the incident light and depends on the frequency of the incident light.

subject: Physics

question_html: If the wavelength of electromagnetic radiation is doubled, what will happen to the energy of photons?
question_text: If the wavelength of electromagnetic radiation is doubled, what will happen to the energy of photons?
explanation: The energy of a photon, {tex}E=h \nu=\frac{h c}{\lambda} \Rightarrow E \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} Clearly, the energy of photons reduces to one-half when the wavelength of radiation is doubled.
solution: The energy of a photon, {tex}E=h \nu=\frac{h c}{\lambda} \Rightarrow E \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex} Clearly, the energy of photons reduces to one-half when the wavelength of radiation is doubled.
solution_html:
The energy of a photon,
{tex}E=h \nu=\frac{h c}{\lambda} \Rightarrow E \propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex}
Clearly, the energy of photons reduces to one-half when the wavelength of radiation is doubled.

question_html:
Show that the de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} of electrons of energy K is given by the relation:
{tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex}.
question_text: Show that the de Broglie wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} of electrons of energy K is given by the relation: {tex}\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m K}}{/tex} .
explanation: If one electron which is in rest in accelerated with potential difference V and due to this acceleration it acquire the velocity v then. {tex}\frac12{/tex} m e v 2 = eV where me = mass of electron e = charge of electron of, m e v 2 2eV If {tex}\rho{/tex} is the momentum of the electron then {tex}\rho^2 m_m^2 v^2{/tex} = m e {tex}\cdot{/tex} m e v 2 = 2 e eV {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2 m_e e V}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\rho}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{2 m E}{/tex}
solution: If one electron which is in rest in accelerated with potential difference V and due to this acceleration it acquire the velocity v then. {tex}\frac12{/tex} m e v 2 = eV where me = mass of electron e = charge of electron of, m e v 2 2eV If {tex}\rho{/tex} is the momentum of the electron then {tex}\rho^2 m_m^2 v^2{/tex} = m e {tex}\cdot{/tex} m e v 2 = 2 e eV {tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2 m_e e V}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\rho}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{2 m E}{/tex}
solution_html:
If one electron which is in rest in accelerated with potential difference V and due to this acceleration it acquire the velocity v then.
{tex}\frac12{/tex} mev2 = eV
where me = mass of electron e = charge of electron of, mev2 2eV
If {tex}\rho{/tex} is the momentum of the electron
then {tex}\rho^2 m_m^2 v^2{/tex} = me {tex}\cdot{/tex} mev2 = 2eeV
{tex}\rho{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{2 m_e e V}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\lambda{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\rho}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{2 m E}{/tex}

question_html: Calculate the kinetic energy of a photoelectron (in eV) emitted on shining light of wavelength 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 m on a metal surface. The work function of the metal is 0.1 eV.
question_text: Calculate the kinetic energy of a photoelectron (in eV) emitted on shining light of wavelength 6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 m on a metal surface. The work function of the metal is 0.1 eV.
solution_html:
{tex}K_{\max }=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-W_{0}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}-0.1{/tex}
= 0.2 - 0.1 = 0.1 eV.
